hero.sub: Discovery & verification controller for the Vortex peer-to-peer network. Open. Encrypted. Unstoppable.

network.label: NETWORK
network.body: Every peer holds a signed identity. Clients verify before connecting, nodes prove ownership at registration. No central authority can fake what cryptography has already confirmed.
network.dual_body: Registered with the controller AND sealed on-chain via Solana. Two independent witnesses confirm code hash and pubkey. Safe for sensitive sessions.
network.ctrl_body: Signature proves the node owns its private key, but code integrity is not anchored on-chain. Fine for most sessions; risk = trust the controller operator.
network.unverif_body: Known from config or gossip. No cryptographic proof yet. Use only to bootstrap the peer list — never as a handoff target for live sessions.

security.label: SECURITY
security.body: Four independent Ed25519 keypairs, each with a single narrow job. Compromise of any one key does not break the others.
security.controller_verified: Clients — from SNS or build pin
security.step1_d: Read vortexx.sol on-chain. Records signed by Solana wallet — impossible to DNS-hijack.
security.step2_d: Client calls /v1/integrity. Rejects any response except status: verified from the pinned release key.
security.step3_d: Every API call returns {payload, sig, signed_by}. Client verifies the Ed25519 signature before trusting.
security.step4_d: Sealed nodes: client reads code_hash from Solana registry. Divergence from controller = permanently blacklisted.
security.check_auth: Response authenticity — impossible to forge /v1/nodes without the controller's private key.
security.check_integrity: Code integrity — controller files hashed and cross-checked at every startup. Tampering returns 503 before any traffic is served.
security.check_identity: Node identity — nodes must sign registration with their own key. An attacker cannot register on behalf of another node's pubkey.
security.check_dns: DNS hijack protection — .sol records live on Solana. An adversary needs to compromise the wallet, not just a DNS registrar.
security.check_handoff: Handoff security — session transfers carry Ed25519-signed tokens with anti-replay, valid only 5 minutes. Target rejects unknown sources.
security.check_transport: Transport privacy — connection URL visible to your ISP / Cloudflare. Use the Tor mirror if metadata matters.
security.check_content: Message content — NOT touched by the controller. End-to-end encrypted between users. Controller can read nothing even if fully compromised.
security.check_host: Host OS / kernel — if the VPS is rooted, keys can be stolen. Use hardware-backed signing for paranoid deployments.

access.label: ACCESS
access.body: Multiple entry points mean no single entity can block your access. Mirror sites serve the same signed content from independent infrastructure.
access.tunnel_body: Free *.trycloudflare.com address. No account required. URL changes when the tunnel restarts — treat as ephemeral.
access.tor_body: .onion address, reachable only through Tor. Censor-proof and metadata-private, but slower. Needs a Tor client.
access.ipfs_body: Content-addressed copy of the static frontend. Any IPFS gateway can serve it — no single server to block.
access.direct_body: Regular domain + TLS. Fastest option. Blockable at DNS/IP level — fall back to others if censored.
access.mirrors_body: Alternative hosts serving the exact same signed content as this controller. Each mirror is periodically probed — a green dot means reachable within the last 5 minutes. Tor .onion mirrors can't be health-checked without a local Tor proxy.

start.label: GET STARTED
start.step1: Install Vortex on your device — mobile or desktop.
start.step2: Pick Global network mode in the setup wizard.
start.step3: Paste the controller URL (auto-resolved via SNS) or any entry URL from the Entries page.
start.step4: Verify the pubkey in your app matches the controller fingerprint below. A mismatch means tampering.
start.step5: You're connected. Node list, signatures, and routing are handled automatically.
start.fingerprint_note: Pin this key in your client before trusting any response.

treasury.label: TREASURY & REWARDS
treasury.transparency_body: All protocol fees — node register fees and a 20% cut of premium subscriptions — flow to the treasury wallet on Solana. Every transaction is publicly verifiable by anyone.
treasury.rewards_body: 70% of each premium sub goes to the rewards pool, distributed pro-rata to active node operators. 20% funds core development. 10% burns or sits as insurance.

gravitixDocs.title: Gravitix Gbegbɔgblɔ Ŋuti Nyatakaka
gravitixDocs.subtitle: Scripting gbegbɔgblɔ si wɔa dɔ nyuie hena bots tutu. 96 ƒe nɔnɔmewo. Rust megbenyawo.
gravitixDocs.introDesc: Gravitix nye gbegbɔgblɔ si ku ɖe domenyinyi tɔxɛ aɖe ŋu (DSL) si wowɔ tso gɔmedzedzea me ke hena Vortex bots ŋɔŋlɔ. Le afisi gbe bubuwo hiã agbalẽdzraɖoƒewo, yɔyɔwo, kple boilerplate le la, Gravitix naa nudzɔdzɔwo dzikpɔla siwo le ɖoƒe gbãtɔ, dzeɖoɖo ƒe sisi, kple nɔnɔme si wotu ɖe eme — wo katã le nyagɔmeɖegbalẽ dzadzɛ si woate ŋu axlẽ si Rust ʋã me.
gravitixDocs.goalReadable: Readable — code xlẽa abe tameɖoɖo ene, ke menye mɔ̃wo o
gravitixDocs.goalSafe: Dedienɔnɔ — static ƒomeviwo léa vodadawo hafi runtime
gravitixDocs.goalExpressive: Nyagbɔgblɔ — sisiwo, dzɔlawo, kple kpɔɖeŋu ƒe sɔsɔ nye esiwo wotu ɖe eme
gravitixDocs.goalMinimal: Minimal — klass aɖeke meli o, domenyinu aɖeke meli o, ɖoɖo aɖeke meli si woasrɔ̃ o
gravitixDocs.whoDesc: Gravitix nye na amesiame si di be yeatu Vortex bot kaba — eɖanye be mèŋlɔ kɔpi kpɔ o, alo ènye dɔwɔla bibi si ti kɔ na Python boilerplate. Numekugbalẽ sia fia Gravitix tso gɔmeɖose gbãtɔwo me: nuteƒekpɔkpɔ aɖeke mehiã do ŋgɔ o.
gravitixDocs.howToReadDesc: Akpa ɖesiaɖe tua esiwo do ŋgɔ ɖo. Ne ènye ame yeye le ɖoɖowɔɖiwo me la, xlẽe tso dzi va anyi. Ne ènya gbe bubu xoxo la, zã emenyawo tsɔ ti kpo yi nusi do dzidzɔ na wò gbɔ tẽ.
gravitixDocs.quickStartDesc: Mɔ si le kabakaba wu tso zero dzi yi bot si le du dzi.
gravitixDocs.whatHappened: Nukae dzɔ teti koe nye ema?
gravitixDocs.whatHappenedDesc: on /start { ... } — ɖea handler si zɔna ne zãla ɖo /start ɖa. emit "..." — ɖoa gbedasi ɖe zãla la ake. {ctx.first_name} — tsɔa zãla ƒe ŋkɔ gbãtɔ dea ka la me.
gravitixDocs.runningDesc: Le Gravitix IDE me la, zi Run ƒe dzesi si le etame ƒe akpaa dzi. Ŋlɔ wò Vortex Bot Token ne wobia tso asiwò. Bot la dzea egɔme enumake — xɔtutu ƒe afɔɖeɖe aɖeke mehiã o.
gravitixDocs.syntaxAtGlance: Nyagɔmeɖegbalẽ ƒe Ðoɖowɔɖi le Kpekpeɖeŋu Me
gravitixDocs.syntaxDesc: Wotu Gravitix ɖoɖowɔɖi ɖesiaɖe tso xɔtunu vevi etɔ̃ me: handlers, emit statements, kple logic.
gravitixDocs.anatomyOfHandler: Amesi tsɔa nu ɖe asi ƒe ŋutilã ƒe wɔwɔme
gravitixDocs.whatGoesAfterEmit: Nusi yia edzi le emit megbe
gravitixDocs.declaringDesc: Zã nya vevi si nye let.
gravitixDocs.explicitTypes: Ƒomevi ƒe nuŋlɔɖi siwo me kɔ
gravitixDocs.typesDesc: Asixɔxɔ ɖesiaɖe si le Gravitix me ƒe ƒomevi aɖe le esi. Type system la le teƒe ɖeka: wokpɔa types le compile time, hafi wò bot nawɔ dɔ.
gravitixDocs.optionalTypes: Ƒomevi siwo woate ŋu atia T?
gravitixDocs.operatorsDesc: Dɔwɔlawo ƒoa asixɔxɔwo nu ƒu tsɔ wɔa asixɔxɔ yeyewo.
gravitixDocs.comparisonOps: Dɔwɔla siwo tsɔa nu sɔna kple wo nɔewo
gravitixDocs.stringsDesc: Ka siwo le Gravitix me nye nuŋɔŋlɔ si woŋlɔ ɖe UTF-8 me. Wodoa alɔ nusiwo wotsɔ dea nyawo me si me kesinɔnuwo le kple dɔwɔwɔ siwo wotu ɖe eme ƒe hatsotso aɖe.
gravitixDocs.stringInterpolation: Ka ƒe nyawo tsɔtsɔ de eme
gravitixDocs.escapeSequences: Sisi ƒe ɖoɖo siwo kplɔ wo nɔewo ɖo
gravitixDocs.multilineStrings: Ka siwo le fli geɖe me
gravitixDocs.ifElifElse: ne / elif / ne menye nenema o
gravitixDocs.matchDesc: Kpɔɖeŋu-tsɔ asixɔxɔ aɖe sɔ kple kpɔɖeŋu geɖe. Bu eŋu be enye switch nyagbɔgblɔ sẽŋu aɖe.
gravitixDocs.functionsDesc: Dɔwɔwɔwo nye kɔpi ƒe ƒuƒoƒo siwo woate ŋu azã ake. Wokpena ɖe ŋuwò nèƒoa asa na nyawo gbugbɔgagblɔ eye wonaa kɔpiwo gɔmesese nɔa bɔbɔe.
gravitixDocs.eventHandlersDesc: Handlers nye Gravitix bot ƒe dzi. Dɔdzikpɔla ɖesiaɖe ɖoa to nudzɔdzɔ ƒomevi aɖe koŋ eye wòƒua du ne nudzɔdzɔ ma dzɔ.
gravitixDocs.guardDesc: Dzɔlawo ɖea mɔ na wò be nàtsɔ nɔnɔme aɖe akpe ɖe asiléla aɖe ŋu. Ne dzɔla la da asɔ be enye nyateƒe ko hafi amesi tsɔa nuawo ƒua du la ƒua du.
gravitixDocs.contextDesc: ctx nu la le eɖokui si le handler ɖesiaɖe me. Nyatakaka siwo ku ɖe gbedasi si li fifia kple ezãla si ɖoe ɖa ŋu le eme.
gravitixDocs.stateDesc: State na wò bot ɖoa ŋku nuwo dzi le gbedasiwo dome. Dukɔ manɔmee la, gbedasi ɖesiaɖe le eɖokui si eye ŋkuɖodzinu aɖeke mele bot la si o.
gravitixDocs.flowsDesc: Sisiwo nana nèŋlɔa dzeɖoɖo siwo me afɔɖeɖe geɖe le le atsyã si le fli me, si woate ŋu axlẽ me. Sisi ate ŋu atɔ vie ahalala zãla ƒe gbedasi si kplɔe ɖo.
gravitixDocs.declaringFlow: Gbeƒãɖeɖe be sisi aɖe le edzi yim
gravitixDocs.emitDesc: Gbedasiwo ɖoɖo ɖa nye nu vevitɔ si bot wɔna.
gravitixDocs.emitTo: emit_to — ɖoe ɖe zãla aɖe koŋ
gravitixDocs.schedulingDesc: Ƒu du code le ɖokuiwò si le ɖoɖowɔɖi aɖe dzi — cron dɔ aɖeke meli o, gotagome dɔwɔna aɖeke mehiã o.
gravitixDocs.everyRepeat: ɖesiaɖe — gbugbɔ le dometsotso aɖe dzi
gravitixDocs.atSpecificTime: at — du le ŋkekea ƒe ɣeyiɣi aɖe koŋ dzi
gravitixDocs.pipeDesc: Pipe operator |> tsɔa asixɔxɔ aɖe abe nyaʋiʋli gbãtɔ ene yia dɔwɔwɔ si kplɔe ɖo gbɔ. Enaa woxlẽa kɔsɔkɔsɔ ƒe dɔwɔwɔwo, tso miame yi ɖusime.
gravitixDocs.errorDesc: Gravitix naa vodadawo gbɔ kpɔkpɔ si woɖo ɖe ɖoɖo nu kple try / catch / finally blocks.
gravitixDocs.structsDesc: Structs na be nàƒo nyatakaka siwo do ƒome kplii nu ƒu ɖe ƒomevi si ŋkɔe nye te. Enums ɖea vovototodedeameme ƒe hatsotso si wotu la gɔme.
gravitixDocs.mathDesc: Gravitix ɖoa akɔntabubu ƒe agbalẽdzraɖoƒe si me nyawo katã le si me dɔwɔwɔ ~180 le.
gravitixDocs.mathConstants: Akɔntabubu ƒe nusiwo nɔa anyi ɖaa
gravitixDocs.geometryInterp: Geometry & Nusiwo wotsɔ dea nyawo me
gravitixDocs.complexDesc: Gravitix ƒe ƒomevi sesẽ aɖe le esi — 64-bit floats eve siwo tsi tre ɖi na akpa ŋutɔŋutɔwo kple esiwo wosusu.
gravitixDocs.calculusDesc: Gravitix lɔ xexlẽdzesiwo ƒe akɔntabubu ɖe eme: derivatives kple integrals siwo wobu ɖe dɔwɔwɔ siwo wodi dzi.
gravitixDocs.sequencesSeries: Nusiwo kplɔ wo nɔewo ɖo & Nusiwo kplɔ wo nɔewo ɖo
gravitixDocs.linearAlgebraDesc: Linear algebra nye vektor kple matriki ƒe akɔntabubu.
gravitixDocs.numberTheoryDesc: Xexlẽdzesiwo ŋuti nufiafia srɔ̃a nu tso xexlẽdzesi blibowo ƒe nɔnɔmewo ŋu. Egbegbe nya ɣaɣlawo ƒe gɔmeɖoanyi.
gravitixDocs.combinatorics: Combinatorics & Nusiwo kplɔ wo nɔewo ɖo
gravitixDocs.statisticsDesc: Akɔntabubu naa dɔwɔnu siwo woatsɔ aɖɔ, adzro eme, ahaƒo nya ta tso nyatakakawo me.
gravitixDocs.probabilityDist: Nusiwo ate ŋu adzɔ ƒe mamawo
gravitixDocs.specialDesc: Dɔwɔwɔ tɔxɛwo nye vovototodedeameme ƒe sɔsɔŋusẽ veviwo gbɔ kpɔnu.
gravitixDocs.gammaRelated: Gamma & dɔwɔwɔ siwo do ƒome kplii
gravitixDocs.ellipticIntegrals: Elliptic integrals (nu vevi siwo le abe elliptic ene).
gravitixDocs.fftDesc: Fourier ƒe tɔtrɔa gblẽa dzesi aɖe ɖe eƒe ɣeyiɣi ƒe didime siwo le eme la me.
gravitixDocs.bp1: Kpɔ nusiwo mènya o gbɔ ɣesiaɣi
gravitixDocs.bp2: Da asi ɖe zãla ƒe nyawo dzi
gravitixDocs.bp3: Zã dzɔlawo ɖe if-kɔsɔkɔsɔwo teƒe hena mɔɖeɖewo
gravitixDocs.bp4: Ðe susuŋudɔwɔwɔ ɖa le dɔwɔwɔwo me
gravitixDocs.bp5: Zã tɔtrɔ kaba be nàɖe atɔwɔwɔ dzi akpɔtɔ
gravitixDocs.bp8: Zã state nàtsɔ axe mɔ ɖe spam nu
gravitixDocs.keepLearningDesc: Mɔ nyuitɔ si dzi nàto asrɔ̃ Gravitix enye be nàtu. Dze egɔme kple echo bot bɔbɔe aɖe, emegbe nàtsɔ nɔnɔme akpe ɖe eŋu, emegbe nàtsɔ flows akpe ɖe eŋu. Nukpɔsusu yeye ɖesiaɖe si nèsrɔ̃ la ʋua mɔnukpɔkpɔ geɖe ɖi na wò.

bestpractices.h1: 1. Kpɔ nusiwo mènya o gbɔ ɣesiaɣi
bestpractices.h2: 2. Da asi ɖe zãla ƒe nyawo dzi
bestpractices.h3: 3. Zã guards ɖe if-kɔsɔkɔsɔwo teƒe hena mɔɖeɖewo
bestpractices.h4: 4. Ðe susuŋudɔwɔwɔ ɖa le dɔwɔwɔwo me
bestpractices.h5: 5. Zã return kaba nàtsɔ aɖe atɔwɔwɔ dzi akpɔtɔ
bestpractices.h8: 8. Zã state nàtsɔ axe mɔ ɖe spam nu
bestpractices.p1: Kpɔɖeŋuwo kple numame siwo nana wò Gravitix bots nye esiwo ŋu kakaɖedzi le wu, woate ŋu axlẽ wo, eye woate ŋu alé be na wo.
bestpractices.p2: Ele be fallback on msg handler nanɔ bot ɖesiaɖe si ale be ezãlawo naxɔ ŋuɖoɖo ɣesiaɣi:
bestpractices.p3: Mègasusui gbeɖe be nusi zãla la ɖo ɖa la sɔ o:
bestpractices.p4: Na asilélawo nanɔ kpuie. Tsɔ susuŋudɔwɔwɔ sesẽwo yi dɔwɔwɔ siwo ŋu ŋkɔ le gbɔ:
bestpractices.p6: Mɔ nyuitɔ si dzi nàto asrɔ̃ Gravitix enye be nàtu. Dze egɔme kple simple echo bot, emegbe nàtsɔ state akpe ɖe eŋu, emegbe nàtsɔ flows akpe ɖe eŋu. Nukpɔsusu yeye ɖesiaɖe si nèsrɔ̃ la ʋua mɔnukpɔkpɔ geɖe ɖi na wò.
bestpractices.h1_a: Kpɔɖeŋuwo kple numame siwo nana wò Gravitix bots nye esiwo ŋu kakaɖedzi le wu, woate ŋu axlẽ wo, eye woate ŋu alé be na wo.
bestpractices.h1_b: Implementation overview for "1. Kpɔ nusiwo mènya o gbɔ ɣesiaɣi". The mechanism is implemented in the corresponding module under `app/`, `ios/Modules/Sources/`, or `android/app/src/main/java/sol/vortexx/android/`. The module README calls out exact file paths.
bestpractices.h1_c: The "1. Kpɔ nusiwo mènya o gbɔ ɣesiaɣi" mechanism evolved across Vortex releases. The design traces back to published research and production practices of prior messengers. Version history in `CHANGELOG.md` records when each piece landed and what motivated it.

bitwise.cc10: Kpɔe ɖa be xexlẽdzesia sɔ hã
bitwise.cc11: XOR swap (tempo ƒe tɔtrɔ aɖeke meli o)
bitwise.cc5: Mɔɖeɖe ƒe aflagawo — bit ɖesiaɖe nye mɔɖeɖe
bitwise.cc7: Fast dzidziɖedzi / mama to ŋusẽ si nye 2
bitwise.h3: Dɔdasi si me nu vovovowo le
bitwise.p1: Bitwise operators wɔa dɔ tẽ ɖe xexlẽdzesi blibowo ƒe binary representation dzi. Xexlẽdzesi blibo ɖesiaɖe nye asixɔxɔ si wode asi na 64-bit; bitwise ops wɔa nu ɖe ​​eŋu abe bit 64 siwo kplɔ wo nɔewo ɖo ene.
bitwise.h1_a: Bitwise operators wɔa dɔ tẽ ɖe xexlẽdzesi blibowo ƒe binary representation dzi. Xexlẽdzesi blibo ɖesiaɖe nye asixɔxɔ si wode asi na 64-bit; bitwise ops wɔa nu ɖe ​​eŋu abe bit 64 siwo kplɔ wo nɔewo ɖo ene.

complex_type.ct1: 🌀 Nukatae xexlẽdzesi sesẽwo le bot gbe me?
complex_type.h1: Nukae nye xexlẽdzesi si me kɔ?
complex_type.h3: Dɔwɔwɔ sesẽ siwo wotu ɖe eme
complex_type.li5: Ŋusẽ: z**n zãa De Moivre ƒe mɔfiame na xexlẽdzesi blibo n
complex_type.p1: Xexlẽdzesi sesẽ aɖe ƒe nɔnɔme nye a + bi, afisi a nye akpa ŋutɔŋutɔ eye b nye akpa si wosusu. i nye susu me nu ɖeka si woɖe fia to i2 = −1 me. Xexlẽdzesi sesẽwo kekea fli ŋutɔŋutɔ ɖe enu va ɖoa yameʋu me, si wɔnɛ be wokpɔa sɔsɔŋuɖoɖowo abe x2 + 1 = 0 siwo ŋu ŋuɖoɖo ŋutɔŋutɔ aɖeke mele o.
complex_type.p2: Le Gravitix me la, èŋlɔa nya ŋutɔŋutɔ siwo wosusu kple i ƒe megbenya:
complex_type.p3: Dɔwɔwɔ vevi eneawo katã wɔa dɔ le asixɔxɔ sesẽwo ŋu tẽ. Gravitix zɔna ɖe akɔntabubu ƒe se sesẽ siwo wozãna ɖaa dzi:
complex_type.p4: Dzesiwo ŋuti dɔwɔwɔ (odio me dzodzro, FFT), fractal dzidzi (Mandelbrot ƒe ƒuƒoƒo), dzɔdzɔmeŋusẽŋununya ƒe nɔnɔmetatawo, elektrikŋusẽ ƒe mɔxexeɖedɔléle ƒe akɔntabubu, kple quantum akɔntabubu katã bia akɔntabubu sesẽwo. Gravitix na be nàte ŋu awɔ akɔntabubu siawo le Vortex bot me tututu gotagome agbalẽdzraɖoƒewo manɔmee.
complex_type.td12: Nyaʋiʋli (dzogoe le radians me): atan2(b, a) .
complex_type.td14: Tso polar nɔnɔme me: r·e^(iθ) = r·cos θ + ir·sin θ
complex_type.td20: Ke vevitɔ si nye square root
complex_type.td24: Trig dɔwɔwɔwo keke ta va ɖo yameʋu sesẽ
complex_type.td6: z ƒe akpa si wosusu le susu me
complex_type.td8: Kpekpeɖeŋunya si me kɔ: a+bi → a−bi

error.p1: Xatsa kɔda ɖesiaɖe si ateŋu ado kpo nu ɖe ​​try ƒe mɔxenu me. Ne vodada aɖe nye thrown — to Gravitix runtime alo tẽ to wò code dzi — ewuwu ti kpo yi catch block la gbɔ. Wobla asixɔxɔ si wolé la ɖe tɔtrɔɖenu si nètsɔ ŋkɔ na catch ŋu.
error.p2: finally ƒua du nɔnɔme aɖeke manɔmee — eɖanye try la kpɔ dzidzedze alo catch kpɔ vodada aɖe gbɔ o. Zãe nàtsɔ aɖe asi le nunɔamesiwo ŋu, awɔ nuŋlɔɖi, alo aɖo gbedasi si ku ɖe nuwuwu ŋu.
error.p3: Zã vodadawo gbɔ kpɔkpɔ na dɔwɔwɔ siwo nɔ te ɖe gotagome nyawo tsɔtsɔ yi eme dzi: zãla ƒe nuŋɔŋlɔwo me toto, APIwo yɔyɔ, nuto ƒe tɔtrɔwo xexlẽ, alo akɔntabubu sesẽwo wɔwɔ le afisi domenyinyi ƒe vodadawo (sqrt of negative, log of zero) ateŋu adzɔ le. Le ememe susuŋudɔwɔwɔ ƒe vodadawo gome la, lɔ̃ guard nyagbewo kple return gbãtɔwo.
error.h1_a: Xatsa kɔda ɖesiaɖe si ateŋu ado kpo nu ɖe ​​try ƒe mɔxenu me. Ne vodada aɖe nye thrown — to Gravitix runtime alo tẽ to wò code dzi — ewuwu ti kpo yi catch block la gbɔ. Wobla asixɔxɔ si wolé la ɖe tɔtrɔɖenu si nètsɔ ŋkɔ na catch ŋu.

intro.li1: Readable — kɔda xlẽna abe tameɖoɖo ene, ke menye mɔ̃wo o
intro.li2: Safe — static ƒomeviwo catch vodadawo hafi dɔwɔwɔ
intro.li3: Nyaɖeɖefia — flows, guards, kple kpɔɖeŋu matching nye esiwo wotu ɖe eme
intro.li4: Minimal — klass aɖeke meli o, domenyinu aɖeke meli o, ɖoɖo aɖeke meli si woasrɔ̃ o
intro.p1: Gravitix nye gbegbɔgblɔ si ku ɖe domenyinyi tɔxɛ aɖe ŋu (DSL) si wowɔ tso gɔmedzedzea me ke hena Vortex bots ŋɔŋlɔ. Le afisi gbe bubuwo hiã agbalẽdzraɖoƒewo, yɔyɔwo, kple boilerplate le la, Gravitix naa nudzɔdzɔwo dzikpɔla siwo le ɖoƒe gbãtɔ, dzeɖoɖo flows, kple state siwo wotu ɖe eme — wo katã le nyagɔmeɖegbalẽ dzadzɛ, si woate ŋu axlẽ si tso Rust gbɔ me.
intro.p2: Gravitix nye na amesiame si di be yeatu Vortex bot kaba — eɖanye mèŋlɔ kɔpi kpɔ o, alo ènye dɔwɔla bibi si Python boilerplate ti kɔ na wò o. Numekugbalẽ sia fiaa nu Gravitix tso gɔmeɖose gbãtɔwo me: nuteƒekpɔkpɔ aɖeke mehiã do ŋgɔ o.
intro.p3: Akpa ɖesiaɖe tua esiwo do ŋgɔ ɖo. Ne ènye ame yeye le ɖoɖowɔɖiwo me la, xlẽe tso dzi va anyi. Ne ènya gbe bubu xoxo la, zã emenyawo tsɔ ti kpo yi nusi do dzidzɔ na wò gbɔ tẽ.
intro.p4: Le nufiame sia katã me la, → gɔmee nye "da asɔ na" alo "ewɔa", eye // ... fia nya siwo dzi nuƒoƒoƒula ŋe aɖaba ƒu.
intro.h1_a: Gravitix nye gbegbɔgblɔ si ku ɖe domenyinyi tɔxɛ aɖe ŋu (DSL) si wowɔ tso gɔmedzedzea me ke hena Vortex bots ŋɔŋlɔ. Le afisi gbe bubuwo hiã agbalẽdzraɖoƒewo, yɔyɔwo, kple boilerplate le la, Gravitix naa nudzɔdzɔwo dzikpɔla siwo le ɖoƒe gbãtɔ, dzeɖoɖo flows, kple state siwo wotu ɖe eme — wo katã le nyagɔmeɖegbalẽ dzadzɛ, si woate ŋu axlẽ si tso Rust gbɔ me.

math_calculus.cc10: Ele be nutome si le gotagome afã kple afã si ƒe didime nye 1: nanye π/2
math_calculus.cc11: Asixɔxɔ si wokpɔ mɔ na: E[X] = ∫ x·p(x) dx le mama si sɔ dzi
math_calculus.cc14: Taylor ƒe xexlẽdzesiwo na e^x ƒo xlã x=0: 1 + x + x2/2! + x3/3 kple afã! + ...
math_calculus.cc16: Newton ƒe mɔnu ƒe afɔɖeɖe: x1 = x1 − f(x1)/f'(x1) .
math_calculus.cc2: X2 ƒe nu evelia si woɖe tso emee nye 2 (akɔta si nɔa anyi ɖaa) .
math_calculus.cc3: Duƒuƒu tso teƒe si wole ŋuti nyatakakawo me
math_calculus.h1: Nusiwo kplɔ wo nɔewo ɖo & Nusiwo kplɔ wo nɔewo ɖo
math_calculus.p1: derivative f'(x) dzidzea alesi f trɔna kabakabae le teƒe x. Le geometri gome la, enye tangent line ƒe dzidzeme. Gravitix zãa titina vovototo na gbãtɔ siwo woɖe tso eme (vodada O(h2)) kple second-order formula na evelia ƒe dzɔdzɔmenuwo.
math_calculus.p2: integral ∫f(x)dx tso a vaseɖe b nye teƒe si le fli la te. Gravitix na mɔnu etɔ̃ siwo ƒe nyateƒetoto le dzidzim ɖe edzi:
math_calculus.td12: O(h4), si de pɛpɛpɛ wu sã
math_calculus.td13: Richardson ƒe nya si wogblɔ le gota
math_calculus.td17: Nuƒoƒoƒu Σ f(k) na k = tso..vaseɖe
math_calculus.td18: Nusi wowɔ Π f(k) na k = tso..vaseɖe
math_calculus.td19: Da akɔ le xexlẽdzesi geɖe ƒe Σ xexlẽdzesiwo[k]·xᵏ ŋu
math_calculus.td2: Gbãtɔ si woɖe tso eme le teƒe x
math_calculus.td6: Xexlẽdzesi si woɖe tso nyatakaka teƒewo me
math_calculus.td8: Gradient vektor si nye f le (x,y) .

math_complex.cc1: Teƒe aɖe tɔtrɔ ɖe dzogoe dzi theta le 2D me
math_complex.cc10: (−1)^(1/3) ƒe ke etɔ̃ le kubi me le yameʋu si me kɔ me
math_complex.cc2: sɔ kple e^(i·theta) dzi ɖeɖe kpɔtɔ .
math_complex.cc4: Ðekawɔwɔ ƒe kewo: 1 ƒe ke n-th nye e^(2πik/n) na k=0..n-1
math_complex.cc6: Möbius ƒe tɔtrɔ tsɔa nutome/fliwo ƒe nɔnɔmetatawo yia gotagome/fliwo dzi
math_complex.cc8: kple a=1,b=0,c=0,d=1 esia nye dzesideŋkɔ: w = z
math_complex.h1: Yameʋu si me nu sesẽwo le
math_complex.h3: Dɔ siwo wowɔna le numekuku me
math_complex.li2: Nyaʋiʋli arg(z) = atan2(b, a) — dzogoe tso axa ŋutɔŋutɔ nyuitɔ gbɔ
math_complex.li3: Polar ƒe nɔnɔme z = |z|·e^(i·arg(z)) = |z|·(cos θ + i·sin θ)
math_complex.p1: Woateŋu akpɔ xexlẽdzesi sesẽ ɖesiaɖe z = a + bi le susu me abe teƒe (a, b) le Argand plane dzi. Axis si le tsia dzi lae nye axis ŋutɔŋutɔ, axis si le tsitrenu lae nye axis si wosusu.
math_complex.p2: Dzesidenu vevitɔwo dometɔ ɖeka le akɔntabubu me: e^(ix) = cos x + i·sin x. Esia fia be wotsɔ dzogoe x tsɔ parametrized unit circle si le complex plane la me.
math_complex.p3: Wokekea xexlẽdzesi, sine, cosine, kple logarithm si sesẽ la ɖe enu va ɖoa yameʋu si me kɔ bliboe la gbɔ. Woƒe Taylor ƒe xexlẽdzesiwo ƒoa ƒu everyafisi (alo le disk dzi hena nuŋlɔɖi).
math_complex.p4: Le z kple w siwo gɔme sese sesẽ gome la, z^w = e^(w·ln z). Esia ɖea mɔ na xexlẽdzesi siwo menye xexlẽdzesi blibo o kple esiwo sesẽ, si ɖea kadodo wɔnukuwo fiana:
math_complex.td10: Kpo siwo le z = π/2 + nπ
math_complex.td4: Alɔdzedɔwɔƒe vevitɔ; discontinuous le negative real axis dzi
math_complex.h1_a: Woateŋu akpɔ xexlẽdzesi sesẽ ɖesiaɖe z = a + bi le susu me abe teƒe (a, b) le Argand plane dzi. Axis si le tsia dzi lae nye axis ŋutɔŋutɔ, axis si le tsitrenu lae nye axis si wosusu.

math_core.cc1: Cosines ƒe se: c2 = a2 + b2 − 2ab·cos(C) .
math_core.cc3: Ðetsɔleme si wotsɔ ƒo ƒui: A = P·e^(rt) .
math_core.h3: Geometri & Nusiwo wotsɔ dea nyawo me
math_core.p1: Trig dɔwɔwɔwo katã kpɔa mɔ na dzogoewo le radians me. Zã radians(deg) nàtsɔ atrɔ tso dzidzenuwo dzi.
math_core.td10: Euler–Mascheroni ƒe γ si nɔa anyi ɖaa
math_core.td16: Cosine ƒe ŋkɔ. cos (0) = 1, cos (π) = −1 ƒe ƒuƒoƒo
math_core.td17: Tangent = nuvɔ̃/cos. Womeɖe egɔme le π/2 + nπ o
math_core.td18: Arcsine ƒe ŋkɔ. Domenyinyi [−1, 1], dometsotso [−π/2, π/2] ƒe didime.
math_core.td19: Arccosine ƒe atike. Domenyinyi [−1, 1], didime [0, π].
math_core.td2: Kplɔ̃ɖoƒe ƒe gotagome kple didime ƒe sɔsɔme
math_core.td20: Arctangent (Actangent) si nye nusi wotsɔna ƒoa ƒui. Afisi wòle (−π/2, π/2) .
math_core.td21: Dzogoe si me vektor (x,y) le tso x-axis nyui dzi. Range (−π, π]. Asiléƒewo x=0.
math_core.td22: Dɔwɔwɔ siwo gbɔ eme: sinh(x)=(eˣ−e−ˣ)/2, kple bubuawo.
math_core.td23: Dɔwɔwɔ siwo nye hyperbolic siwo trɔna ɖe megbe
math_core.td25: Trɔ degreewo woazu radians: d × π/180
math_core.td26: Dzɔdzɔme log (gɔmeɖoanyi e). ln (e) = 1, ln (1) = 0. Domenyi x > 0. Ƒe 1999 me.
math_core.td27: Binary log. log2(1024) = 10. Bit ƒe xexlẽme si hiã.
math_core.td28: Nuŋlɔɖi si bɔ. log11(1000) = 3. Ðoɖo si nu woƒe lolome le.
math_core.td29: Gɔmeɖoanyi si wowɔ le wo ɖokui si: log_b (x) = ln (x) / ln (b) .
math_core.td34: Goglo va ɖo xexlẽdzesi blibo si te ɖe eŋu wu (si didi tso zero gbɔ afã) .
math_core.td35: Klãe ɖe zero ŋu: trunc(3.9) = 3, trunc(−3.9) = −3
math_core.td36: Akpa si le akpa sue aɖe me: akpa sue (3.7) = 0.7, akpa sue (−3.7) = −0.7
math_core.td39: Nyateƒe ne |a−b| < eps (1e-9 ƒe gɔmedzedze). Dedie float tsɔtsɔ sɔ kple wo nɔewo.
math_core.td4: Euler ƒe xexlẽdzesi, si nye dzɔdzɔme logaritm ƒe gɔmeɖoanyi
math_core.td40: Ke si le dzogoe ene me. sqrt (2) ≈ 1.41421 ƒe ɣleti gbãtɔ...
math_core.td41: Kubi ƒe ke. cbrt (8) = 2 ƒe xexlẽme
math_core.td43: √(x2+y2) — hypotenuse ƒe didime, si li ke le xexlẽdzesi nu

math_linalg.cc1: Dzogoe si le vektor eve dome
math_linalg.cc3: [[2,1], [1,2]] ƒe asixɔxɔ tɔxɛwo: λ = 3 kple λ = 1
math_linalg.p1: Vektor nye xexlẽdzesiwo ƒe xexlẽdzesi siwo woɖo ɖe ɖoɖo nu. Ate ŋu atsi tre ɖi na teƒe aɖe le yamenutome, mɔfiame aɖe, sesẽme aɖe, alo nɔnɔme aɖe le ML me.
math_linalg.p2: Matrix nye xexlẽdzesiwo ƒe ƒuƒoƒo si le 2D me. m×n matriki ƒe fli m kple sɔti n le esi. Matrix dɔwɔwɔwoe nyea flidzidzedze ƒe nu vevitɔ.
math_linalg.td1: Dot ƒe nuwɔna: Σ aᵢ·bᵢ. Emetsonu nye scalar. dometsotso (a,b) = |a||b|cos θ
math_linalg.td10: Matrix dzidziɖedzi (A nye m×k, B nye k×n → emetsonu m×n)
math_linalg.td11: Tsɔ every element dzi ɖe edzi kple scalar s
math_linalg.td12: Trɔ fliwo kple sɔtiwo: Aᵀ[i][j] = A[j][i].
math_linalg.td13: Nusi dea dzesii (LU gbegblẽ). Menye zero o ↔ nusi woate ŋu atrɔ.
math_linalg.td14: Matriki A−1 si trɔ megbe ale be A·A−1 = I (Gauss-Jordan) .
math_linalg.td16: Fli/sɔti siwo le wo ɖokui si le fli me ƒe xexlẽme
math_linalg.td17: n×n dzeside matriki (1s le diagonal dzi) .
math_linalg.td19: Kpɔ flidziɖoɖo Ax = b gbɔ to Gauss ƒe ɖeɖeɖa dzi
math_linalg.td2: 3D vektor eve ƒe atitsoga ƒe nuwɔna. Emetsonu ⊥ evea siaa ƒe nyawo. |a×b| = |a||b|nuvɔ̃ θ
math_linalg.td20: Eigenvalues of 2×2 matrix (mɔfiame si sɔ pɛpɛpɛ) .
math_linalg.td4: Unit vektor: v / |v|. Didime va zua 1.
math_linalg.td7: Tsɔ scalar s dzidze nu ɖesiaɖe ɖe edzi
math_linalg.td8: Element-nunya me tsɔtsɔ kpe ɖe matriki siwo ƒe lolome sɔ ŋu

math_numth.cc1: RSA dzidzime veviwo ɖoa ŋu ɖe modinv ŋu:
math_numth.cc4: Goldbach ƒe nususugblɔ ƒe dodokpɔwɔla: every n>2 gɔ̃ hã nye xexlẽdzesi gbãtɔ eve ƒe ƒuƒoƒo
math_numth.h3: Nusiwo wotsɔ ƒo ƒui & Nusiwo kplɔ wo nɔewo ɖo
math_numth.td1: Mamãla gãtɔ kekeake si bɔ to Euclidean algorithm dzi. gcd (12,8) = 4. Ƒe 1999 me
math_numth.td13: Mɔ siwo dzi woato atia k tso n
math_numth.td15: Tiatia siwo woɖo ɖe ɖoɖo nu
math_numth.td19: Stirling xexlẽdzesiwo ƒe ƒomevi evelia: n ƒe mama ɖe k ƒe hatsotso sue siwo menye ƒuƒlu o me
math_numth.td2: Agbɔsɔsɔme si bɔ wu: a·b / gcd(a,b) .
math_numth.td21: Catalangbe me xexlẽdzesiwo: akɔtagbalẽviwo ƒe ɖoɖo, ati eve
math_numth.td23: Harmonic series ƒe akpa aɖe ƒe ƒuƒoƒo
math_numth.td25: Bernoulli ƒe xexlẽdzesiwo (edze le Taylor ƒe trig/log ƒe xexlẽdzesiwo me) .
math_numth.td3: N ƒe mama nyuiwo katã, woɖo wo ɖe hatsotsowo me. mamalawo(12)=[1,2,3,4,6,12] .
math_numth.td4: Euler ƒe totient φ(n): xexlẽdzesi blibo siwo le [1,n] me ƒe xexlẽme coprime va ɖo n
math_numth.td5: Modular inverse: x ale be a·x ≡ 1 (mod m). Wozãe le RSA me.
math_numth.td6: Modular ƒe ɖeɖefia kabakaba: base ^ exp mod. O(log exp) ƒe agbalẽ.
math_numth.td7: Miller-Rabin ƒe dodokpɔ gbãtɔ (si woɖo ɖi na n < 3.2×1018)
math_numth.td8: Primes katã va ɖo n to Sieve of Eratosthenes dzi
math_numth.td9: Prime factorization abe xexlẽdzesi ene. nu veviwo (12) = [2,2,3].

math_special.cc12: Ellipse ƒe gotagome kple afã-axes a, b:
math_special.cc2: P(−σ < X < σ) na mama si sɔ
math_special.cc3: Apéry ƒe nusi nɔa anyi ɖaa: ζ(3) nye nusi me susu mele o gake womenya nɔnɔme si wotu o
math_special.cc4: J1(x): eʋuʋuna eye wògblẽna — ɖɔa ƒutsotsoe siwo le dzogoe ene me
math_special.cc5: Y(x): eɖea eɖokui ɖa le x=0, eʋuʋuna na x gã
math_special.cc6: Ai gblẽna le mɔ si sɔ nu na x→+∞, eʋuʋuna na x→-∞
math_special.cc9: Ɣeyiɣi si me pendulum si ƒe amplitude θ₀ le:
math_special.h1: Gamma & dɔ siwo do ƒome kplii
math_special.h7: Elliptic integrals (nu vevi siwo le abe elliptic ene).
math_special.p1: Gamma dɔwɔwɔ Γ(n) nye factorial ƒe generalization si yia edzi: Γ(n) = (n−1)! na xexlẽdzesi blibo nyuiwo. Ekekea xexlẽdzesi siwo nye factorial ɖe enu va ɖoa xexlẽdzesi ŋutɔŋutɔwo kple esiwo gɔme sese sesẽ dzi.
math_special.p2: Vodada ƒe dɔwɔwɔ erf(x) = (2/√π)∫₀ˣ e^(−t2)dt nye Gauss ƒe integral. Edo ƒome tẽ kple CDF si sɔ eye wòdzena le dzoxɔxɔ ƒe sɔsɔ kple nusiwo ate ŋu adzɔ ƒe nufiafia me.
math_special.p3: ζ(wo) = Σ 1/nˢ na n=1,2,3,... (ne Re(wo)>1). Do ƒome kple prime mama to Euler ƒe nuwɔna dzi. Riemann ƒe Nukpɔsusu (si gbɔ womekpɔ o!) ku ɖe ζ ƒe zero siwo le yameʋu si me kɔ la me ŋu.
math_special.p4: Bessel ƒe dɔwɔwɔ Jₙ(x) kple Y3(x) nye Bessel ƒe vovototodedeameme ƒe sɔsɔŋuɖoɖo ƒe egbɔkpɔnuwo: x2y'' + xy' + (x2−n2)y = 0. Wodzena le ƒutsotsoewo ƒe kaka, dzoxɔxɔ ƒe tsɔtsɔ le tsilindowo me, kple quantum mechanics me.
math_special.p5: Ai(x) kple Bi(x) nye y'' = xy ƒe egbɔkpɔnuwo. Wodzena le quantum tunneling (WKB approximation) kple optics (diffraction) me.
math_special.p6: Polynomial ƒome siawo dona abe eigenfunctions of important operators eye wozãa wo le spectral mɔnuwo, quadrature, kple quantum mechanics me.
math_special.p7: K(k) kple E(k) nye elliptic integrals blibo siwo le ƒomevi gbãtɔ kple evelia me. Wobua ɣeyiɣi si pendulum si mele fli me o kple ellipse ƒe gotagome.
math_special.td1: Γ(x) = ∫₀^∞ tˣ−1e−dt. Lanczos ƒe akɔntabubu. Γ(n+1)=n, ʋeʋeʋe!
math_special.td2: ln|Γ(x)|. Avoids wuflow na x gã.
math_special.td5: P3(x): dzogoe si le tsiatrenu le [−1,1] dzi. Haidrodzin ƒe ƒutsotsoewo ƒe dɔwɔwɔ ƒe akpa si le dzogoe ene me.
math_special.td6: H3(x): dzogoe si le tsiatrenu w.r.t. e^(−x2) ƒe ŋkɔ. Quantum harmonic ƒe ʋuʋudedi.
math_special.td7: T3 (x) = cos (n·acos (x)): xexlẽdzesi geɖe ƒe tsɔtsɔ sɔ kple wo nɔewo si sɔ nyuie wu.
math_special.td8: L3(x): le dzogoe ene me le [0,∞) dzi w.r.t. e^(−x) ƒe nɔnɔme. Hydrogen radial wavefunctions ƒe dɔwɔwɔwo.
math_special.h1_a: Gamma dɔwɔwɔ Γ(n) nye factorial ƒe generalization si yia edzi: Γ(n) = (n−1)! na xexlẽdzesi blibo nyuiwo. Ekekea xexlẽdzesi siwo nye factorial ɖe enu va ɖoa xexlẽdzesi ŋutɔŋutɔwo kple esiwo gɔme sese sesẽ dzi.

math_stats.cc2: Amewo ƒe akpa sue kae xɔ dzesi si mede 600 o le SAT (μ=500, σ=100) dzi?
math_stats.cc3: Anɔ eme be ta 3 pɛpɛpɛ le gaku 10 dzɔdzɔe flips me
math_stats.cc4: Le mama dedie nu la, asisi 2/aɖabaƒoƒo, kakaɖedzi be 5 pɛpɛpɛ le miniti ɖeka me?
math_stats.cc5: Wɔ nyatakaka siwo sɔ le vome
math_stats.h1: Kadodo kple ƒomedodo & Covariance ƒe vovototodedeameme
math_stats.h2: Nusiwo ate ŋu adzɔ ƒe mamawo
math_stats.li1: r = 1: ƒomedodo nyui deblibo (ne X le tsitsim la, Y hã le tsitsim ɖe ɖoɖo nu) .
math_stats.li3: r ≈ 0: ƒomedodo aɖeke mele fli me o
math_stats.p1: Ne wotsɔ xexlẽdzesiwo ƒe xexlẽdzesiwo na la, dɔwɔwɔ siawo ƒoa nyatakakaawo ƒe titina ƒe nɔnɔme kple kaka nu ƒu kpuie:
math_stats.p2: Covariance cov(X,Y) = E[(X−μl)(Y−μᵧ)] dzidzea nenye be tɔtrɔ eve ʋãna ɖekae. Kadodo r = cov(X,Y)/(σl·σᵧ) na esia trɔna ɖe ɖoɖo nu wòzua [−1, 1]:
math_stats.p3: linreg(xs, ys) sɔ kple fli nyuitɔ y = a + bx to nyatakaka teƒewo to square suetɔ dzrowo zazã me. Trɔ gbɔ [slope, intercept, r_squared].
math_stats.td11: CDF: P(X ≤ x) na mama si sɔ. Zãa erf ƒe tsɔtsɔ sɔ kple wo nɔewo.
math_stats.td12: P(X=k) = e^(−λ)·λᵏ/k! na Poisson ƒe mama
math_stats.td14: float si sɔ le vome le [0, 1) me.
math_stats.td15: Kpɔɖeŋu tso N(μ,σ) me to Box-Muller ƒe tɔtrɔ zazã me
math_stats.td2: Akɔntabubu ƒe mama dedie: Σxᵢ / n
math_stats.td3: Titina ƒe asixɔxɔ ne woɖoe ɖe ɖoɖo nu. Le n gɔ̃ hã gome la, titina ƒe asixɔxɔ eve ƒe mamã dedie.
math_stats.td4: Asixɔxɔ si wozãna zi geɖe wu
math_stats.td5: Mamã dedie ƒe vovototo si le dzogoe ene me tso mama dedie nu: Σ(xᵢ−μ)2/n
math_stats.td6: Deviation si woɖo ɖi: √vovototo. Unit mawo ke abe data ene.
math_stats.td7: p-th ƒe alafa memamã (0≤p≤100). p=50 nye titina.
math_stats.td8: Normalize to zero mamã dedie unit variance: (xᵢ−μ)/σ

math_transforms.cc2: (kpɔɖeŋu ƒe agbɔsɔsɔme = 8 kpɔɖeŋuwo na wɔwɔfia, zazã ŋutɔŋutɔ anye 44100)
math_transforms.cc3: spectrum[1] ƒe amplitude ~1.0 (gɔmedzedze ƒe ɣeyiɣi ƒe didime) .
math_transforms.cc5: Kpɔ frequency bin ɖesiaɖe ƒe lolome ɖa
math_transforms.cc6: Convolution: zã aɖaka me filter (moving average) ɖe nyatakaka siwo le bɔbɔe ŋu
math_transforms.cc7: le mama dedie nu la, aƒelika 3 ye
math_transforms.cc8: Mɔzɔzɔ yiyi kple gbɔgbɔ: fft → ifft ele be wòagaxɔ gbãtɔ
math_transforms.cc9: wogbugbɔ ≈ dzesi (le floating-teƒe ƒe nyateƒetoto me)
math_transforms.h1: Trɔ asi le ame ŋu & FFT ƒe .
math_transforms.li1: |X[k]| nye amplitude si nye frequency f = k·sampleRate/N
math_transforms.li3: FFT la bua akɔnta le emetsonu ma ke si nye DFT gake le O(n log n) vs O(n2) — na n=1024 la, ema nye dɔwɔwɔ 1024 vs miliɔn 1
math_transforms.p1: Ne wona N kpɔɖeŋu siwo kplɔ wo nɔewo ɖo x[0], x[1], ..., x[N−1] la, Discrete Fourier Transform la wɔa N complex frequency components X[k]:
math_transforms.p2: MP3 kple AAC odio ƒe nutete, JPEG nɔnɔmetata ƒe nutete, 5G kaƒomɔ̃ ƒe nyatakakawo ɖoɖoɖa, radar ƒe dzesiwo ŋuti dɔwɔwɔ, kple MRI nɔnɔmetatawo kpɔkpɔstruction katã ɖoa ŋu ɖe FFT ƒe akɔntabubu ŋu. Egɔmesese naa nèsea egbegbe mɔ̃ɖaŋununya ƒe akpa gã aɖe gɔme.
math_transforms.td1: Fourier ƒe Tɔtrɔ Kpata (Cooley-Tukey). xs: xexlẽdzesi floats alo complex. Trɔa xexlẽdzesi siwo le complex. Nu nyuitɔ kekeake na N = ŋusẽ si nye 2.
math_transforms.td2: Trɔ asi le FFT ŋu. Reconstructs ɣeyiɣi-domenyinyi dzesi tso spektrum.
math_transforms.td3: Naive O(n2) DFT ƒe ŋkɔ. Nu ma ke si do tso eme abe fft ene, wɔa dɔ na N ɖesiaɖe.
math_transforms.td4: Ʋuʋudedi: (a★b)[n] = Σₖ a[k]·b[n−k]. Wozãnɛ le FIR ƒe ʋuʋudediwo me.
math_transforms.td5: Da Laplace ƒe tɔtrɔ ƒe polynomial kpɔ le complex s
math_transforms.h1_a: Ne wona N kpɔɖeŋu siwo kplɔ wo nɔewo ɖo x[0], x[1], ..., x[N−1] la, Discrete Fourier Transform la wɔa N complex frequency components X[k]:

architexDocs.title: Architex Language Reference
architexDocs.subtitle: A declarative UI language for Vortex Mini Apps. Reactive state, composable layouts, zero build step.
architexDocs.introDesc: Architex is a small declarative DSL designed to ship UI inside the Vortex messenger. You describe what the screen should look like; the runtime keeps pixels in sync with state automatically. No virtual DOM, no bundler, no JSX — the `.arx` file is the app.
architexDocs.goalDeclarative: Declarative — you write the UI you want, not the steps to build it
architexDocs.goalReactive: Reactive — `~count += 1` and every label that reads it updates in place
architexDocs.goalPortable: Portable — the same `.arx` runs on web, iOS, and Android runtimes without edits
architexDocs.goalTiny: Tiny — parser + renderer ship in under 30 KB, so Mini Apps open instantly
architexDocs.goalReadable: Readable — indentation is structure; modifiers are chained with `::`; there is no punctuation tax
architexDocs.whoDesc: Anyone who wants to ship an interactive screen in Vortex — customer forms, dashboards, voting pages, Telegram-style Mini Apps. If you have ever written HTML+CSS+JS, Architex will feel familiar but faster to write. If you haven't, Architex is also a gentle introduction to reactive programming because the language keeps the footprint small.
architexDocs.howToReadDesc: Each section builds on the one before. Start at Quick Start, then read Syntax, State, Layout, Modifiers, Actions, and the advanced section last. Jump around via the table of contents if you already know React or Svelte — the concepts map cleanly.
architexDocs.quickStartDesc: The shortest path from a blank file to a running Mini App.
architexDocs.firstScreenExample:
@screen Hello

  ~name = "world"

  col :: pad(24) gap(12) center

    header "Hello, {~name}!" :: bold size(28)

    input ~name :: pad(8) radius(6) border(#ccc)

    text "Type a name above — this text updates as you type."
      :: size(12) color(#888)
architexDocs.whatHappenedDesc: `@screen Hello` declares the root. `~name = "world"` creates a reactive variable. `col`, `header`, `input`, `text` are built-in components. `::` attaches modifiers (padding, colours, size). When the user types into the input, every `{~name}` interpolation updates — no wiring, no subscriptions, no renders by hand.
architexDocs.runningItDesc: Open the Architex tab in the Vortex bot studio, paste the code, press Run. The runtime renders into the preview pane and hot-reloads on save. When you publish, your users open the app from any chat — no installs.
architexDocs.syntaxDesc: Every Architex program is made of four things: declarations that start with `@`, reactive variables prefixed with `~`, components written as bare identifiers, and modifiers attached after `::`.
architexDocs.anatomyDeclDesc: `@screen Name`, `@theme`, `@import "./lib.arx"`, and `@for item in ~list` all start in column 0. Everything indented below them belongs to that block.
architexDocs.anatomyCompDesc: Components sit on their own line: `text`, `button`, `image`, `header`, `label`, `badge`, `divider`, `col`, `row`. Their positional argument comes right after the name: `text ~message`, `button "Save"`, `image "/logo.png"`.
architexDocs.anatomyModifierDesc: After `::` you can chain as many modifiers as you like. Each takes zero or more arguments in parentheses: `:: pad(24) gap(16) center bold size(20) color(#4f8ef7)`. Modifiers are positional, not keyword — order does not matter except for the last-writer-wins ones like `color`.
architexDocs.anatomyActionDesc: `=>` on a button (or any interactive component) runs the assignment or function call that follows. Multiple `=>` chain side-by-side: `button "Submit" => ~busy = true => send(action: "save", data: ~form)`.
architexDocs.reactiveVarsDesc: Any name prefixed with `~` is a reactive cell. Write `~count = 0` to declare, `~count = 5` to re-assign, `~count += 1` to update. Every UI node that reads the cell — directly or via interpolation — re-renders when the cell changes.
architexDocs.computedVarsDesc: Use `:=` instead of `=` to declare a computed expression: `~total := ~count * ~step`. The right-hand side re-evaluates whenever any dependency changes. There is no manual subscription and no stale-value bug — if you read `~a` somewhere in the expression, you automatically depend on it.
architexDocs.typeAnnotationsDesc: Annotations are optional but help with autocomplete and error messages: `~count: number = 0`, `~name: string = ""`, `~ok: boolean = false`, `~items: array = []`, `~user: object = {}`. If you skip them the type is inferred from the initialiser.
architexDocs.supportedTypesList: `number`, `string`, `boolean`, `array`, `object`, `null`. Dates and blobs are passed through as strings and objects respectively. The runtime uses JavaScript's coercion rules under the hood, so `~count + 1` works even if `~count` started life as a string.
architexDocs.stringInterpolationDesc: Any string can embed expressions with `{...}`: `"Hello, {~user.name}!"`, `"Total: {~count * ~price}"`. Interpolation is always reactive.
architexDocs.layoutDesc: Architex has two composable containers and a dozen leaf components. Nest them with indentation — no closing tags.
architexDocs.containersDesc: `col` stacks children vertically, `row` horizontally. Both accept `gap(n)`, `pad(n)`, `center`, `grow`, and a width/height modifier. Indent children one level deeper to nest.
architexDocs.containerExample:
row :: gap(8) pad(16) center
  image "/avatar.png" :: w(40) h(40) radius(20)
  col :: grow gap(2)
    text ~name :: bold
    text ~bio  :: size(12) color(#888)
architexDocs.leafComponentsList: `text` — any body copy. `header` — semantically a heading. `label` — small caption text. `button` — tap-able control. `input` — text field bound to a reactive var. `image` — picture from a URL. `badge` — pill-shaped tag. `divider` — thin separator line. `toast` — ephemeral snackbar. `tabs` / `tab` — tab switcher.
architexDocs.componentTextDesc: `text "Hello"` renders a static string. `text ~message` renders a reactive one. Combine with interpolation: `text "Total: {~count}"`.
architexDocs.componentHeaderDesc: Heading text. Defaults to larger, bolder than `text`. Same positional argument: `header "Settings"`. Size scales with screen — override with `size(n)`.
architexDocs.componentButtonDesc: Tap-able element. The positional string is the label; the `=>` action runs on tap. `button "Save" => ~draft = null`. Multiple `=>` fire in order.
architexDocs.componentInputDesc: Two-way-bound text field. The reactive variable after the name holds the current value: `input ~email`. Modifiers: `placeholder("…")`, `debounce(300)` for throttled updates.
architexDocs.componentImageDesc: Loads from an HTTPS URL or `/uploads/...` path. Modifiers `w(px)`, `h(px)`, `radius(px)` trim the box. Falls back to a placeholder silhouette if the URL 404s.
architexDocs.componentDividerDesc: Hairline separator, 1px, honours the current theme colour. No arguments.
architexDocs.componentBadgeDesc: Pill that hugs its content: `badge ~count`, `badge "new"`. Use for unread counters and tag chips.
architexDocs.componentLabelDesc: Caption/helper text — visually subdued. Good for form field names.
architexDocs.componentToastDesc: Non-blocking notification. `toast ~visible "Saved!" :: duration(2000)` shows for 2 s whenever `~visible` is true, then auto-dismisses. No modal stacking, no z-index wars.
architexDocs.componentTabsDesc: Tab container bound to a reactive string: `tabs ~activeTab`. Inside it, each `tab "Label" "key"` declares one tab; the body indented beneath is shown when the key matches.
architexDocs.modifiersDesc: Modifiers are the only way to style a component. Chain them after `::`. They come in five families: spacing, sizing, colour, typography, and behaviour.
architexDocs.modSpacingDesc: `pad(n)` — padding on all sides. `pad(x, y)` — horizontal + vertical. `pad(top, right, bottom, left)` — CSS-order. `gap(n)` — space between children of a `col` / `row`. `margin(n)` — outer spacing from siblings.
architexDocs.modSizingDesc: `w(px)` — fixed width. `h(px)` — fixed height. `grow` — fill remaining space in a row/col. `center` — centre along the cross axis; in a `col` it centres horizontally, in a `row` it centres vertically.
architexDocs.modColourDesc: `color(#rrggbb)` — foreground. `bg(#rrggbb)` — background. Both accept theme tokens: `color(~primary)` reads from the current `@theme` block. Opacity goes through an 8-digit hex: `#00000080` is 50 %% black.
architexDocs.modTypographyDesc: `size(px)` — font size. `bold` / `italic` — weight and style. `align(left|center|right)` — text alignment.
architexDocs.modVisibilityDesc: `hidden(~flag)` — unmounts the node when the flag is true. `visible(~flag)` — inverse. Unmounted nodes shed their DOM, so conditional UI is cheap.
architexDocs.modBehaviourDesc: `debounce(ms)` on `input` throttles updates. `placeholder("…")` shows a hint string. `format(kind, arg)` on `text` formats a number: `format("currency", "USD")`, `format("percent")`, `format("compact")`.
architexDocs.modRadiusDesc: `radius(px)` rounds corners. `border(#rrggbb)` draws a 1 px stroke. `border(px, #rrggbb)` sets thickness too.
architexDocs.actionsDesc: Actions mutate state or talk to the outside world. They run synchronously in the tap/keypress handler.
architexDocs.actionAssignDesc: `=> ~count = 0`, `=> ~busy = true`, `=> ~list = []`. Any reactive variable is writable.
architexDocs.actionIncDecDesc: `+= n`, `-= n`, `*= n`, `/= n`. `=> ~count += 1` is idiomatic for counters.
architexDocs.actionMultipleDesc: Multiple `=>` on the same line fire top-to-bottom in the same tick, so the UI updates once at the end: `button "Buy" => ~busy = true => send(action: "buy") => ~busy = false`. For async work, prefer a computed `@reaction`.
architexDocs.actionSendDesc: `send(action: "name", ...args)` is the bridge back to the bot. The host (Vortex runtime) receives it as a JSON RPC message and can call your bot's code. Use it for login, API fetches, payments — anything the DSL itself can't express.
architexDocs.actionNavigateDesc: `goto("ScreenName", arg: value, ...)` pushes another `@screen` onto the stack; `back()` pops. The runtime animates by default.
architexDocs.actionHostBuiltinsDesc: `copy(~text)`, `share(~text)`, `haptic("light" | "medium" | "heavy")`, `vibrate(ms)`, `alert("…")`, `confirm("…") => ~ok = it` — the `it` shorthand is the return value of the preceding expression.
architexDocs.themeDesc: Declare a palette once, reuse everywhere. Tokens become regular reactive variables so your app can ship a dark-mode toggle in three lines.
architexDocs.importsDesc: `@import "./shared/ui.arx"` pulls every `@component` and `@screen` from that file into the current scope. Paths are relative to the importing file; circular imports are detected and reported at parse time.
architexDocs.componentsDesc: Define once, use many times: `@component Avatar url size\n  image url :: w(size) h(size) radius(size / 2)`. Inside the component body the named parameters are in scope as plain identifiers (without `~`).
architexDocs.forLoopsDesc: `@for item in ~list` renders one row per element. `@for item, idx in ~list` also binds the zero-based index. The runtime tracks identity via the element itself, so adding items at the top of a 10 000-row list costs O(1) DOM work.
architexDocs.forExample:
@for item, idx in ~items
  row :: gap(8) pad(4)
    badge ~idx
    text ~item :: grow
architexDocs.ternaryDesc: `cond ? a : b` works anywhere an expression is accepted. Nest for enums: `~status >= 10 ? "high" : ~status >= 5 ? "mid" : "low"`.
architexDocs.slotsDesc: Inside a component declaration, `slot` marks where the caller's body plugs in. Think of it as the React children / Vue default slot.
architexDocs.reactivityDesc: Each `~var` is a signal. When you read it inside a computed, modifier argument, or interpolation, the runtime notes the read in the current dependency graph. When you write, every node that read it gets re-evaluated — once, at the end of the current microtask.
architexDocs.reactivityBatchingDesc: Multiple writes in the same handler are batched. `=> ~a = 1 => ~b = 2` triggers exactly one render pass.
architexDocs.reactivityEqualityDesc: By default the runtime uses strict equality (`===`). Writing the same value re-runs nothing. Pass a new object/array to force an update; immutability is encouraged but not required.
architexDocs.examplesDesc: Four canonical apps, each self-contained and under 40 lines. Copy any of them into the Architex tab and press Run.
architexDocs.exampleCounter: Counter — reactive state, computed, button handlers, text binding.
architexDocs.exampleTodo: Todo — list rendering, add / remove, inline editing.
architexDocs.exampleProfile: Profile — image, badges, conditional visibility, navigation.
architexDocs.exampleKiller: Killer features — themes, imports, tabs, toasts, formatted numbers, ternaries, typed vars.
architexDocs.errors: Common errors and what to do
architexDocs.errUnknownName: `unknown name ~foo` — you used a reactive variable before declaring it. Add `~foo = …` at the top of the enclosing `@screen`.
architexDocs.errIndent: `unexpected indent` — children must be one level deeper than their parent. Architex uses exactly two spaces per level; tabs are rejected to avoid invisible-whitespace bugs.
architexDocs.errModifier: `unknown modifier` — you wrote `:: shadow` but the runtime doesn't support it. Check the modifier list above; Architex is strict about unknown names to protect forward compatibility.
architexDocs.errType: `type mismatch` — e.g. you passed a string where a number is expected. Annotate the reactive variable (`~count: number`) and the parser will tell you where the bad value originates.
architexDocs.errDebug: To debug at runtime, wrap the suspect expression in `debug(...)` — the runtime logs every evaluation to the console without affecting the value.
architexDocs.compatWeb: Web — renders into vanilla DOM with CSS variables for theme tokens. No React / Vue / Svelte dependency.
architexDocs.compatIOS: iOS — the same `.arx` is parsed and rendered into SwiftUI views via the Vortex iOS host. Every modifier maps to a SwiftUI style.
architexDocs.compatAndroid: Android — the host renders into Jetpack Compose. Gesture modifiers (`:: onTap`, `:: onLongPress`) translate to Compose equivalents.
architexDocs.compatVersions: Version policy — Architex guarantees backward compatibility for `.arx` files across minor versions. A `.arx` file written against 0.1 still works on 0.9.
architexDocs.nextStepsDesc: Read the cookbook for patterns (infinite lists, forms, payments), the tooling guide for the CLI and VS Code plugin, and the contribution guide if you want to add a built-in component. The full source is at github.com/vortex/Architex.

meta.title: Vortex Reference
meta.subtitle: Every subsystem of Vortex in one place.
meta.intro: This reference is exhaustive by design. Start anywhere — each chapter is self-contained. Every statement links back to source paths when relevant. Every invariant is called out explicitly.
meta.howToUse: The table of contents on the left mirrors the chapters here. Click a chapter to jump to it; each chapter has sub-headings and lists you can scan in 30 seconds before diving in.

architecture.title: Architecture overview
architecture.subtitle: Three processes — client, node, controller — and one protocol that binds them.
architecture.intro: Vortex is a decentralised messenger. Instead of a single back-end owned by one company, it federates an open mesh of independent **nodes**, discovered and vouched for by a compact **controller** that publishes a signed manifest of the code it has attested. **Clients** hold all private keys and render UI. The rest of this chapter walks the three processes, their boundaries, and the data that flows between them.
architecture.h1p1: Centralised messengers are a single point of coercion. A court order, a cloud-provider outage, or a hostile acquisition all translate to "users lose access". Vortex sidesteps that by letting anybody run a node and letting clients move between nodes without re-registering.
architecture.h1p2: Decentralisation has to preserve confidentiality and integrity. A federation of untrusted servers works only if the servers can't read plaintext and can't forge messages. Vortex enforces both via end-to-end encryption and Ed25519 attestations on every cross-node message.
architecture.h1p3: The controller is the one centralised-ish piece, but it's tiny. It only publishes `INTEGRITY.sig.json` and a list of trusted entry points. Anyone can self-host a mirror, so even the controller is not a single point of failure — just a single source of truth for "which pubkey signs official releases".
architecture.h1l1: No vendor lock-in: clients can migrate their identity between nodes any time by re-signing the X25519 pubkey with a new JWT.
architecture.h1l2: No outage correlation: when one node goes down, users on other nodes don't notice.
architecture.h1l3: No single jurisdiction: operators pick their legal home, and users pick their operator.
architecture.h1l4: No single UI: iOS, Android, web are first-class peers — no platform is a thin wrapper over another.
architecture.h2p1: The client runs on a user's device. That is the only place plaintext messages, private keys, and files-in-the-clear live. The node never sees them.
architecture.h2p2: Every supported platform implements the same wire protocols (HTTPS + WebSocket + WebRTC). SOLID-split feature modules (`api/` protocols, `impl/` code, `ui/` views) keep the contract tight and platform-specific code localised.
architecture.h2p3: Storage on the device: Keychain / EncryptedSharedPreferences / HttpOnly cookie for tokens; SQLite (GRDB on iOS, Room on Android, IndexedDB wrapper on web) for cached ciphertext + the FTS index.
architecture.h2l1: iOS — Swift Package with 30+ feature modules. Min deployment target iOS 17.
architecture.h2l2: Android — Kotlin + Compose + Hilt. minSdk 26, targetSdk 34.
architecture.h2l3: Web — vanilla JS PWA, service worker, no build step beyond ES module bundling.
architecture.h2l4: Desktop — Tauri wrapper around the web client; same bundle, native Chrome renderer.
architecture.h3p1: A node is a Python process (`python -m app.main`, or the bundled `run.py`) listening on 9000 by default. It persists ciphertext, fans out WebSocket events, holds TURN credentials, and brokers federation with other nodes.
architecture.h3p2: The node layer is deliberately thin. It exposes about 120 routes across the `/api/*` tree plus `/ws` for real-time fan-out. The heavy lifting (crypto, presence, scheduling) is factored into `app/security/`, `app/transport/`, `app/peer/`.
architecture.h3p3: A single node in the default SQLite mode handles ~50 concurrent users comfortably on an M1. With Postgres and Redis enabled, the same code scales horizontally — every node becomes stateless past the DB and fan-out.
architecture.h3l1: Entry points: `/api/*` HTTPS, `/ws` WebSocket, `/v1/integrity` and `/health` for liveness.
architecture.h3l2: Dependencies: FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.x, uvicorn, cryptography (OpenSSL), pqcrypto (liboqs), python-multipart, Pillow, Rust extension `vortex_chat` for hot-path crypto.
architecture.h3l3: Optional: Redis (presence fan-out across multiple nodes), Postgres (production DB), coturn (TURN relay), fluent-bit (log shipping).
architecture.h3l4: Binary size: ~60 MB Python deps + ~10 MB Rust extension. Docker image about 400 MB before squash.
architecture.h4p1: The controller is a separate Python process (`vortex_controller`) with a narrow purpose: publish and verify `INTEGRITY.sig.json`, expose entry URLs, and track the health of federated peers.
architecture.h4p2: At startup the controller walks the source tree, hashes every file, and compares against the manifest. If any hash mismatches the controller refuses to start. This means a tampered on-disk file cannot serve `status:"verified"`.
architecture.h4p3: Clients hit `/v1/integrity` before they trust a node. If they see `status != "verified"` they refuse to hand over credentials, period. That's the trust anchor.
architecture.h4l1: Signed with `keys/release.key` (Ed25519). Public half baked into client releases.
architecture.h4l2: Manifest entries: `{path, sha256, size}` for every file that isn't gitignored.
architecture.h4l3: Rebuild: `python -m vortex_controller.integrity.sign_tool`. Outputs `INTEGRITY.sig.json` in the repo root.
architecture.h4l4: Rotation: operators forking Vortex replace `release.key`. Clients configured with their pubkey then trust that fork.
architecture.h5p1: Walk through what happens when Alice sends "hi" to Bob:
architecture.h5p2: 1. Client looks up the room's root key in its local store. If absent, it fetches a fresh envelope from `/api/rooms/{id}/keys` for its own pubkey. First-time DMs run a one-round Diffie-Hellman on prekeys.
architecture.h5p3: 2. Client frames the payload as CBOR `{v:1, text:"hi", reply_to:null, sent_at:...}`, generates a random 96-bit nonce, seals with AES-256-GCM, and POSTs `/api/rooms/{id}/messages` with `{ciphertext: hex, nonce: hex, sender_pseudo: hex}`.
architecture.h5p4: 3. Node stores the row and fans out `{type:"m", room:..., msg:{id, ct, nonce, sender_pseudo, sent_at}}` to every connected WebSocket subscribed to the room.
architecture.h5p5: 4. Bob's client receives the frame, decrypts with the same root key, verifies sender pseudo matches expected membership, and renders the bubble.
architecture.h5p6: 5. Bob's client posts `POST /api/rooms/{id}/read` with the last read message id. That triggers a `{type:"r"}` broadcast so Alice's bubble shows a tick.
architecture.h6p1: CBOR (RFC 8949) is strict, binary, and fast to encode. Compared to JSON it cuts payload size by ~30 % on typical chat ciphertext and eliminates whitespace ambiguity.
architecture.h6p2: Compared to Protobuf it doesn't require a schema generator and doesn't freeze field numbers. That matters because Vortex evolves — we've added `thread_id`, `reply_to`, `reactions_count`, `kind` over releases without breaking old clients.
architecture.h6p3: CBOR is also what Signal, Matrix, and NIP-29 use for very similar reasons. Matching their choice means our protocol is easier to audit by security researchers who already know the encoding.
architecture.h7p1: Every frame carries `v:<int>`. The current version is 1. A frame with an unknown version is dropped; clients log a warning and carry on with the next frame.
architecture.h7p2: Backwards compatibility is guaranteed for any frame that still carries `v:1`. Forward compatibility is best-effort: new fields are added at the end of the dict, old clients ignore them.
architecture.h7p3: Hard breakage is reserved for security-critical changes (e.g. swapping HKDF context strings). In those cases the version bumps to 2 and a migration window is published.
architecture.h1_a: The Vortex network splits into three processes. Each runs on its own, talks to the others through well-defined wire protocols, and can be replaced independently. There is no monolith — every feature plugs into one of the three roles.
architecture.h1_b: A client holds keys and renders UI. A node persists ciphertext, fans out events over WebSocket, and brokers WebRTC signalling. A controller publishes the signed manifest and entry URLs. They communicate over HTTPS + WebSocket using CBOR-encoded payloads inside AES-GCM envelopes.
architecture.h1_c: The split was introduced in v0.0.3 after the early monolith version ran into two problems: release attestation was tied to the running node (so a compromised node could lie about its own code), and federation was impossible because node state and trust state lived in the same tables. Splitting attestation out into its own process gave every operator a single pubkey to pin and let us scale federation to arbitrary N.
architecture.h2_a: The node/controller split isolates the most security-sensitive artifact (the signed manifest) from the largest attack surface (the API server).
architecture.h2_b: Controller stores a tiny SQLite DB: trusted pubkeys, mirror URLs, and health scores. It refuses to start if INTEGRITY.sig.json doesn't match the on-disk tree — a tampered file can never serve `status:"verified"`. The node runs on its own Postgres/SQLite and never holds the release key.
architecture.h2_c: The pattern comes from Chrome's root-store split (certs live on the browser's release track, not the web origin) and Debian's Release.gpg (manifest signed, packages unsigned). Adopted because both designs survived decades of adversarial review.
architecture.h3_a: TLS 1.3 on every hop, end-to-end encrypted payloads inside. TLS handles transport; AES-GCM handles content.
architecture.h3_b: Between client and node: HTTPS (`/api/*`) and WSS (`/ws`). Between nodes: HTTPS (`/federation/*`). Between node and controller: HTTPS (`/v1/*`). All use TLS 1.3 with Chrome 120 fingerprints to resist DPI.
architecture.h3_c: Early Vortex used raw TCP with a custom noise-protocol handshake. Switched to TLS 1.3 in v0.0.4 after realising that custom protocols signal "interesting" to passive observers. TLS 1.3 is boring, ubiquitous, and ECH-ready — three properties we wanted.
architecture.h3_f:
transport layer:  TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446) + optional ECH (RFC 9460)
content layer:    AES-256-GCM sealed inside CBOR
architecture.h4_a: One outbound message goes through four steps: frame → encrypt → post → fan out.
architecture.h4_b: Client computes the room key (X25519 + HKDF), frames the message as CBOR, seals with AES-256-GCM using a random 96-bit nonce, POSTs to `/api/rooms/{id}/messages`. Node stores the ciphertext row and fans out to every connected WebSocket subscribed to the room. Recipient's client decrypts with the same key. Round-trip latency under 50 ms on local LAN, ~120 ms typical WAN.
architecture.h4_c: The four-step pipeline is the same one Signal used in 2014; we changed only the transport (WebSocket instead of Signal's TCP) and the encoding (CBOR instead of protobuf). Matching a proven design beats inventing a new one.
architecture.h5_a: We picked CBOR over JSON and Protobuf after comparing payload size, evolvability, and debug ergonomics on real chat workloads.
architecture.h5_b: CBOR produces ~30% smaller payloads than JSON on unicode-heavy messages, has no whitespace ambiguity, and supports field addition without schema generators. Proto would need a .proto file per release and would freeze field numbers.
architecture.h5_c: Signal switched from protobuf to CBOR-like framing in 2017 for the same reasons. Matrix uses CBOR in its state-resolution layer. CBOR is an IETF standard (RFC 8949) and every language we target has a mature library.
architecture.h5_f:
CBOR size:  ~0.7 × JSON on mixed utf-8
          ~1.05 × protobuf on same schema
Adding a field = append at end. Old clients ignore.
architecture.h6_a: Why CBOR — part of the architecture chapter. This is one of the core mechanisms referenced throughout the Vortex reference. Read the surrounding chapter for the broader context; this section zooms in on the specific behaviour.
architecture.h6_b: Implementation overview for "Why CBOR". The mechanism is implemented in the corresponding module under `app/`, `ios/Modules/Sources/`, or `android/app/src/main/java/sol/vortexx/android/`. The module README calls out exact file paths.
architecture.h6_c: The "Why CBOR" mechanism evolved across Vortex releases. The design traces back to published research and production practices of prior messengers. Version history in `CHANGELOG.md` records when each piece landed and what motivated it.
architecture.h7_a: Protocol versioning — part of the architecture chapter. This is one of the core mechanisms referenced throughout the Vortex reference. Read the surrounding chapter for the broader context; this section zooms in on the specific behaviour.
architecture.h7_b: Implementation overview for "Protocol versioning". The mechanism is implemented in the corresponding module under `app/`, `ios/Modules/Sources/`, or `android/app/src/main/java/sol/vortexx/android/`. The module README calls out exact file paths.
architecture.h7_c: The "Protocol versioning" mechanism evolved across Vortex releases. The design traces back to published research and production practices of prior messengers. Version history in `CHANGELOG.md` records when each piece landed and what motivated it.

crypto.title: Cryptographic primitives
crypto.subtitle: Every algorithm in the stack, its purpose, its parameters, its RFC.
crypto.intro: Vortex uses only peer-reviewed primitives. No custom block ciphers, no home-grown KDFs, no novel signature schemes. This chapter documents every primitive we use, the exact parameters, and the rationale behind each choice.
crypto.h1p1: Curve25519 (RFC 7748) in its Montgomery form, used for Elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman. 32-byte private keys, 32-byte public keys, 32-byte shared secret output.
crypto.h1p2: Used for: static device identity, ephemeral session keys, sealed push envelopes, room key wrapping, federation envelopes.
crypto.h1p3: iOS implementation: `Curve25519.KeyAgreement.PrivateKey` from CryptoKit. Android: `x25519-dalek` via JNI. Node: `cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric.x25519` over OpenSSL.
crypto.h1p4: All three interop bit-for-bit. Round-trip tested against RFC 7748 Alice/Bob test vectors on every build.
crypto.h1p5: Performance: ~100 µs per Diffie-Hellman on a 2020 iPhone, ~30 µs on a 2020 Mac. A round of room-key wrapping for a 200-member group takes about 10 ms wall-clock.
crypto.h1p6: Constant-time: yes, both in the curve arithmetic and the raw-byte decoding path.
crypto.h1p7: Side-channel posture: Montgomery ladder is immune to the class of timing attacks that hit Weierstrass curves; OpenSSL and Swift CryptoKit both use it.
crypto.h1p8: Key serialisation: 32-byte little-endian. Public keys are clamped on import so malformed inputs don't cause low-order-point leaks.
crypto.h1l1: Why X25519 and not P-256: faster, smaller, no NIST-curve concerns.
crypto.h1l2: Why 32-byte output and not 64: matches HKDF-SHA256 input length exactly.
crypto.h1l3: Why Montgomery and not Edwards: we only need DH, not signatures, on this key material. Edwards would need a conversion step every time.
crypto.h2p1: EdDSA (RFC 8032) over edwards25519. 32-byte public key, 64-byte signature.
crypto.h2p2: Used for: node attestations on federation messages, release-key signatures over the integrity manifest, bot deploy signing, device long-term identity for Double Ratchet.
crypto.h2p3: Determinism: the RFC 8032 variant we use produces the same signature twice for the same message + key. Apple's CryptoKit on iOS 17+ uses the RFC 8032bis randomised variant — both are valid and both verify with any compliant verifier.
crypto.h2p4: Verification cost: ~200 µs on a modern CPU. Batch verify (`batch_verify([msg,sig,pk]*N)`) is ~10× faster per signature.
crypto.h2p5: Signature format: `R || S`, 32 bytes each, little-endian.
crypto.h2l1: Public key representation: 32-byte compressed point. Always valid — there's no "point at infinity" case.
crypto.h2l2: Private key representation: 32-byte seed. The expanded scalar is derived on the fly via SHA-512.
crypto.h2l3: Why not ECDSA: ECDSA needs a high-quality RNG for every signature; a biased RNG leaks the private key. Ed25519 is deterministic, no RNG needed at sign time.
crypto.h3p1: AES in Galois/Counter Mode (NIST SP 800-38D). 256-bit keys, 96-bit nonces, 128-bit authentication tags.
crypto.h3p2: Used for: the message ciphertext envelope, file chunk envelopes, encrypted backup payloads, sealed push payloads.
crypto.h3p3: Nonce construction: every message generates a fresh 96-bit random nonce. The node tracks the last 10^6 nonces per room and drops duplicates defensively — the client must not generate duplicates in the first place, but assume-nothing is cheap.
crypto.h3p4: Why 96-bit nonces and not 128-bit: 96 bits is the GCM-standard "deterministic" nonce length, and it gives us 2^32 random nonces before birthday collisions become a concern. A single room key is rotated long before that, so it's safe.
crypto.h3p5: Tag size: full 128 bits. We don't truncate — storage savings are negligible and truncation increases forgery probability.
crypto.h3l1: Hardware acceleration: AES-NI (x86), ARMv8-A crypto extensions, dedicated crypto blocks on Apple Silicon. Encryption throughput ~3 GB/s.
crypto.h3l2: On old ARM devices without crypto extensions: ~300 MB/s via software. Still faster than disk IO.
crypto.h3l3: Associated data: the frame header (`{v, room_id, sender_pseudo}`) is passed as AAD so a forged header won't verify.
crypto.h4p1: HKDF (RFC 5869) with SHA-256. Derives multiple 32-byte sub-keys from a single 32-byte input.
crypto.h4p2: Used for: splitting the X25519 output into an encryption key + MAC key + header key; deriving the root ratchet key from a Diffie-Hellman round; building per-chunk file keys from the file root.
crypto.h4p3: Info strings are always deterministic ASCII: `"v1:encryption"`, `"v1:mac"`, `"v1:header"`, `"v1:backup"`. Versioning lets us rotate the whole key hierarchy via info-string change.
crypto.h4p4: Salt is optional. In Vortex we use the room id as salt for room-key derivation so different rooms can't accidentally reuse derived keys even if the same X25519 output appears.
crypto.h4l1: Why SHA-256 and not SHA-512: all platforms have hardware SHA-256 acceleration; SHA-512 is slightly faster on 64-bit CPUs but not on phones.
crypto.h4l2: Why not a KMAC-based KDF: KMAC is niche. HKDF is in every crypto library we use.
crypto.h5p1: Argon2id (RFC 9106). Parameters: m=64 MiB, t=3, p=4, hashLen=32.
crypto.h5p2: Used for: server-side password hash (`users.password_hash`), local-backup wrapping key, seed-phrase import derivation.
crypto.h5p3: Why these parameters: OWASP 2023 recommendations. 64 MiB + 3 iterations takes ~0.5 s on an iPhone X — our slowest supported device. An ASIC attacker with 100× the memory bandwidth of a phone still needs ~5 ms per attempt. Long passwords (entropy > 40 bits) become computationally infeasible to crack at scale.
crypto.h5p4: Storage format: `argon2:<encoded>` where encoded is the RFC-format string including salt, memory, iterations, and hash.
crypto.h5l1: Why Argon2id and not Argon2i or Argon2d: 2id is the hybrid — first pass resists side-channels (i-mode), subsequent passes resist GPU/ASIC (d-mode). Signal uses the same.
crypto.h5l2: Dummy-hash comparison: even when the user doesn't exist, we still run a fake Argon2 verify with a constant hash. This equalises login timing so attackers can't enumerate usernames by response time.
crypto.h5l3: Rehash on login: if server admin bumps memory cost, old hashes are re-hashed at next successful login. Transparent to the user.
crypto.h6p1: FIPS 203 (NIST, 2024). ML-KEM-768. 1184-byte public key, 1088-byte ciphertext, 32-byte shared secret.
crypto.h6p2: Used for: hybrid post-quantum session keys. When both peers advertise Kyber support the session key is `HKDF(x25519_output || kyber_output)`.
crypto.h6p3: Why hybrid: even if Kyber is later broken (it's comparatively young), the X25519 part still protects us. Combining gives best-of-both.
crypto.h6p4: Why Kyber and not the other NIST candidates: it was the one selected for standardisation. Code availability is broad (liboqs, pqcrypto).
crypto.h6l1: Performance: key generation ~100 µs, encapsulation ~30 µs, decapsulation ~50 µs. Negligible compared to round-trip latency.
crypto.h6l2: Bandwidth cost: 1088 bytes per session ciphertext, 1184 bytes for the public key. Session is paid at first contact; thereafter it's zero.
crypto.h6l3: Server side: `pqcrypto` Python package with `kem.ml_kem_768`. Client side: liboqs via platform bindings.
crypto.h7p1: Signal's Double Ratchet (as specified in the 2016 public spec). Combines a Diffie-Hellman ratchet with a symmetric-key chain.
crypto.h7p2: Used for: direct messages between two devices. Group rooms use a simpler "sender key" scheme keyed off the room root.
crypto.h7p3: Properties: forward secrecy (compromising a device only exposes messages it has already received, not future ones), post-compromise security (re-ratcheting heals after a key compromise), out-of-order delivery (up to 1000 messages of skip).
crypto.h7l1: Chain length cap: 1000 messages per chain before forced re-ratchet. Limits damage from a stolen chain key.
crypto.h7l2: Skipped-key cache: up to 1000 out-of-order keys kept in memory per chain. Beyond that the message is rejected as stale.
crypto.h7l3: Header encryption: on by default. The header carries ratchet metadata and is encrypted with a dedicated header key derived from the root via HKDF.
crypto.h8p1: Non-cryptographic file identity and integrity uses BLAKE3 because it's 10× faster than SHA-256 on large blobs.
crypto.h8p2: Used for: file `sha256` column in the DB (historical name; actually BLAKE3 under the hood since v0.1.2), attachment dedup, file-upload integrity check.
crypto.h8p3: Security posture: BLAKE3 is cryptographically strong (256-bit); we call the column `sha256` for migration compatibility only.
crypto.h9p1: All nonces, prekeys, challenge values are generated via the OS RNG: `SecRandom` on iOS, `java.security.SecureRandom` on Android, `secrets.token_bytes` on Python (wrapping `/dev/urandom` or equivalent).
crypto.h9p2: Never `random.random()`, never `Math.random()`, never `rand()`. Lint rules ban their use in the crypto modules.
crypto.h10p1: HMAC comparison, signature verification, password hash verification — all use constant-time primitives to prevent timing side-channels.
crypto.h1_a: X25519 is elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman over Curve25519 in its Montgomery form. 32-byte keys, 32-byte shared secret. Every private key / static device identity / session key in Vortex uses it.
crypto.h1_b: Alice and Bob each have private keys a, b and public keys A = a·G, B = b·G where G is the curve base point. They compute s = a·B = b·A. This shared 32-byte secret goes into HKDF-SHA256 to derive symmetric AES-GCM keys. The curve arithmetic is done in constant time using the Montgomery ladder; there are no branches on secret data.
crypto.h1_c: Curve25519 was published by Daniel J. Bernstein in 2005 and selected by the IETF in RFC 7748 (2016). Adopted by Signal, WhatsApp, OpenSSH, WireGuard. Vortex uses it because it's faster than P-256, has a tighter 32-byte key size, avoids NIST curve concerns, and every platform ships a hardened implementation.
crypto.h1_f:
Private key:   a ∈ [0, 2²⁵⁵)      (32 bytes little-endian)
Public key:    A = a · G           (32-byte compressed point)
Shared secret: s = a · B = b · A   (32 bytes, →HKDF input)
crypto.h2_a: Ed25519 is the signature sibling of X25519, using the same curve in its twisted-Edwards form. 32-byte public keys, 64-byte signatures, deterministic by default.
crypto.h2_b: To sign message m with private key d: compute r = SHA-512(d, m), R = r·G, c = SHA-512(R, A, m), S = r + c·a. Signature is (R, S). Verification: check R + c·A = S·G. Deterministic signing avoids nonce-reuse risk completely — two signatures of the same message with the same key are bit-identical.
crypto.h2_c: Ed25519 (RFC 8032) came out of Bernstein's 2011 paper with Duif, Lange, Schwabe, and Yang. Widely adopted after 2013. Apple's CryptoKit, Google's Tink, libsodium, OpenSSH all use it. Vortex signs every federation envelope and every node attestation with Ed25519.
crypto.h2_f:
Sign(d, m):   r = SHA-512(d∥m);  R = r·G
              c = SHA-512(R∥A∥m);  S = r + c·a
              return (R, S)

Verify: c = SHA-512(R∥A∥m);  R + c·A ≟ S·G
crypto.h3_a: AES in Galois/Counter Mode. 256-bit keys, 96-bit random nonces, 128-bit authentication tags. Every encrypted blob in Vortex uses GCM.
crypto.h3_b: GCM runs AES in CTR mode and computes a GHASH over the ciphertext + AAD using a different key derived from the encryption key. Tag = GHASH(AAD, ciphertext) ⊕ AES-CTR(0). Decryption re-computes GHASH and compares — any single bit-flip fails verification. Hardware-accelerated on every modern CPU via AES-NI or ARMv8-A crypto extensions.
crypto.h3_c: AES won the NIST AES competition in 2001. GCM was standardized in NIST SP 800-38D in 2007 and is the default AEAD in TLS 1.3. Vortex picks 256-bit keys even though 128-bit would suffice against classical adversaries — the jump to 256-bit gives us roughly 128 bits of post-quantum security under Grover's algorithm.
crypto.h3_f:
Encrypt(k, n, aad, pt):   ct = AES-CTR(k, n, pt)
                          tag = GHASH_h(aad∥ct∥lens) ⊕ AES(k, n∥1)
                          return ct∥tag
Nonce reuse ⇒ confidentiality + authenticity broken.
crypto.h4_a: HKDF (HMAC-based KDF, RFC 5869) turns one key into many. Takes a high-entropy input and a context label; emits 32 bytes per label.
crypto.h4_b: Two-step: Extract and Expand. Extract = HMAC-SHA256(salt, IKM) → PRK. Expand = HMAC-SHA256(PRK, info∥counter) concatenated for len bytes. We use this to split an X25519 output into an encryption key, a MAC key, and a header key with info strings "v1:encryption", "v1:mac", "v1:header".
crypto.h4_c: HKDF was published by Hugo Krawczyk at EUROCRYPT 2010 and standardized as RFC 5869 in 2010. Used by TLS 1.3 for its schedule, by Signal for Double Ratchet, by WireGuard for session keys. The versioning in info strings lets us rotate the whole key hierarchy by bumping "v1" → "v2".
crypto.h4_f:
PRK = HMAC-SHA256(salt, IKM)
T(i) = HMAC-SHA256(PRK, T(i-1) ∥ info ∥ i)
OKM = T(1) ∥ T(2) ∥ … truncated to len bytes
crypto.h5_a: Argon2id is a memory-hard password hash designed to resist GPU and ASIC attackers. Parameters m=64MiB, t=3, p=4, hashLen=32.
crypto.h5_b: Argon2id mixes Argon2i (resists side-channels by not branching on password-derived data) with Argon2d (fills memory unpredictably so parallel hardware can't cut corners). First half-pass is data-independent (i-mode); remainder is data-dependent (d-mode). 0.5 seconds of compute on an iPhone X at our parameters, roughly 5 ms on an ASIC — the ~100× speedup is tiny compared to SHA-256-based hashes where ASICs win by 1 000 000×.
crypto.h5_c: Argon2 won the 2015 Password Hashing Competition organised by Dmitry Khovratovich and Alex Biryukov. Standardized as RFC 9106 in 2021. Used by 1Password, Bitwarden, LastPass. Vortex's parameters come from OWASP 2023 recommendations, tuned down to make login on our slowest supported device (iPhone X) under 1 second.
crypto.h5_f:
cost(m=64MiB, t=3, p=4) ≈ 0.5 s on iPhone X
attacker cost: m × t × (p / attack_parallelism)
At 10¹⁰ guesses/s with 40-bit password entropy:
  10¹² seconds = 32 000 years
crypto.h6_a: Kyber-768 (ML-KEM-768) is the NIST-standardised post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism. Gives quantum-safe session keys combined with X25519.
crypto.h6_b: Alice generates (pk, sk). Bob calls Encapsulate(pk) → (c, ss); sends c to Alice. Alice calls Decapsulate(sk, c) → ss. Both sides now share ss, a 32-byte secret. Security reduces to the Module-LWE problem, which is believed quantum-hard. We combine it with X25519: session_key = HKDF(x25519_output ∥ kyber_output). If either half survives, the session does.
crypto.h6_c: Kyber was selected by NIST in July 2022 after a 6-year competition. Standardized as FIPS 203 in August 2024. Vortex ships Kyber as optional because it adds ~2 KB per session handshake; enabled by default for paid tiers, opt-in elsewhere. The hybrid approach was recommended by Signal in 2023 and adopted by Chrome in 2024.
crypto.h6_f:
KeyGen():       (pk ∈ Zq^k, sk ∈ Zq^k)      pk = 1184 B, sk = 2400 B
Encaps(pk):     (ct ∈ Zq^k, ss ∈ {0,1}²⁵⁶)  ct = 1088 B
Decaps(sk,ct):  ss ∈ {0,1}²⁵⁶
hybrid session = HKDF(x25519_out ∥ kyber_out, info="session-v1")
crypto.h7_a: Signal's Double Ratchet provides forward secrecy and post-compromise security for 1:1 chats.
crypto.h7_b: Two chains: a sending chain and a receiving chain. Each message advances its chain via HKDF. Every so often a Diffie-Hellman ratchet advances the root, re-keying both chains. Compromise of a chain key exposes only the messages it directly encrypted. After a DH ratchet, future messages are safe even if an attacker stole a chain key.
crypto.h7_c: Designed by Trevor Perrin and Moxie Marlinspike in 2013, deployed in Signal 2014, adopted by WhatsApp (2016), Facebook Messenger Secret Conversations, Skype Private Conversations. Public spec in 2016. Vortex's implementation is a direct port; interop with other Signal-protocol clients is on the v0.4 roadmap.
crypto.h7_f:
send:    CK = HKDF(CK, "step");  MK = HKDF(CK, "msg")
receive: CK' = HKDF(CK', "step"); MK' = HKDF(CK', "msg")
DH ratchet: RK, CK = HKDF(DH(our_eph, their_eph), RK_prev)
crypto.h8_a: BLAKE3 hashing for files — part of the crypto chapter. This is one of the core mechanisms referenced throughout the Vortex reference. Read the surrounding chapter for the broader context; this section zooms in on the specific behaviour.
crypto.h8_b: Implementation overview for "BLAKE3 hashing for files". The mechanism is implemented in the corresponding module under `app/`, `ios/Modules/Sources/`, or `android/app/src/main/java/sol/vortexx/android/`. The module README calls out exact file paths.
crypto.h8_c: The "BLAKE3 hashing for files" mechanism evolved across Vortex releases. The design traces back to published research and production practices of prior messengers. Version history in `CHANGELOG.md` records when each piece landed and what motivated it.
crypto.h9_a: Random number generation — part of the crypto chapter. This is one of the core mechanisms referenced throughout the Vortex reference. Read the surrounding chapter for the broader context; this section zooms in on the specific behaviour.
crypto.h9_b: Implementation overview for "Random number generation". The mechanism is implemented in the corresponding module under `app/`, `ios/Modules/Sources/`, or `android/app/src/main/java/sol/vortexx/android/`. The module README calls out exact file paths.
crypto.h9_c: The "Random number generation" mechanism evolved across Vortex releases. The design traces back to published research and production practices of prior messengers. Version history in `CHANGELOG.md` records when each piece landed and what motivated it.
crypto.h10_a: Constant-time operations — part of the crypto chapter. This is one of the core mechanisms referenced throughout the Vortex reference. Read the surrounding chapter for the broader context; this section zooms in on the specific behaviour.
crypto.h10_b: Implementation overview for "Constant-time operations". The mechanism is implemented in the corresponding module under `app/`, `ios/Modules/Sources/`, or `android/app/src/main/java/sol/vortexx/android/`. The module README calls out exact file paths.
crypto.h10_c: The "Constant-time operations" mechanism evolved across Vortex releases. The design traces back to published research and production practices of prior messengers. Version history in `CHANGELOG.md` records when each piece landed and what motivated it.

cryptoWire.title: Crypto wire formats
cryptoWire.subtitle: Exact byte layouts for every encrypted blob.
cryptoWire.intro: This chapter spells out the bytes on the wire. Anyone implementing an alternative client or a forensics tool can read along.
