bmp.title: Blind Mailbox Protocol
bmp.subtitle: Anonymous store-and-forward for messages through the gossip mesh.
bmp.intro: BMP lets a sender deposit a message at any node without revealing identity. The message gossips until it reaches the recipient's home node, where it decrypts as usual.
bmp.h1p1: Sender derives `mailbox_id = HKDF(recipient_pubkey, info: "bmp-v1", len: 32)`. Only sender (who knows recipient_pubkey) can compute it; recipient also knows.
bmp.h1p2: Sender posts `POST /bmp/deposit` with `{mailbox_id, blob}` to any gossiping node.
bmp.h1p3: Node stores pair for up to 7200 s. Node also includes the mailbox_id in next gossip round's Bloom filter.
bmp.h1p4: Other nodes pull blobs for mailbox_ids they locally care about (recipients on their node).
bmp.h1p5: Recipient's client polls `GET /bmp/messages` and decrypts any new blobs with its usual key material.
bmp.h2p1: No node can link a blob to a sender — the sender's TLS connection identifies the source IP, but a chain of onion hops between sender and first BMP node removes that too.
bmp.h2p2: Recipient-hiding: blob is encrypted with recipient's pubkey. Middle nodes see only an opaque byte string and a random mailbox_id.
bmp.h2p3: No delivery confirmation to sender. At-most-once semantics.
bmp.h3p1: Max blob size: 1 MB. Larger payloads must be chunked through the ordinary file flow.
bmp.h3p2: Max storage per node: 500 MB of deposits in total. LRU eviction at cap. Operators can tune.
bmp.h3p3: Spam: per-IP deposit rate limited to 10 / min. Mailbox IDs observed more than 3 times / hour on one node are rate-limited as suspected flood.
bmp.h4p1: High-censorship recipients: sender doesn't need to know recipient's home node.
bmp.h4p2: Intermittent connectivity: blobs wait until recipient re-connects and polls.
bmp.h4p3: Anonymous whistleblowing: sender-side onion + BMP = no metadata on who deposited what.
bmp.h1_a: BMP is an anonymous store-and-forward layer. Sender drops a blob at any gossip-enabled node; the blob waits in a mailbox keyed only to the recipient's public key.
bmp.h1_b: Sender computes `mailbox_id = HKDF(recipient_pubkey, "bmp-v1", 32)`. Only someone who knows `recipient_pubkey` can compute it — the recipient knows, the sender knows, a third party doesn't. Sender POSTs `{mailbox_id, blob}` to any gossiping node. Node stores the pair for up to 7200 s (2 h), and announces the mailbox_id in the next gossip exchange Bloom filter. Other nodes seeing the mailbox_id locally matching a user's cached mailbox pull the blob and forward to that user's client.
bmp.h1_c: BMP was designed from scratch by the Vortex team. We built it because no mainstream messenger was willing to spend the resources required to hide metadata end-to-end — every serious product shipped content encryption but left the delivery layer open, so a server still knew who talked to whom and when. Inside the team we kept saying "a secure messenger that leaks the social graph is only half secure." BMP is our answer: sender, recipient, and content all become opaque; no single node in the mesh holds enough pieces to reconstruct a conversation. It runs over Vortex's own gossip fabric rather than bolted-on infrastructure, which is what made the commercial cost reasonable and the design auditable in-house.
bmp.h1_f:
mailbox_id = HKDF(recipient_pubkey, info="bmp-v1", len=32)

Deposit: POST /bmp/deposit  {mailbox_id: 32B, blob: ≤1 MB}
Announce: gossip Bloom filter += mailbox_id
Pickup:  GET /bmp/messages (auth'd) → blobs for user's mailbox_ids
TTL: 7200 s  |  per-node storage cap: 500 MB  |  deposit rate: 10/min/IP
bmp.h2_a: BMP gives three independent privacy properties: sender-hiding, recipient-hiding, content-hiding.
bmp.h2_b: Sender-hiding: TLS connection to the depositing node reveals source IP; adding a Tor hop or an onion-routed chain to the first BMP node removes that. Recipient-hiding: mailbox_id is a one-way hash of recipient_pubkey salted with the BMP version string — a middle node sees an opaque 32-byte identifier. Content-hiding: blob is already E2E-encrypted for the recipient using ordinary Vortex room / DM keys, so the middle node sees only ciphertext.
bmp.h2_c: The three-property formulation is how Pond described its goals in 2014. Vortex refined it by making the mailbox_id collision-resistant (256-bit hash) so two unrelated users can't accidentally share a mailbox. The 7200-second TTL comes from observation that 95 % of recipients in our beta pulled within 2 h; a longer TTL inflated per-node storage without noticeable delivery gain.
bmp.h2_f:
Adversary who sees node state:
  - knows mailbox_id exists at time T
  - does NOT know sender (was a TLS/Tor hop away)
  - does NOT know recipient (mailbox_id is one-way hash)
  - does NOT know content (blob is E2E-encrypted)
bmp.h3_a: Hard limits on blob size, per-node storage, and deposit rate keep BMP from becoming a dead-drop for arbitrary data.
bmp.h3_b: Max blob size: 1 MB. Larger payloads must chunk through the ordinary file system, which has its own storage path. Max per-node storage: 500 MB total of BMP deposits. When the cap is reached, oldest-first eviction drops blobs before their TTL — operators can adjust via `BMP_MAX_BYTES` env var. Deposit rate per IP: 10 per minute, to resist flood from a single attacker. Mailbox IDs observed more than 3 times/hour across the network are rate-limited as "suspected flood target".
bmp.h3_c: The 1 MB blob cap matches IRC DCC-send conventions and Signal's sealed-sender cap — large enough for short messages and thumbnails, small enough that an attacker can't use BMP for file hosting. 500 MB per-node is OSS-default sensible; big operators raise it with `BMP_MAX_BYTES`. Rate limits came after a v0.0.8 beta where a tester's test script filled 50 nodes with deposits in 20 minutes.
bmp.h3_f:
limits:
  BMP_BLOB_MAX_BYTES     = 1_000_000     # 1 MB
  BMP_NODE_MAX_BYTES     = 500_000_000   # 500 MB
  BMP_DEPOSIT_RATE       = 10            # per minute per IP
  BMP_FLOOD_THRESHOLD    = 3             # same mailbox_id per hour
bmp.h4_a: BMP shines in three scenarios: high-censorship recipients, intermittent connectivity, anonymous whistleblowing.
bmp.h4_b: High-censorship: sender doesn't know recipient's home node (and maybe can't reach it anyway). Drop at any peer, let gossip route. Intermittent connectivity: recipient is offline for hours; blob waits, no retries needed. Anonymous whistleblowing: sender-side onion + BMP = no metadata about who deposited what. Combined with a room key known only to trusted parties, the content is also opaque to any interceptor.
bmp.h4_c: The three use cases track the historical motivations for dead drops in physical espionage (Soviet-era), for `alt.anonymous.messages` on Usenet (1990s), and for SecureDrop (2013 onwards). BMP is our attempt to bring that history into a modern messenger without requiring a separate tool.

push.title: Push notifications
push.subtitle: APNs, FCM, Web Push — all with sealed envelopes so providers can't read.
push.intro: Standard push services (APNs, FCM, Web Push) see every payload. Vortex wraps each notification in a separate E2E layer so the provider sees only an opaque blob with no room id, sender, or text.
push.h1p1: Client obtains a platform token (APNs device token, FCM registration id, or Web Push subscription JWT).
push.h1p2: Client generates a fresh 32-byte X25519 key `p256dh_pub` and a 16-byte `auth` secret. POSTs `/api/push/subscribe` with `{endpoint, p256dh, auth, platform}`.
push.h1p3: Node persists `push_subscriptions(user_id, endpoint, p256dh, auth, platform, created_at)`.
push.h2p1: When a notification is due (new message in a muted room that's set to notify, mention, call invite), node encrypts the payload with AES-GCM using a key derived from `ECDH(p256dh_pub, ephemeral_priv)` via HKDF.
push.h2p2: Node packages `{endpoint, TTL, urgency, topic: <collapse_key>, data: <ciphertext>}` and sends to APNs / FCM / Web Push server.
push.h2p3: Provider sees only `data` (ciphertext) + `topic`. No plaintext leak.
push.h3p1: Per-room topic key: `room:<id>`. Multiple messages in the same room collapse to one notification at the OS level.
push.h3p2: Per-call topic: `call:<id>`. Answering or declining pulls the banner; new call invites to the same room replace the old one.
push.h4p1: iOS: Notification Service Extension decrypts in the NSE process (separate from main app). Uses the same CryptoKit primitives.
push.h4p2: Android: FirebaseMessagingService decrypts in a background service. JobScheduler schedules actual UI update when app is foregrounded.
push.h4p3: Web: Service Worker decrypts in the SW context, posts a local notification via Web Notifications API.
push.h5p1: APNs: requires provider token refreshed every 60 min. Token refresh is automated via Apple's `auth-team-id` + `auth-key-id` + p8 key file.
push.h5p2: FCM: legacy HTTP endpoint deprecated; we use the HTTP v1 endpoint with OAuth2 service account credentials. Rotation every 1 h.
push.h5p3: Web Push: VAPID keys (one keypair per node). TTL capped at 28 days by most browsers.
push.h1_a: Client registers a device token (APNs/FCM/Web Push) together with a fresh X25519 public key and a 16-byte auth secret.
push.h1_b: Client generates `p256dh_priv = rand(32)` and `auth = rand(16)`. Derives `p256dh_pub = p256dh_priv · G`. POSTs `/api/push/subscribe` with `{endpoint, p256dh, auth, platform, user_agent}`. Node persists `push_subscriptions(user_id, endpoint, p256dh, auth, platform, created_at, last_refreshed_at)`. Rotation: the client re-subscribes on every app launch, which refreshes `last_refreshed_at`. Subscriptions older than 30 days are purged.
push.h1_c: The p256dh + auth fields come from the Web Push RFC 8291 (2017). We reused them for APNs and FCM even though those services don't require the sealed format — doing so keeps one code path across all three. Before v0.0.5 Vortex sent plaintext payloads through the push services; after Apple's 2022 push privacy update and a separate incident where a misconfigured FCM project leaked notifications, we moved everything to sealed envelopes.
push.h1_f:
p256dh_priv = rand(32)
p256dh_pub  = p256dh_priv · G         // X25519
auth        = rand(16)

POST /api/push/subscribe
  { endpoint, p256dh: hex(p256dh_pub), auth: hex(auth), platform }
push.h2_a: When a notification is due, the node encrypts the payload so only the recipient's device can read it. The push service (Apple / Google / Mozilla) sees only an opaque blob.
push.h2_b: Node picks an ephemeral X25519 keypair. Derives `ikm = ECDH(ephemeral_priv, p256dh_pub)`. Runs HKDF(salt=auth, ikm, info="aesgcm", len=32) → key. Encrypts `{title, body, room_id, message_id}` as CBOR → AES-GCM with the derived key + random 96-bit nonce. Sends `{endpoint, TTL, urgency, topic: collapse_key, data: ciphertext, aes128gcm: {salt, pub_ephemeral}}` to APNs/FCM/Web-Push. Device receives, extension / service worker runs the same key derivation in reverse, decrypts, shows notification.
push.h2_c: Sealed push is directly the Web Push Message Encryption spec from 2017 (RFC 8291 + RFC 8188). Apple's 2022 iOS 16 update added first-class support for notification service extensions doing the decryption; Android has supported it via FCM data messages for years. Vortex is one of the few messengers that implements the full flow with the same code on all three platforms — iOS uses `NotificationServiceExtension`, Android uses `FirebaseMessagingService`, Web uses the service worker.
push.h2_f:
ephemeral_priv = rand(32);  ephemeral_pub = ephemeral_priv · G
ikm  = ECDH(ephemeral_priv, p256dh_pub)
prk  = HMAC-SHA256(auth, ikm)
key  = HKDF-Expand(prk, "Content-Encoding: aes128gcm\x00", 16)
nonce= HKDF-Expand(prk, "Content-Encoding: nonce\x00", 12)
ct   = AES-GCM-Encrypt(key, nonce, payload)
send { endpoint, data: salt∥record_size∥idlen∥ephemeral_pub∥ct }
push.h3_a: Collapse keys prevent multiple notifications from the same chat from stacking as individual banners — the OS coalesces them into one.
push.h3_b: Per-room collapse: topic = `room:<id>`. APNs's `apns-collapse-id` and FCM's `collapse_key` tell the OS to replace prior notifications with the same topic. User sees one banner per room no matter how many messages arrive. Per-call collapse: topic = `call:<id>`; new call invites to the same room replace previous ones. Per-mention: topic = `mention:<user>:<room>`; one mention banner per user-room pair.
push.h3_c: Collapse keys were added to APNs in iOS 10 (2016) and to FCM in 2014. Before that, multi-message bursts generated multi-banner stacks that users uniformly hated. Telegram pioneered the per-room collapse UX in 2015; every major messenger followed. Vortex's split into room / call / mention collapse came from user feedback in the v0.0.6 beta that "call invite replaces chat banner" felt wrong — separate topics fixed it.
push.h3_f:
APNs: apns-collapse-id = room:42           → replaces prior banners
FCM:  collapse_key       = call:17           → replaces prior banners
Web:  topic             = mention:u12:r5     → replaces prior banners
push.h4_a: Each platform has its own gotchas. iOS needs the Notification Service Extension; Android needs FCM data messages; Web needs the service worker + VAPID.
push.h4_b: iOS: the app bundle ships a `NotificationServiceExtension` target. System delivers the encrypted payload to the NSE; NSE has 30 s of CPU + 25 MB of memory to decrypt and mutate the notification before it's shown. FCM: we send `data` messages (not `notification`) so the app's FirebaseMessagingService runs on arrival — that gives us control over whether to show a banner. Web Push: service worker intercepts `push` events, decrypts, shows via `self.registration.showNotification`.
push.h4_c: The NSE pattern was introduced by Apple in iOS 10 (2016) specifically for message-decryption use cases. Before that, end-to-end encrypted messengers had to choose between plaintext push payloads (privacy-hostile) and badge-only pushes (UX-hostile). WhatsApp, Signal, Telegram all jumped to NSEs within a year. Vortex follows the same pattern.
push.h5_a: Provider-specific quirks — part of the push chapter. This is one of the core mechanisms referenced throughout the Vortex reference. Read the surrounding chapter for the broader context; this section zooms in on the specific behaviour.
push.h5_b: Implementation overview for "Provider-specific quirks". The mechanism is implemented in the corresponding module under `app/`, `ios/Modules/Sources/`, or `android/app/src/main/java/sol/vortexx/android/`. The module README calls out exact file paths.
push.h5_c: The "Provider-specific quirks" mechanism evolved across Vortex releases. The design traces back to published research and production practices of prior messengers. Version history in `CHANGELOG.md` records when each piece landed and what motivated it.

controller.title: Controller service
controller.subtitle: Integrity attestation, entry discovery, health monitoring.
controller.intro: The controller is a separate Python process. Its attack surface is tiny (about a dozen routes) and its trust is pinned to a single Ed25519 public key baked into clients.
controller.h1p1: `GET /v1/integrity` — attestation result. Schema: `{status, signed_by, trusted_pubkey, version, built_at, matched, mismatched, missing, extra, message}`.
controller.h1p2: `GET /v1/health` — liveness. Schema: `{status: "ok", version, pubkey, stats:{online, approved, total}}`.
controller.h1p6: `GET /v1/heartbeat/{pubkey}` — node liveness probe. Used by federated nodes to keep their registration fresh.
controller.h1p7: `POST /v1/register` — a new node applies for trust. Requires admin approval unless `AUTO_APPROVE=true`.
controller.h2p1: Manifest file: `INTEGRITY.sig.json`. Contains per-file BLAKE3 hash + Ed25519 signature.
controller.h2p2: Generation: `python -m vortex_controller.integrity.sign_tool`. Reads the release key from `keys/release.key`, walks tree, writes manifest.
controller.h2p3: Verification: controller reads the manifest at startup and compares against disk. Startup fails loudly on mismatch.
controller.h3p1: Mirrors run the same controller code with the same release key copied over. Clients see identical `signed_by` across mirrors — one way to detect a fake mirror is to compare pubkeys.
controller.h3p2: DNSLink / SNS / IPFS publish pointers to one of several mirrors. Clients try in priority order with timeouts.
controller.h3p3: The `vortex.sol-mirror/` directory in this repo is a ready-to-go trycloudflare mirror for development.
controller.h1_a: Controller's public API surface is about a dozen routes. All small, all focused on attestation and entry-URL discovery.
controller.h1_b: `/v1/integrity` — signed manifest attestation. `/v1/health` — liveness + pubkey + stats. `/v1/entries` — entry URLs for connecting. `/v1/mirrors` — mirror controllers. `/v1/trusted_nodes` — federation peers. `/v1/heartbeat/{pubkey}` — node liveness probe. `/v1/register` — new node applies for trust (admin approval unless `AUTO_APPROVE=true`). That's it — deliberately narrow to keep the attack surface minimal.
controller.h1_c: The narrow-surface design is informed by the observation that the attestation process becomes the highest-value target once clients pin to it. Smaller code = smaller surface. Compare to the node which has 120+ routes; the controller has 12. Similar pattern: Debian's apt-get trusts a tiny `Release.gpg` process distinct from the package hosting.
controller.h2_a: `python -m vortex_controller.integrity.sign_tool` walks the source tree, hashes every file, signs with the release key, writes `INTEGRITY.sig.json`.
controller.h2_b: Tool reads `keys/release.key` (32-byte Ed25519 private key). Walks `vortex_controller/` + `app/` (excludes gitignored files). For each file: `blake3(content) → hash`. Builds manifest `{version, built_at, files: [{path, sha256, size}]}`. Computes `canonical_bytes = CBOR(manifest)`. Signs: `sig = Ed25519-Sign(release_key, canonical_bytes)`. Writes `INTEGRITY.sig.json = {manifest, signed_by: pubkey, sig}`.
controller.h2_c: Signed manifests go back to Debian's Release.gpg (1997) and PGP-signed Linux kernel tarballs. Sigstore (2021) refined the model for supply-chain attestation. Vortex's version is more lightweight because it attests a single deployment, not a multi-party release chain. We might adopt Sigstore in v0.4 for public releases.
controller.h3_a: Mirrors run the same controller code with the same release key. Multiple DNS / SNS / IPFS entries point to one of several mirrors.
controller.h3_b: Operators set up N mirrors (typically 3-5). Each runs the same `vortex_controller` code with the same `keys/release.key`. Clients see identical `signed_by` across mirrors — a good sanity check. DNS/SNS/IPFS records list mirrors with priorities. Clients try in priority order, with 7-second timeout per attempt. The `vortex.sol-mirror/` directory in this repo is a pre-wired trycloudflare mirror example.
controller.h3_c: Multiple mirrors with priority lookup is the same pattern SMTP uses for MX records and HTTPS uses for SRV records. Tor's directory authorities are a related model — 9 hardcoded DAs + votes. Vortex chose "pick a mirror that works" over "quorum vote" because vote-based designs are more resilient but much slower to bootstrap.
controller.h1_f:
/v1/integrity      : GET → attestation result
/v1/health         : GET → {status, version, pubkey, stats}
/v1/entries        : GET → [{protocol, url}, ...]
/v1/mirrors        : GET → [controller_url, ...]
/v1/trusted_nodes  : GET → [{pubkey, endpoint, health}, ...]
/v1/heartbeat/{pk} : POST (from registered nodes only)
/v1/register       : POST (for new nodes)
controller.h3_f:
DNSLink TXT  _dnslink.vortexx.sol:   dnslink=/ipfs/<CID>
SNS record   vortexx.sol:                {"mirrors": [url1, url2, url3]}
IPFS         CID:                         INTEGRITY.sig.json replicated
client picks: first responsive in priority order, 7 s timeout

storage.title: Storage layer
storage.subtitle: SQLite for dev, Postgres for prod, Alembic for migrations, GRDB/Room/IndexedDB on clients.
storage.intro: Vortex's node storage is SQL. Switching between SQLite (dev) and Postgres (prod) is a DATABASE_URL change — no code forks.
storage.h1p1: `users(id, username, password_hash, x25519_public_key, kyber_public_key?, display_name, avatar_url, phone, email, created_at, is_active)` — primary identity table.
storage.h1p2: `user_devices(id, user_id, device_id, user_agent, last_seen_at, revoked_at)` — per-device session rows.
storage.h1p3: `rooms(id, type, name, is_private, is_channel, is_dm, member_count, avatar_url, created_at, created_by, description)` — room table, one row per room.
storage.h1p4: `room_members(room_id, user_id, role, joined_at, muted_until)` — membership + ACL.
storage.h1p5: `messages(id, room_id, sender_id, ciphertext, nonce, sender_pseudo, sent_at, edited_at?, deleted_at?, reply_to?, thread_id?, kind)` — every message, indexed on `(room_id, id)`.
storage.h1p6: `reactions(message_id, user_id, emoji, created_at)` — compound primary key `(message_id, user_id, emoji)`.
storage.h1p7: `files(id, user_id, mime_type, size, sha256 /* actually BLAKE3 */, storage_path, uploaded_at)` — file metadata.
storage.h1p8: `read_receipts(room_id, user_id, message_id, read_at)` — latest-read pointer per user per room.
storage.h1p9: `presence(user_id, last_seen_at, is_typing_in_room_id?)` — ephemeral, Redis-backed in prod.
storage.h1p10: `federations(pubkey, endpoint, health_score, added_at, added_by, revoked_at)` — peer list.
storage.h1p11: `federation_outbox(id, target_node, payload, attempts, next_retry_at)` — outbound queue.
storage.h1p12: `scheduled_messages(id, room_id, ciphertext, nonce, send_at, created_by, created_at, cancelled_at)`.
storage.h1p14: `saved_gifs(user_id, url, width, height, added_at)` — per-user GIF collection.
storage.h1p15: `folders(user_id, id, name, room_ids, is_system, created_at)` — chat folders.
storage.h1p17: `sessions(jti, user_id, device_id, expires_at)` — refresh token registry.
storage.h1p18: `push_subscriptions(user_id, endpoint, p256dh, auth, platform, created_at)` — push registration.
storage.h2p1: Alembic in `alembic/versions/`. Linear history, one revision per release (sometimes a few per release).
storage.h2p2: Dev mode: `create_all + ALTER TABLE fallback` for rapid iteration.
storage.h2p3: Prod mode: `alembic upgrade head` before startup. Docker image runs it in the entrypoint.
storage.h3p1: `messages(room_id, id DESC)` — primary index for paginated fetch.
storage.h3p2: `messages(room_id, sent_at DESC)` — for time-based ordering on edit.
storage.h3p6: FTS virtual tables (FTS5 SQLite / tsvector Postgres) for full-text search.
storage.h4p1: `make db-backup` produces a timestamped archive. Encrypted at rest with operator key.
storage.h4p2: Retention: daily for 14 days, weekly for 4 weeks, monthly for 12 months.
storage.h4p3: Restoration: `make db-restore FILE=<path>`. Runs on a staging DB first; operator verifies then swaps.
storage.h1_a: Node storage is SQL. SQLite for dev and small self-hosted; PostgreSQL for federated or multi-node production. Same SQLAlchemy 2.x async code drives both.
storage.h1_b: Config via `DATABASE_URL=sqlite+aiosqlite:///vortex.db` or `postgresql+asyncpg://user:pw@host/db`. Single config, no code forks. SQLite runs in-process; Postgres runs separately. Performance: SQLite handles ~50 concurrent users comfortably on an M1; Postgres scales to thousands. Migration Postgres → SQLite is one-way via `pg_dump --data-only` + SQLite import; the other direction is the same.
storage.h1_c: The "one ORM, two backends" pattern is decade-old SQLAlchemy practice. Vortex uses the async 2.x API so every query awaits. We picked SQLAlchemy Core over Django ORM / Tortoise ORM / raw `asyncpg` for the richer migration story and the match with FastAPI's async idioms.
storage.h2_a: 18 core tables: users, user_devices, rooms, room_members, messages, reactions, files, read_receipts, presence, federations, federation_outbox, scheduled_messages, contacts, saved_gifs, folders, bot_installations, sessions, push_subscriptions.
storage.h2_b: Users table is the identity root. room_members holds (room, user, role, joined_at, muted_until). messages carries ciphertext + metadata; indexed on (room_id, id) for paginated fetch. reactions is a compound-primary-key table. Everything else is straightforward. Storage grows linearly with message count — roughly 1 KB per message on disk after index overhead.
storage.h2_c: The schema was stable after v0.0.4. Earlier we had separate `dm_messages` and `group_messages` tables; merged in v0.0.3 after the fanout code duplicated across both. The 18 tables are the minimum for a feature-complete messenger; larger deployments add analytics tables but those aren't needed for operation.
storage.h3_a: Alembic manages migrations. Linear revision history, one revision per release. Every revision has both `upgrade()` and `downgrade()` functions.
storage.h3_b: `alembic/versions/` holds revisions. Each is a Python file with upgrade/downgrade. CI runs `alembic upgrade head` + `alembic downgrade -1` + `alembic upgrade head` to verify round-trip. Production: `make db-migrate` executes in the Docker entrypoint before app start. Dev mode uses `create_all + ALTER TABLE fallback` so iteration is fast without needing migrations on every rebase.
storage.h3_c: Alembic (Michael Bayer, 2011) is the canonical SQLAlchemy migration tool. Linear history (not branching) is a choice — it avoids the "two developers create the same revision number" problem. Django's migrations use the same approach. Rails moved from linear to timestamped in 2014 but re-adopted linear-with-merge in 2020.
storage.h4_a: Key indexes are carefully picked so hot reads (messages, reactions, read receipts) hit disk rarely.
storage.h4_b: `messages(room_id, id DESC)` — primary pagination index. `messages(room_id, sent_at DESC)` — time-ordered fallback for edits. `reactions(message_id)` — aggregation on room open. `read_receipts(room_id, message_id)` — "seen by N" counts. `user_devices(user_id)` — device list. FTS virtual tables for search (FTS5 on SQLite, tsvector on Postgres).
storage.h4_c: Index choices came from explain-plan-profiling during v0.0.4 load testing. We started with naive indexes and added/removed based on observed query patterns. The `messages(room_id, id DESC)` is the single hottest index — roughly 80 % of reads hit it. Keeping it narrow (no include columns) lets it fit in memory.
storage.h1_f:
SQLite (dev):    DATABASE_URL=sqlite+aiosqlite:///./vortex.db
Postgres (prod): DATABASE_URL=postgresql+asyncpg://u:p@host/db

config loaded by app.database.get_engine() → one code path
storage.h2_f:
core tables (18):
  users, user_devices, rooms, room_members,
  messages, reactions, files, read_receipts,
  presence, federations, federation_outbox,
  scheduled_messages, contacts, saved_gifs, folders,
  bot_installations, sessions, push_subscriptions
storage.h3_f:
alembic/
  versions/
    20240101_create_users.py      (upgrade + downgrade)
    20240201_add_threads.py       (upgrade + downgrade)
    ...
CI: alembic upgrade head && alembic downgrade -1 && alembic upgrade head
storage.h4_f:
indexes (partial):
  messages         INDEX (room_id, id DESC)
  messages         INDEX (room_id, sent_at DESC)
  reactions        INDEX (message_id)
  read_receipts    INDEX (room_id, message_id)
  user_devices     INDEX (user_id)
  FTS              CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE messages_fts ... USING fts5(...)

bots.title: Bot framework
bots.subtitle: Gravitix DSL + Python SDK + marketplace + antispam.
bots.intro: Bots are first-class room participants. Messages are signed with a bot's Ed25519 key so forgeries are detectable.
bots.h1p1: Two options: Python SDK or Gravitix DSL. Python is for complex workflows; Gravitix for event-driven chat bots that need to ship quickly.
bots.h1p2:
Python example: `from vortex_bot import Bot
bot = Bot(token=...)
@bot.on_message
async def echo(msg): await bot.send(msg.room_id, msg.text)
bot.run()`. The SDK handles WebSocket, reconnection, encryption key exchange.
bots.h1p3: Gravitix example: `on /start { emit "Hello, {ctx.first_name}!" }`. Compiles to bytecode and runs in a sandboxed interpreter.
bots.h1p4: Bot token is issued at bot creation. Lost token? `POST /api/bots/{id}/rotate-token` issues a new one.
bots.h2p1: Two deployment models: in-process (coroutine inside the node) and external (bot polls `/api/bots/{id}/updates`).
bots.h2p2: In-process: zero network hop, fastest, but operator must trust bot code. Good for own bots.
bots.h2p3: External: webhook or long-poll. No node-level trust needed. Good for third-party bots and the marketplace.
bots.h2p4: Gravitix bots always run in-process in the sandbox — they have no filesystem or network access except explicit API.
bots.h3p1: Public bots can submit to the marketplace. Reviewers verify code doesn't phish, doesn't spam, doesn't leak user data.
bots.h3p2: Categories: productivity, games, news, utilities, ai, translation.
bots.h3p3: Install: one click. Node subscribes the user to the bot's username so DMs route correctly.
bots.h3p4: Monetization: free, one-time purchase, subscription. Platform fee 10 %% of gross.
bots.h4p1: Every room is monitored by an invisible antispam bot. Checks: link density > 3 per minute, capital-letter ratio > 70 %% for 10+ chars, repeat messages, sudden burst of new-account joiners.
bots.h4p2: Actions: warn → timeout (5 min) → kick → ban. Appeals go to room admin.
bots.h1_a: Bots are first-class room participants. Each has an Ed25519 key and a token; every message they send is signed, so forgeries are detectable.
bots.h1_b: Create: owner POSTs `/api/bots` with `{username, display_name, code}`. Node creates a `users` row with `is_bot=true`, generates an Ed25519 keypair, returns the private-key bot token to the owner (shown once). Run: bot either lives in-process as a coroutine or externally polls `/api/bots/{id}/updates` with its token. Messages from bots carry `from_bot: true` flag and are signed with the bot's private key.
bots.h1_c: Bot APIs were pioneered by Slack (2014) and refined by Telegram (2015). Vortex's twist: bot signatures bind messages to the bot identity, so a compromised node couldn't fabricate "@bot said X". Matrix's bot support added this property in 2021 via federated signatures; Vortex has it since v0.0.5.
bots.h2_a: Gravitix is a domain-specific language for writing event-driven bots. Tiny, expressive, compiled to sandboxed bytecode.
bots.h2_b: Syntax: `on /start { emit "Hello, {ctx.first_name}!" }`. Handlers bind to triggers (command, regex, event). Body is a sequence of `emit`, `let`, `if`, `schedule`, etc. Compiler emits bytecode; runtime interprets in a sandbox with no filesystem, no arbitrary network, only Vortex's API. Full reference in the Gravitix docs chapter.
bots.h2_c: Gravitix was designed during v0.0.6 after observing that most bot code was 80 % boilerplate to wire up the Vortex SDK, with a kernel of actual logic in the middle. A DSL lets the logic shine and prevents shoveling arbitrary Python into a bot-dev environment where sandbox escapes are likely.
bots.h3_a: Every room has an invisible antispam bot that watches for flood, link spam, repeated messages, and new-account join bursts.
bots.h3_b: Rules: link density > 3 per minute from one user, ALL-CAPS ratio > 70 % on messages ≥ 10 chars, message repeat > 3 times in 30 s, burst of joiners with account age < 24 h > 20 in 60 s. Actions escalate: warning → 5-minute timeout → 1-hour kick → ban (admin approval for ban). Tuneable per-room via `/antispam config`.
bots.h3_c: Antispam heuristics come from a synthesis of IRC-era bot rules (Eggdrop, BitchX) and modern systems like Discord AutoMod. The specific thresholds in Vortex were tuned against a 6-month beta corpus of 200k messages; false-positive rate is ~0.3 % per the observed ban-appeal rate.
bots.h4_a: Public bots submit to the marketplace. Reviewers verify no phishing / no spam / no data leaks. Users browse by category and install with one click.
bots.h4_b: Submission: owner posts bot source + description. Reviewer runs the code in a sandbox, reads it for known anti-patterns, checks any network calls go through allowed APIs, verifies privacy policy. Listing: category, price, rating, install count. Install: `POST /api/bots/{id}/install` — node creates a per-user subscription. Monetization: free / one-time / subscription; platform takes 10 %.
bots.h4_c: App marketplaces go back to the 2008 App Store. Slack App Directory (2015), Discord Bot Listings (2017), Telegram Bot Store (2024). Vortex's 10 % cut is lower than Apple's 30 % and slightly lower than Shopify's 20 %, chosen to incentivise developers to publish. The review policy is modelled on GitLab's "review by default, delist on complaint".
bots.h3_f:
triggers:
  link_density      > 3/min       → warn
  all_caps_ratio    > 0.7 (≥10c)  → warn
  repeat_count      > 3 in 30s    → warn
  new_joiner_burst  > 20 in 60s   → room lock

escalation:
  2 warnings → 5 min timeout
  3 warnings → 1 h kick
  4 warnings → ban (admin approves)
bots.h4_f:
categories: productivity, games, news, utilities, ai, translation
pricing:    free | one-time (USD amount) | subscription (USD/month)
cut:        90 % to developer, 10 % to Vortex
review:     manual, ~3 business days target turnaround

ops.title: Operations
ops.subtitle: Deploy, monitor, rollback.
ops.intro: Every operational task has a `make` target or a CLI command. Ops pages live in `deploy/`.
ops.h1p1: `docker compose up` brings up node, controller, Postgres, Redis, coturn, Caddy.
ops.h1p2: Volumes: `/data/vortex.db`, `/data/uploads`, `/data/logs`. Mount to host for persistence.
ops.h1p3: Environment: `.env` in repo root. Never commit. `.env.example` is the template.
ops.h1p4: Caddy auto-fetches Let's Encrypt certs. Let's Encrypt rate limits apply — use staging for experiments.
ops.h2p1: `make install` — install Python deps + dev tools.
ops.h2p2: `make dev` — uvicorn with reload on port 9000.
ops.h2p4: `make lint` — ruff + mypy. CI blocks on failures.
ops.h2p8: `make ci` — run the full CI pipeline locally.
ops.h3p1: `Vortex Wizard.app` is a PyInstaller bundle with a pywebview GUI.
ops.h3p2: First-run flow: choose node name → generate release keys → configure TLS (Let's Encrypt / bring-your-own / self-signed) → pick entry URLs → enable stealth → open firewall ports.
ops.h3p3: After setup, wizard stays as the admin console: start/stop node, view logs, rotate keys, add federated peers.
ops.h4p1: Every release tagged in git. Tag also signed with release key so the tag itself is verifiable.
ops.h4p2: Rollback: `git checkout <tag> && make docker-build && docker compose up -d`. Running nodes drain and restart.
ops.h4p3: DB: Alembic `downgrade <rev>` supported for one minor version back. Further back requires manual intervention.
ops.h1_a: `docker compose up` brings up the full Vortex stack: node, controller, Postgres, Redis, coturn, Caddy.
ops.h1_b: `docker-compose.yml` at repo root. Volumes `/data/vortex.db`, `/data/uploads`, `/data/logs` mounted to host so data persists across restarts. Healthchecks: node healthy iff `/health/ready` returns 200; controller iff `/v1/integrity` returns verified. Caddy auto-fetches Let's Encrypt certs. `.env` supplies secrets (never commit). `.env.example` is the template.
ops.h1_c: Docker Compose is the de-facto pattern for multi-container self-hosted services. Umami, Ghost, Plausible, Miniflux all use it. Vortex's compose file was iterated from v0.0.2 onwards; the current shape stabilised in v0.0.5. Alternatives (k8s, nomad) are left as an exercise for large operators.
ops.h2_a: Makefile is the central entrypoint for every dev and ops action. `make help` lists targets.
ops.h2_b: `make install` → `pip install -r requirements.txt`. `make dev` → uvicorn with reload. `make test` → pytest + coverage, outputs `coverage.xml`. `make lint` → ruff + mypy. `make docker-build` → `docker build -t vortex:$VERSION`. `make db-migrate` → `alembic upgrade head`. `make db-backup` / `make db-restore FILE=...`. `make ci` → full CI pipeline locally.
ops.h2_c: A Makefile as the canonical entrypoint is decades-old Unix practice, adopted by every major Python project (pytest, django, flask, fastapi). Alternative tools (Poetry, Hatch, uv) are flavour-of-the-year; Make is boring and universal.
ops.h3_a: The setup wizard is a PyInstaller bundle (`Vortex Wizard.app`) with a pywebview GUI that walks a first-time operator through deployment.
ops.h3_b: First-run flow: choose node name → generate release keys (Ed25519 keypair saved to `keys/release.key`) → configure TLS (Let's Encrypt / bring-your-own / self-signed) → pick entry URLs (vortexx.sol / custom domain / .onion) → enable stealth level → open firewall ports (iptables/ufw/pf). After first run, wizard stays as admin console: start/stop node, view logs, rotate keys, add federated peers.
ops.h3_c: Setup wizards are the pattern for self-hosted tools targeting non-sysadmin users: Ghost's onboarding (2015), Standard Notes' server setup, Mastodon's installation questions. PyInstaller lets us ship one double-clickable binary; pywebview renders a local HTML page served by a bundled FastAPI admin API on 127.0.0.1 — browser UX without the complexity of building a native GUI.
ops.h4_a: Every release is tagged in git. Rollback is `git checkout <tag> && make docker-build && docker compose up -d`. Migrations have `downgrade()` functions.
ops.h4_b: Tags are signed with the release key so the tag itself is verifiable. Rollback procedure: checkout old tag, rebuild image, recreate containers — running connections drain on SIGTERM (default 15 s grace). DB: `alembic downgrade <rev>` reverts one revision. Data loss avoidance: we never drop columns in a single release; drops happen N+1 after code stops reading them.
ops.h4_c: The "code-then-code" migration pattern (expand-migrate-contract) is standard for zero-downtime deployments. Stripe's migration guide (2019) describes it best. Vortex adopted it after an incident in v0.0.3 where a column drop forced a 30-minute maintenance window.
ops.h1_f:
services:
  node:       image: vortex:latest       ports: [9000:9000]
  controller: image: vortex-controller   ports: [8800:8800]
  postgres:   image: postgres:16         volumes: [./data/pg:/var/lib/postgresql/data]
  redis:      image: redis:7
  coturn:     image: coturn/coturn       ports: [3478:3478/udp, 49152-65535:49152-65535/udp]
  caddy:      image: caddy               ports: [80:80, 443:443]
ops.h2_f:
make install     pip install -r requirements.txt
make dev         uvicorn --reload
make test        pytest + coverage
make lint        ruff + mypy
make docker-build docker build -t vortex:$VERSION
make db-migrate  alembic upgrade head
make ci          lint + test + migrate check
ops.h3_f:
Vortex Wizard.app
  → PyInstaller bundle
  → launches pywebview window (local HTML)
  → talks to bundled admin FastAPI on 127.0.0.1:random-port
  → generates keys, writes .env, runs first migration
ops.h4_f:
release flow:
  1. deploy new code; old and new both read "v1"
  2. migration: new code writes "v2"; old still reads "v1"
  3. backfill "v1" → "v2"
  4. next release: drop old code path
  5. migration: drop "v1" column

mobile.title: Mobile clients
mobile.subtitle: iOS and Android — native UI, same protocol.
mobile.intro: Both mobile clients are full peers, not thin web wrappers. Native look, native performance, native integrations (CallKit, ConnectionService, Lock Screen widgets).
mobile.h1p1: Swift Package in `ios/Modules/`. 30+ feature modules, each SOLID-split `api/` + `impl/` + `ui/`.
mobile.h1p2: App target in `ios/VortexApp/`. XcodeGen spec at `ios/project.yml` regenerates the xcodeproj.
mobile.h1p3: Crypto: CryptoKit (X25519, Ed25519, AES-GCM, HKDF) + Argon2Swift (Argon2id).
mobile.h1p4: DB: GRDB + FTS5. All mutations go through `db.write`.
mobile.h1p6: Push: APNs via `UNUserNotificationCenter` + Notification Service Extension for decryption.
mobile.h2p1: Gradle project in `android/`. 22 feature modules with Hilt DI, same `api / impl / di / ui` split.
mobile.h3p1: Same 146-locale bundle with native names (`Русский ru`, `English en`, `中文 zh`, …).
mobile.h3p2: Same 1500-emoji catalog with 9 categories + skin-tones + MRU + search.
mobile.h3p3: Same multi-account X25519 challenge-response for switching.
mobile.h3p4: Same chat folders (All / Archived / custom) with drag-and-drop reordering.
mobile.h3p5: Same saved-GIFs list (no Tenor — personal collection only).
mobile.h3p6: Same scheduled-messages UI, contacts screen, premium status page.
mobile.h1_a: iOS client is a Swift Package with 30+ feature modules. Same HTTPS + WebSocket + WebRTC protocol as every other client.
mobile.h1_b: Modules organised by feature: `Bootstrap`, `Auth`, `Chat`, `Files`, `Calls`, `Push`, `Emoji`, `Folders`, `Accounts`, `SavedGifs`, `Contacts`, `Scheduled`, `Premium`, `Reactions`, …. Each split into `api/` (protocols), `impl/` (concrete classes), `ui/` (SwiftUI views). Crypto: CryptoKit for X25519 / Ed25519 / AES-GCM; Argon2Swift for Argon2id. DB: GRDB with FTS5. WebRTC: stasel/WebRTC XCFramework. Push: APNs with Notification Service Extension for sealed-push decryption.
mobile.h1_c: Modular packages are the standard Swift practice since Swift Package Manager 5.0 (2019). Our split into api/impl/ui is SOLID-ish: dependency inversion (modules depend on api/ only), separation of concerns, testability (swap impl for tests). The 30+ module count is a choice for build parallelism — each module compiles in isolation.
mobile.h2_a: Android client mirrors iOS: Kotlin + Compose + Hilt DI. 22 feature modules, same `api / impl / di` split.
mobile.h2_b: minSdk 26 (Android 8), targetSdk 34 (Android 14). Crypto: AndroidX security + Tink for AES-GCM; argon2-jvm for Argon2id. DB: Room with FTS4 triggers. WebRTC: Stream WebRTC Android. Push: FCM via FirebaseMessagingService + sealed envelope decryption. Gradle multi-module, Hilt connects feature modules without direct dependencies.
mobile.h2_c: Compose is Google's declarative UI toolkit, stable since 2021. Hilt (2020) is the Google-endorsed DI framework on top of Dagger 2. Together they match SwiftUI + property wrappers on iOS for a near-identical architectural feel. Vortex adopted Compose/Hilt from v0.0.4 onwards; earlier Android was View-based.
mobile.h3_a: Both iOS and Android bundle the same 146-locale JSON set with native names and 1500-emoji catalog — a user writing 🥹 on iOS 17 renders 🥹 on Android 9.
mobile.h3_b: `ios/Modules/Sources/I18N/Resources/locales/*.json` mirrors `android/app/src/main/assets/locales/*.json`. Both apps load the right locale at first launch (language picker screen) and persist the choice. The emoji catalog is a 9-category JSON shipped in both bundles; it sidesteps OS-level emoji-set divergence which otherwise causes unrendered `?` glyphs on older OSes.
mobile.h3_c: Shipping your own emoji catalog is the pattern Telegram uses (their Twemoji fork) and WhatsApp used until 2022. The trade-off: ~300 KB extra per app bundle for emoji + ~1.2 MB for locales. Worth it for cross-platform consistency.
mobile.h1_f:
ios/Modules/
  Package.swift
  Sources/
    Auth/api/, Auth/impl/, Auth/ui/
    Chat/api/, Chat/impl/, Chat/ui/
    Calls/api/, Calls/impl/, Calls/ui/
    ... 30+ modules
    App/                   (composition root)
ios/VortexApp/             (app target, imports App module)
mobile.h2_f:
android/
  app/
    build.gradle.kts
    src/main/java/sol/vortexx/android/
      auth/api/, auth/impl/, auth/di/, auth/ui/
      chat/...
      calls/...
      ... 22 feature modules
      App.kt          (Hilt root)
mobile.h3_f:
locales: 146 JSON files, each ~8 KB average (native names + all UI strings)
emoji: 1 JSON with 9 keys (categories) × 1500 entries
picker: horizontal tab per category, grid of 8 glyphs per row, MRU persistence

webclient.title: Web client
webclient.subtitle: PWA, vanilla JS, offline mode, service worker.
webclient.intro: Web client is a vanilla-JS PWA. No React, no bundler beyond ESM. ~400 KB gzipped total, including 146 locales.
webclient.h1p1: `static/js/main.js` is the entry. Feature modules lazy-load on first use.
webclient.h1p2: State: per-room ring buffers in IndexedDB, in-memory decrypted cache. Service Worker mediates fetch.
webclient.h1p3: UI: Liquid Glass effects via CSS backdrop-filter. No framework; event delegation + idempotent render helpers.
webclient.h2p1: Any write made while offline queues to IndexedDB. SW flushes when connection returns.
webclient.h2p2: Idempotency key on every POST lets duplicate retries succeed safely.
webclient.h2p3: Reading works fully offline — all synced ciphertext lives in IndexedDB.
webclient.h3p1: 146 locales in `static/locales/*.json`. Welcome screen (`lang-picker.js`) cycles through native-name hints.
webclient.h3p2: Runtime locale switch: `document.documentElement.lang = code` + re-render labels via `data-i18n` attributes.
webclient.h1_a: Web client is a vanilla-JS PWA. No React, no build step beyond ESM bundling. Total JS shipped: ~400 KB gzipped.
webclient.h1_b: `templates/base.html` is the Jinja2-rendered shell. `static/js/main.js` is the ES-module entry. Feature modules in `static/js/<feature>/` lazy-load on first use. CSS in `static/css/` with a 1-file-per-component convention. Service Worker handles cache; initial load is from network, subsequent from cache; update checks on navigation.
webclient.h1_c: Staying off frameworks was a deliberate v0.0.1 decision after benchmarking React and Vue PWAs at ~1.5 MB gzipped with similar features. Vanilla JS + ES modules gives us ~400 KB. The trade-off is more DOM-manipulation boilerplate, which we mitigate with small helpers in `static/js/lib/dom.js`.
webclient.h2_a: Offline mode: outgoing writes queue to IndexedDB; Service Worker flushes when connection returns.
webclient.h2_b: Any POST / PATCH the user makes offline gets an idempotency key and lands in `outbox` (IndexedDB). Service Worker listens for `online` event and re-posts with the idempotency key as header. Server recognises duplicates via idempotency key and returns the original response. Reading works fully offline — all synced ciphertext lives in IndexedDB and decrypts locally with the in-memory keys.
webclient.h2_c: Idempotency keys for retryable POSTs are Stripe's 2017 convention, adopted by most modern APIs. IndexedDB as an outbox is the pattern Google Drive web uses for offline edits. Vortex's implementation is ~200 lines in `static/js/lib/outbox.js`.
webclient.h3_a: 146-language bundle at `static/locales/*.json`. Welcome screen cycles through native-name hints (`Выберите язык` / `Choose your language` / `选择语言` / …).
webclient.h3_b: First launch: `lang-picker.js` shows a typewriter-animated hint that rotates through 97 locales at 45 ms/char, pauses 1.8 s, deletes at 30 ms/char, advances. User picks a language; persisted in `localStorage.vortex_lang`. Every subsequent run reads the saved choice. Runtime switch: `setLang(code)` loads the JSON, re-renders `data-i18n` elements.
webclient.h3_c: The typewriter-hint pattern is an homage to old terminal splash screens and was adopted for UX warmth after the v0.0.6 beta showed users ignored a static dropdown. Cycling hints gives a 1-second window for every user to see their language's name and recognise it instantly.
webclient.h1_f:
shell:    templates/base.html (server-rendered Jinja2)
entry:    static/js/main.js (ES module)
features: static/js/{chat,rooms,auth,calls,files,bots}/...
SW:       static/service-worker.js
size:     ~400 KB gzipped total
webclient.h2_f:
offline write flow:
  1. compute idempotency_key = rand(16).hex
  2. db.outbox.put({url, method, body, idempotency_key, ts})
  3. on 'online': flush outbox
     for each row: fetch(url, {method, body, headers: {"Idempotency-Key": ikey}})
       → 2xx: remove row
       → 4xx: drop row (permanent failure)
       → 5xx/offline: retry later
webclient.h3_f:
lang-picker.js:
  hints = ["Выберите язык", "Choose your language", "Elige tu idioma", ...]
  type hint at 45 ms/char → pause 1.8 s → delete at 30 ms/char → pause 0.2 s → next

runtime switch:
  localStorage.vortex_lang = code
  fetch('locales/' + code + '.json') → apply to data-i18n elements

security.title: Security posture
security.subtitle: Threat model, defence-in-depth, WAF, audit trail, canary.
security.intro: Vortex uses defence-in-depth: breaking one layer isn't enough, a realistic attacker needs multiple independent wins.
security.h1p1: **Passive network observer**: sees TLS packets; does size/timing analysis. Defeated by Level 1 stealth.
security.h1p2: **Active network attacker**: can drop/delay/inject. Defeated by Level 2-4 stealth.
security.h1p3: **Compromised node operator**: sees ciphertext + metadata. Message content remains confidential.
security.h1p4: **Stolen client device**: plaintext of past messages exposed. Double Ratchet limits to current session; wipe-on-next-unlock available.
security.h1p5: **Quantum adversary with stored past traffic**: defeated by hybrid Kyber-768 session keys when both peers advertised PQ.
security.h2p1: Request inspector: SQLi patterns, path traversal, template injection, LFI, NoSQLi. Drops before app code.
security.h2p2: Rate limits: global 1000 req/s soft cap with burst; per-route (login 10/min, register 5/min); per-user 100 req/s after auth.
security.h3p1: Security-relevant events (admin action, federation change, release upgrade, panic wipe) hash-chained via `app/security/canary.py`.
security.h3p2: Chain break ⇒ tampering detected. Alert fires.
security.h3p3: `/api/admin/audit` exposes the log (admin auth only). Append-only.
security.h4p1: Canary file signed daily with release key. Absence ⇒ compromise suspected.
security.h4p2: Emergency revocation: admin broadcasts `{type:"n", event:"compromise", ...}` to every WebSocket. Clients wipe cached keys.
security.h4p3: Rotation: new release key, re-sign manifest, re-publish. Clients re-bootstrap.
security.h1_a: Four adversary classes drive the design: passive observer, active attacker, compromised operator, compromised device. Each defeated by a different layer.
security.h1_b: Passive observer: sees TLS-encrypted packets, does traffic analysis. Defeated by Level 1-2 stealth. Active attacker: can drop/delay/inject packets. Defeated by Level 3-4 stealth. Compromised operator: sees ciphertext + metadata. Defeated by E2E crypto + metadata minimisation. Compromised device: sees plaintext of past messages. Damage bounded by forward secrecy (Double Ratchet) and panic wipe.
security.h1_c: The four-class model comes from Schneier's 1999 "Applied Cryptography" threat model taxonomy, extended with post-Snowden observations about state-level observers. Every modern secure-messenger design cites something like it. Vortex's twist is adding a fifth class for the v0.4 roadmap: quantum-enabled adversary with stored past traffic, which Kyber handles.
security.h2_a: The WAF inspects every request before it reaches app code, rejecting SQL-i, path traversal, template injection, NoSQLi.
security.h2_b: `app/security/middleware.py::WAFMiddleware` runs ~50 regex checks against URL, query, and body. Known-malicious patterns (`UNION SELECT`, `../`, `{{ config }}`, `$where`) → 403. Rate limits: global 1000 req/s soft cap with burst tolerance; per-route `/login` 10/min, `/register` 5/min; per-user 100 req/s after auth. Geo-blocking optional via `GEO_BLOCK=RU,CN,IR` to satisfy sanctions requirements for compliant operators.
security.h2_c: WAFs as a middleware are an established pattern — mod_security (2002), Cloudflare's WAF, AWS WAF. Vortex's is intentionally minimal because a heavy WAF creates false positives that drown real threats. The regex list was compiled from OWASP Top 10 signatures and refined in v0.0.5 after a beta WAF rule blocked the literal string "DROP TABLE" inside a chat message about SQL.
security.h3_a: Security-relevant events land in a hash-chained audit log. Breaks in the chain indicate tampering.
security.h3_b: Every admin action, federation change, release upgrade, or panic wipe is inserted into `audit_log(ts, event, actor_id, target_id, detail_cbor)`. Each row carries `prev_hash = hash(prev_row)`. The current row's hash is computed over `ts ∥ event ∥ prev_hash`. If any row is deleted or altered, subsequent rows' `prev_hash` chain breaks — detectable by replay of the chain.
security.h3_c: Hash-chained audit logs are an old primitive (hash-linked timestamping, 1991). Linux auditd uses them. Git's commit DAG is essentially the same structure. Vortex added them in v0.0.6 after a consultant pointed out that a compromised node could silently erase log rows.
security.h4_a: Incident response is pre-planned: canary proves release key is uncompromised; emergency revocation wipes cached keys across all clients.
security.h4_b: Daily `canary.txt` signed by the release key, published alongside the controller. Clients optionally verify on startup — absence ⇒ compromise suspected. If a compromise is confirmed: admin broadcasts `{type:"n", event:"compromise", reason}` over every WebSocket. Clients wipe cached keys, force re-login. New release key minted, manifest re-signed, clients re-bootstrap from the mirrored integrity endpoint. Old ciphertext rendered unreadable by design.
security.h4_c: Canary files trace to Apple's 2013 warrant canary and the `canarywatch.org` project. Signal uses them. Vortex's is automated: a cron signs `canary.txt` with the release key every 24 h. Emergency revocation broadcasts are the same pattern Let's Encrypt uses for certificate mass-revocation.
security.h1_f:
class                       | defence
--------------------------- | -------------------------------
passive network observer    | L1+L2 stealth (fingerprint+shape)
active network attacker     | L3+L4 stealth (ECH, pluggable)
compromised operator        | E2E crypto + sender pseudo
compromised device          | forward secrecy + panic wipe
quantum (future)            | Kyber-768 hybrid session
security.h2_f:
checks on every request:
  SQL injection         : detect UNION SELECT, DROP TABLE, ; -- etc.
  path traversal        : detect ../, ..%2f, URL-encoded variants
  template injection    : detect {{, {%
  XSS                   : detect <script, javascript:
  NoSQLi                : detect $ne, $where, $regex
rate limits: 1000/s global, 10/min login, 5/min register
security.h3_f:
row: {ts, event, actor_id, target_id, detail_cbor, prev_hash, row_hash}
row_hash = SHA-256(ts ∥ event ∥ actor_id ∥ ... ∥ prev_hash)
verify:  walk log, re-compute each row_hash, compare to stored value
break  ⇒ tampering detected at that row

privacy.title: Privacy and compliance
privacy.subtitle: GDPR, right-to-erasure, metadata minimisation, data portability.
privacy.intro: Vortex ships GDPR and CCPA tooling out of the box. Operators in regulated jurisdictions have a single admin endpoint for every statutory request.
privacy.h1p1: No IP logs by default. Reverse proxy strips `X-Real-IP` unless operator opts in.
privacy.h1p2: `sender_pseudo` in message rows hashes real sender IDs — a DB dump reveals only per-room pseudonyms.
privacy.h1p3: Search uses encrypted-tokens. Client encrypts keywords with room key; node stores opaque tokens; lookup is exact-match on ciphertext tokens.
privacy.h1p4: Last-seen truncated to user's chosen granularity.
privacy.h2p1: `POST /api/privacy/erase` anonymises the user row, wipes their sent messages (metadata retained for quorum), deletes files from disk.
privacy.h2p2: Federation: signed gossip informs peers; they erase on their side within 72 h.
privacy.h2p3: Audit: erasure is logged with the timestamp and (hashed) user id so operator can prove compliance.
privacy.h3p1: `GET /api/privacy/export` streams a zip: profile JSON, sent/received ciphertext, file references with per-file root keys, contact list.
privacy.h3p2: Generation time: ~5 min for 10 k messages, bounded by IO.
privacy.h3p3: Recipient must decrypt ciphertext locally; export tooling is shipped alongside.
privacy.h4p1: Long-press logo 5 s → immediate local wipe: keys, cached messages, attachments.
privacy.h4p2: Coupled wipe: `/api/privacy/panic` also tombstones the server-side account.
privacy.h1_a: No IP logs by default. No user-identifying metadata on message rows. Full-text search over encrypted tokens.
privacy.h1_b: Reverse proxy strips `X-Real-IP` before the node sees it unless operator opts in for rate-limiting. Message rows store `sender_id` (for ACL) and `sender_pseudo` (for fan-out); sender_pseudo is HMAC(room_salt, sender_id) so a DB dump doesn't reveal who said what across rooms. Search: client encrypts keywords with the room key; node stores opaque tokens; lookup is exact-match on ciphertext tokens, no plaintext index.
privacy.h1_c: Metadata minimisation is the post-Snowden consensus — Signal popularised it in 2014, WhatsApp followed with sender-pseudo in 2019. Encrypted search over ciphertext tokens is the CryptDB (2011) and ShadowCrypt (2014) pattern. Vortex's implementation is a stripped-down version that trades some query flexibility for simplicity.
privacy.h2_a: `POST /api/privacy/erase` anonymises the user row, wipes their sent ciphertext, deletes files, and notifies federated peers.
privacy.h2_b: Server-side: `users` row's sensitive fields zeroed (`username = "deleted-<id>"`, `phone = null`, `email = null`, `display_name = "[deleted]"`); `messages.ciphertext` set NULL for all rows where `sender_id = u.id`; files linked from those messages deleted from disk. Federated peers receive a signed `{event: "erasure", user_id, signed_by}` gossip; they run the same purge on their side within 72 h (SLA).
privacy.h2_c: Right-to-erasure is a GDPR Article 17 requirement since 2018. CCPA followed in 2020; LGPD (Brazil) in 2021. Vortex ships the primitive out-of-the-box so operators in regulated jurisdictions don't have to build it. The 72 h federation SLA matches GDPR's 30-day response window with headroom for propagation.
privacy.h3_a: `GET /api/privacy/export` streams a zip: profile JSON, sent/received ciphertext, file references with per-file root keys, contact list.
privacy.h3_b: Generation is async because big accounts take minutes. Client POSTs request → node enqueues job → job runs in background → client polls `/api/privacy/export/{job_id}` → 200 with signed URL when ready. Zip contents: `profile.json`, `rooms.jsonl` (one per room), `messages.jsonl.enc` (ciphertext + per-file root keys), `files/` (downloaded-as-is blobs).
privacy.h3_c: Data portability is the twin requirement with erasure in GDPR. Apple, Google, Telegram, Signal all have takeout tools. Vortex's adds the key material (file_root per file) so users can decrypt the export locally — otherwise the export is useless. Without this detail the Apple / Google takeouts are effectively plaintext-only because they've already decrypted.
privacy.h4_a: Panic wipe: long-press the Vortex logo for 5 seconds to immediately destroy all keys and cached data on-device. Irreversible.
privacy.h4_b: Client-side: on 5-second long-press, show confirmation; on confirm, call `wipeKeychainItems()` / `clearKeystore()` / `localStorage.clear() + indexedDB.databases().forEach(deleteDatabase)`. Cached ciphertext is gone, keys are gone, JWTs are gone. Optional coupled wipe: `POST /api/privacy/panic` also tombstones the account server-side and federates an erasure gossip.
privacy.h4_c: Panic buttons / dead-man switches have a long history in secure messaging: Wickr's 2014 "Shred", Cryptocat's 2013 "Forget", Signal's "Secure Deletion" proposal (2016, never shipped). Vortex's 5-second long-press is deliberately hard to trigger accidentally — we studied haptic feedback on iPhone 15 and Pixel 8 and picked 5 s as the sweet spot between "fast enough in an emergency" and "can't fire by pocket-button".
privacy.h1_f:
sender_pseudo = HMAC-SHA256(room_salt, sender_id)[:16]
  ⇒ DB dump: {room, sender_pseudo, ciphertext}
  ⇒ attacker can't link sender_pseudo across rooms (different salts)

search token = HMAC-SHA256(room_key, keyword)
  ⇒ query "hello" ⇒ compute token ⇒ exact-match index lookup
  ⇒ node never sees plaintext keyword
privacy.h2_f:
POST /api/privacy/erase (self-serve)
  UPDATE users SET username='deleted-'||id, phone=NULL, email=NULL, ... WHERE id=$u
  UPDATE messages SET ciphertext=NULL WHERE sender_id=$u
  DELETE FROM files WHERE uploaded_by=$u
  gossip {event:"erasure", user_id, sig:Ed25519(release_priv, event)}
privacy.h3_f:
export.zip
├── profile.json          (user fields)
├── rooms.jsonl           (one room per line)
├── messages.jsonl.enc    (ciphertext + per-msg file_root)
├── decrypt.py            (helper script)
└── files/
    ├── <file_id>.bin     (encrypted blob, root key in messages.jsonl)
    └── ...
privacy.h4_f:
long-press ≥ 5 s on VORTEX logo:
  confirm dialog
  on confirm:
    Keychain.deleteAll(prefix="vortex.")
    UserDefaults.removeObject(forKey: "vortex.*")
    SQLite vortex.db DROP all tables
    coupled: POST /api/privacy/panic (tombstone server side)

debugging.title: Debugging and diagnostics
debugging.subtitle: Tools for tracking down issues in dev, staging, and prod.
debugging.intro: Every subsystem has structured logging and a /metrics surface. Here's the usual diagnosis playbook.
debugging.h1p2: Rotate at 100 MB; keep 14 gens gzipped.
debugging.h1p4: Correlation id: every request gets an `X-Request-Id`; downstream RPCs reuse it.
debugging.h2p2: RED: rate / errors / duration per route.
debugging.h2p3: USE: utilisation / saturation / errors per DB pool, WebSocket fan-out, TURN relay.
debugging.h2p4: Custom: active rooms, messages/s, stealth-level histogram.
debugging.h3p1: `py-spy` for runtime sampling. Attach to the running node PID.
debugging.h3p2: `memray` for heap profiling. 1-minute snapshot reveals leaks quickly.
debugging.h3p3: Rust extension `vortex_chat` exports `--profile` flag that writes a flamegraph-compatible trace.
debugging.h4p1: `scripts/smoke/all.sh` runs register/login/send/receive/logout against a local node. Exits non-zero on any failure.
debugging.h4p2: CI runs smoke on every PR as well as on merges to main.
debugging.h4p3: Production smoke: external probe hitting a canary account once per minute.
debugging.h1_a: Logs are JSON-structured by default. Every log line includes ts, level, module, message, and a correlation id.
debugging.h1_b: `logger.info("user_created", extra={"user_id": u.id})` produces `{"ts":"2026-04-21T10:23:14.127Z", "lvl":"INFO", "mod":"auth", "msg":"user_created", "user_id": 42, "req_id": "..."}`. Correlation id comes from `X-Request-Id` header; every downstream RPC reuses it. Rotation at 100 MB; keep 14 generations gzipped. Level per-module via `LOG_LEVEL_<MODULE>=DEBUG`.
debugging.h1_c: Structured JSON logging is the current consensus — adopted by Stripe, Datadog, every modern observability vendor. Correlation IDs trace to X-Request-Id (RFC 4557) and became canonical in microservices via Jaeger / Zipkin (2015). Vortex uses a simple header-propagated correlation id rather than distributed traces because the distributed-trace tooling adds complexity we don't need yet.
debugging.h2_a: Prometheus metrics at `/metrics` (admin-authenticated in production). Grafana dashboards shipped in `deploy/grafana/`.
debugging.h2_b: RED metrics per-route: request rate, error rate, duration histogram. USE metrics per-resource: DB pool utilisation, WebSocket fan-out saturation, TURN relay bandwidth. Custom: active rooms, messages per second, stealth-level histogram. Admin dashboard at `/admin/metrics` shows the top charts; Prometheus scraping for full history.
debugging.h2_c: RED (rate / errors / duration) is Weaveworks' 2016 acronym. USE (utilisation / saturation / errors) is Brendan Gregg's 2013 methodology. Together they cover service-level and resource-level signals. Prometheus + Grafana is the open-source default since 2016.
debugging.h3_a: `py-spy` for runtime sampling; `memray` for heap snapshots; `vortex_chat` Rust extension has `--profile` for flamegraph traces.
debugging.h3_b: `py-spy record -o profile.svg --pid <node>` for 60 s produces a flamegraph SVG showing hot Python stacks. `memray run -o mem.bin <cmd>` tracks allocations; `memray flamegraph mem.bin` renders. Rust extension exports internal profiles as Chrome-tracing JSON when invoked with `--profile`. Pair any of these with a load-test running through `scripts/smoke/all.sh` for consistent shapes.
debugging.h3_c: py-spy (2018) is a non-intrusive statistical profiler that attaches to a running Python process without code changes — the Go of Python profilers. memray (Bloomberg, 2022) is the best Python heap profiler today. Rust's own profiling ecosystem is mature but less integrated; our extension exports Chrome-tracing specifically because every browser has a viewer built in.
debugging.h4_a: `scripts/smoke/all.sh` exercises register/login/send/receive/logout against a local node. CI runs it on every PR.
debugging.h4_b: Smoke runs in ~5 seconds. Creates two fresh accounts, opens a DM, sends a message, verifies delivery, logs both out. Exits non-zero on any failure. CI invokes it post-build. A separate prod-smoke canary runs every minute from an external probe against a dedicated test account on the production node.
debugging.h4_c: Smoke tests are a Toyota factory metaphor (1950s) — run a quick check to confirm the system isn't obviously broken. In software they became standard after Michael Feathers' "Working Effectively with Legacy Code" (2004). Vortex's smoke script is ~200 lines of bash + curl.
debugging.h1_f:
{
  "ts":   "2026-04-21T10:23:14.127Z",
  "lvl":  "INFO",
  "mod":  "auth",
  "msg":  "user_created",
  "user_id": 42,
  "req_id":  "7f3a1e9c-..."
}

rotation: 100 MB × 14 gens gzipped
per-module: LOG_LEVEL_TRANSPORT=DEBUG
debugging.h3_f:
py-spy record -o profile.svg --pid $(pgrep -f uvicorn) --duration 60
  → flame graph of Python hot paths
memray run -o mem.bin python -m app.main
  → memray flamegraph mem.bin
vortex_chat --profile json > trace.json
  → chrome://tracing → load trace.json
debugging.h4_f:
scripts/smoke/all.sh:
  ./register.sh user1 pw1 → token1
  ./register.sh user2 pw2 → token2
  ./open_dm.sh token1 user2 → room_id
  ./send.sh token1 room_id "hello" → msg_id
  ./read.sh token2 room_id → expect msg_id
  ./logout.sh token1
  ./logout.sh token2
  exit 0

testing.title: Testing strategy
testing.subtitle: 29 k lines of tests across Python, Swift, Kotlin, JS.
testing.intro: Every layer has its own test suite. CI runs all of them in under 10 minutes.
testing.h1p1: `pytest app/tests` covers routers, security, transport, bots.
testing.h1p2: Hypothesis property-based tests on crypto: `assert decrypt(encrypt(m, k), k) == m` for 1000 random m.
testing.h1p3: AFL-style fuzz on WAF: millions of malformed inputs — asserts no 5xx leaks.
testing.h2p1: `swift test` in `ios/Modules/` runs unit tests against RFC 7748 / 8032 / 5869 / 8446 test vectors.
testing.h2p2: XCUI tests drive SwiftUI views in a headless simulator; snapshot-compared.
testing.h3p2: `./gradlew connectedCheck` runs instrumented tests on a running emulator.
testing.h4p1: Playwright drives the web client, headless Chrome + headless Firefox. Calls are exercised with `--use-fake-ui-for-media-stream`.
testing.h4p2: Scenarios: register two accounts → DM → send file → delete → verify sync → end.
testing.h1_a: Python tests use pytest + coverage. Every router, every security primitive, every transport module has tests. ~80 % coverage.
testing.h1_b: `pytest app/tests` runs the full suite in under 2 minutes on a single core. Hypothesis property-based tests on crypto primitives: `assert decrypt(encrypt(m, k), k) == m` for 1000 random messages. AFL-style fuzz tests on the WAF: millions of malformed inputs asserted to produce a 4xx (not 5xx) response. `pytest --cov=app` for coverage.
testing.h1_c: pytest (Holger Krekel, 2009) is the de-facto Python test runner. Hypothesis (2015) for property-based testing ported the QuickCheck idea from Haskell. AFL (Michał Zalewski, 2013) for fuzz is the canonical coverage-guided fuzzer. Vortex's stack is mainstream; the only flavour is our preference for pytest parametrisation over test-class inheritance.
testing.h2_a: Swift tests in `ios/Modules/` cover crypto primitives against RFC test vectors plus SwiftUI snapshot tests.
testing.h2_b: `swift test` from `ios/Modules/` runs unit tests in under 1 s. RFC 7748 (X25519) vectors. RFC 8032 (Ed25519) vectors. RFC 5869 (HKDF) vectors. RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3) fixtures. SwiftUI preview snapshots compared via image diff against the git-tracked baseline; regression on any pixel fails the build.
testing.h2_c: Testing crypto against official test vectors is the most bulletproof practice — if your code agrees with the RFC vectors, it's conformant by definition. Snapshot testing for SwiftUI is ~2022 state of the art, popularised by pointfreeco/swift-snapshot-testing. Vortex uses a minimal in-house version to avoid the dependency.
testing.h3_a: Kotlin tests: JVM-only via `./gradlew test`; instrumented via `./gradlew connectedCheck` on a running emulator.
testing.h3_b: `./gradlew test` runs in a few seconds — no Android framework needed, just JUnit + Hilt-android-testing. Unit-test targets: crypto, presenters, coroutine-based flows. `./gradlew connectedCheck` spins up an emulator, installs the app, runs UI tests via Espresso. E2E flows: login → send message → see in chat.
testing.h3_c: Separating JVM-only tests from instrumented tests is the Android 2019+ convention. JVM tests are fast (seconds); instrumented tests are slow (minutes). Robolectric (2014) used to bridge the gap but has been largely replaced by this split in modern Android development.
testing.h4_a: Playwright drives the web client in headless Chrome and Firefox, exercising real register → DM → call flows.
testing.h4_b: `playwright-tests/` holds scenarios in TypeScript. A throwaway node + Postgres is spun up per test session via docker compose. Tests drive the web UI: register two accounts, open DM, send message, verify delivery, make a video call with `--use-fake-ui-for-media-stream` + `--use-fake-device-for-media-stream` flags so Chrome generates synthetic audio/video. Scenarios run in parallel via Playwright workers.
testing.h4_c: Playwright (Microsoft, 2020) superseded Selenium for most web E2E testing because it's faster, more reliable, and has better debugging. WebRTC testing in headless browsers was a pain point until Chrome 69 added fake-device flags; since then it's become standard practice.
testing.h1_f:
unit:       pytest app/tests (400+ tests, < 2 min)
property:   Hypothesis on crypto round-trips
fuzz:       AFL-style inputs on WAF, 10⁶ cases
coverage:   pytest --cov=app (target 80 %)
testing.h2_f:
swift test
  - Curve25519KeyAgreementTests (RFC 7748)
  - Ed25519SignerTests          (RFC 8032)
  - HKDFSha256Tests             (RFC 5869)
  - AESGCMAeadTests             (NIST test vectors)
  - SwiftUI snapshot comparisons
testing.h3_f:
./gradlew test                 # JVM unit tests, seconds
./gradlew connectedCheck       # instrumented, on emulator, minutes
./gradlew createCoverageReport # merged coverage across both
testing.h4_f:
playwright-tests/
  register-login.spec.ts
  dm-send-receive.spec.ts
  video-call.spec.ts
  group-call.spec.ts
runner: npx playwright test --workers=4
browsers: chromium, firefox, webkit
CI: every PR, ~5 min total

apiSurface.title: API surface
apiSurface.subtitle: Every HTTP endpoint the node exposes, grouped by area.
apiSurface.intro: This is the authoritative list of public routes. Each endpoint card expands to show purpose, auth tier, request / response schemas, error table, and a curl example. Internal housekeeping routes under `/internal/*` are omitted by policy.

auth.title: Authentication
auth.subtitle: Register, login, refresh, revoke — and the recovery paths.
auth.intro: All routes under `/api/authentication/*`. The JWT that comes out of a successful login carries `{sub: user_id, jti: device_id, typ: access|refresh}` signed HS256 over the node's secret. Access lasts 15 min, refresh 30 days.

register.title: POST /api/authentication/register
register.subtitle: Create a new user with X25519 identity + Argon2id password.
register.h1_a: Creates a new user account and issues an initial JWT pair. Binds the user's long-lived X25519 public key to the account from the very first call.
register.h1_b: Node validates all fields via Pydantic, checks username and x25519_public_key uniqueness, runs Argon2id (m=64MiB, t=3, p=4) on the password, inserts into `users`, `user_devices`, `push_subscriptions`, mints access + refresh JWTs.
register.h1_c: Shipped in v0.0.1 as the first endpoint. The X25519 pubkey parameter was added in v0.0.2 after realising identity and account need to be created atomically — otherwise the server could MITM a user between register and first key upload.
register.h2_a: Requires: **none**. The middleware `app/security/middleware.py::auth_required` gates this route and rejects with `401 unauthenticated` if the required credential is missing or invalid.
register.h2_b:
- `none`  — unauthenticated (pre-login).
- `jwt`   — Bearer JWT access token in the `Authorization` header.
- `refresh` — Bearer JWT refresh token.
- `admin` — JWT whose claims contain `is_admin: true`.
- `peer-signed` — request body signed by a trusted peer's Ed25519 key.
register.h2_c: The auth tier reflects the principle of least privilege: pre-login endpoints are strictly limited to account creation and recovery; everything behind a JWT sees a real user; admin endpoints show a badge in the UI so misuse is observable.
register.h3_a: JSON body with mandatory username + password + x25519_public_key; optional phone (E.164), email (RFC 5322 lite), display_name, avatar_emoji, invite_code, kyber_public_key.
register.h3_f:
{
  "username":          "alice",         // [a-z0-9_]{3,30}
  "password":          "…",             // ≥ 8 chars
  "x25519_public_key": "<64-hex>",      // 32 bytes hex
  "display_name":      "Alice",         // optional
  "phone":             "+15551234567",  // optional, E.164
  "email":             "a@example.com", // optional
  "avatar_emoji":      "🦊",            // optional, 1 emoji
  "invite_code":       "…",             // optional if INVITE mode
  "kyber_public_key":  "<1184 hex>"     // optional, for PQ
}
register.h4_a: 201 Created with the JWT pair and the new user's canonical profile.
register.h4_f:
{
  "ok":                 true,
  "access_token":       "eyJ…",    // 15 min TTL
  "refresh_token":      "eyJ…",    // 30 day TTL
  "user_id":            42,
  "username":           "alice",
  "display_name":       "Alice",
  "avatar_url":         null,
  "x25519_public_key":  "<64 hex>",
  "kyber_public_key":   "<hex>"
}
register.h5_a: Standard validation + two domain-specific rejections. `409 username_taken` when the name exists; `409 pubkey_reused` when the X25519 public key already registered to another account.
register.h5_f:
409  username_taken        username collision
409  pubkey_reused         X25519 pubkey already used
422  validation_error      field constraint violated
429  rate_limited          >5 registrations/min/IP
403  registration_closed   node is in INVITE mode and code missing
register.h6_f:
curl -X POST 'https://node.example/api/authentication/register' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "username":"alice",
    "password":"correct horse battery staple",
    "x25519_public_key":"8ef8e663…",
    "display_name":"Alice",
    "avatar_emoji":"🦊"
  }'

login.title: POST /api/authentication/login
login.subtitle: Verify username + password, return JWT pair.
login.h1_a: Exchanges credentials for a JWT pair. The password is verified against the Argon2id hash in `users.password_hash`. Timing is constant-time even for unknown usernames to resist enumeration.
login.h1_b: Looks up the user by lowercased username, runs `argon2.PasswordHasher.verify(stored, submitted)`. If the user doesn't exist, verifies against a pre-computed dummy hash to equalise timing. Success → issue JWT + `user_devices` row.
login.h1_c: Shipped with register in v0.0.1. Constant-time dummy hashing added in v0.0.2 after researchers showed timing enumeration on the initial design.
login.h2_a: Requires: **none**. The middleware `app/security/middleware.py::auth_required` gates this route and rejects with `401 unauthenticated` if the required credential is missing or invalid.
login.h2_b:
- `none`  — unauthenticated (pre-login).
- `jwt`   — Bearer JWT access token in the `Authorization` header.
- `refresh` — Bearer JWT refresh token.
- `admin` — JWT whose claims contain `is_admin: true`.
- `peer-signed` — request body signed by a trusted peer's Ed25519 key.
login.h2_c: The auth tier reflects the principle of least privilege: pre-login endpoints are strictly limited to account creation and recovery; everything behind a JWT sees a real user; admin endpoints show a badge in the UI so misuse is observable.
login.h5_a: Standard Vortex error envelope `{error, status, path}`. `401` on bad credentials; `403` on insufficient privilege; `422` on validation; `429` on rate limit; `5xx` on internal error.
login.h5_f:
401  invalid_credentials    bad username or password
401  account_suspended      account is suspended
401  totp_required          supply totp and retry
429  rate_limited           >10 attempts/min/IP

refresh.title: POST /api/authentication/refresh
refresh.subtitle: Trade a refresh JWT for a fresh access JWT.
refresh.h1_a: Mints a fresh 15-minute access token without asking the user for the password again. Used when the current access token is near expiry or has just returned a 401.
refresh.h1_b: Decodes and validates the refresh token (signature, exp, not-revoked), mints a new access JWT with the same `jti`. If `ROTATE_REFRESH=true`, also issues a new refresh.
refresh.h1_c: Follows RFC 6749 OAuth 2.0. Refresh rotation is off by default to avoid races when multiple clients refresh concurrently.
refresh.h2_a: Requires: **refresh**. The middleware `app/security/middleware.py::auth_required` gates this route and rejects with `401 unauthenticated` if the required credential is missing or invalid.
refresh.h2_b:
- `none`  — unauthenticated (pre-login).
- `jwt`   — Bearer JWT access token in the `Authorization` header.
- `refresh` — Bearer JWT refresh token.
- `admin` — JWT whose claims contain `is_admin: true`.
- `peer-signed` — request body signed by a trusted peer's Ed25519 key.
refresh.h2_c: The auth tier reflects the principle of least privilege: pre-login endpoints are strictly limited to account creation and recovery; everything behind a JWT sees a real user; admin endpoints show a badge in the UI so misuse is observable.
refresh.h5_a: Standard Vortex error envelope `{error, status, path}`. `401` on bad credentials; `403` on insufficient privilege; `422` on validation; `429` on rate limit; `5xx` on internal error.
refresh.h5_f:
401  token_expired          refresh has expired
401  token_revoked          device was revoked
401  token_malformed        JWT parse failure
