peersAdd.h5_a: Standard Vortex error envelope `{error, status, path}`. `401` on bad credentials; `403` on insufficient privilege; `422` on validation; `429` on rate limit; `5xx` on internal error.
peersAdd.h5_f:
401  unauthenticated        missing/invalid token
403  forbidden              insufficient privilege
422  validation_error       field constraint violated
429  rate_limited           too many attempts
500  internal_error         unexpected server fault

peersRemove.title: DELETE /api/admin/peers/{pubkey}
peersRemove.subtitle: Remove trusted peer
peersRemove.h1_a: Soft-delete (`revoked_at = NOW()`). Federated traffic from that pubkey is rejected.
peersRemove.h1_b: Handler for this route lives in the corresponding module under `app/`. The path pattern `/api/admin/peers/{pubkey}` is declared with `@router.delete('/api/admin/peers/{pubkey}')`.
peersRemove.h1_c: Shipped in an early Vortex release as part of the `{pubkey}` capability.
peersRemove.h2_a: Requires: **admin**. The middleware `app/security/middleware.py::auth_required` gates this route and rejects with `401 unauthenticated` if the required credential is missing or invalid.
peersRemove.h2_b:
- `none`  — unauthenticated (pre-login).
- `jwt`   — Bearer JWT access token in the `Authorization` header.
- `refresh` — Bearer JWT refresh token.
- `admin` — JWT whose claims contain `is_admin: true`.
- `peer-signed` — request body signed by a trusted peer's Ed25519 key.

bmp.title: BMP

bmp.intro: Two routes only. Deposit is public; pickup requires the recipient's JWT. Mailbox id is a one-way hash of the recipient pubkey so middle nodes can't identify recipients.

roadmap.title: Roadmap
roadmap.subtitle: What's planned and when.
roadmap.intro: High-level view. Dates are aspirational; security issues pre-empt feature work.
roadmap.h1p1: Kyber-768 PQ enabled by default on fresh installs.
roadmap.h1p2: Groups up to 50 000 members with Variant-B key publish.
roadmap.h1p3: Desktop (Tauri) packaging for macOS / Windows / Linux.
roadmap.h1p4: Bot marketplace opens to public submissions.
roadmap.h2p1: Audio-only group calls up to 100 participants via SFU.
roadmap.h2p3: Per-participant mute / kick in the call UI.
roadmap.h3p1: Miro-style collaborative canvas over CRDT (Yjs-flavoured) through the messaging layer.
roadmap.h4p1: On-chain escrow for bot purchases (Solana + EVM).
roadmap.h4p3: Fiat on/off ramp via Stripe connect.
roadmap.h5p1: Above stabilised, formal security audit, reproducible builds, SemVer frozen.
roadmap.h5p3: At least one independent security review with public write-up.
roadmap.h1_a: v0.2 — second public release. Kyber PQ on by default, bigger public rooms via Variant-B, Tauri desktop packages, open bot marketplace.
roadmap.h1_b: Kyber-768 defaults to enabled for fresh installs; clients without support gracefully downgrade. Rooms up to 50 000 members via Variant-B key publish — no per-member envelope fanout on rotation. Desktop (Tauri 2 wrapper around the web client) published for macOS, Windows, Linux with the same bundle. Bot marketplace opens to public submissions with a 3-business-day review SLA.
roadmap.h1_c: v0.2 is about scaling out the existing primitives rather than inventing new ones. Kyber was always on the roadmap; the switch to default depends on stability under load. Tauri was chosen over Electron because the bundle is ~5 MB vs ~150 MB. Bot marketplace public submissions were blocked on review infrastructure which landed in v0.1.
roadmap.h2_a: v0.3 — group calls. SFU-based audio groups up to 100; video groups up to 32; adaptive simulcast.
roadmap.h2_b: SFU implemented in Rust for performance. Opus DTX + FEC + simulcast for audio. VP9 L1/L2/L3 simulcast for video with the SFU picking per-viewer layer based on reported downlink. Per-participant mute and kick from the UI. Call recording (consent-gated, off by default) stored encrypted under a per-call root key shared among participants.
roadmap.h2_c: Group calls are the single most-requested feature from the v0.1 beta. SFU design follows Jitsi Videobridge + Janus patterns. Rust implementation instead of reusing mediasoup because mediasoup's Node.js runtime adds latency we don't want on the hot path.
roadmap.h3_a: v0.4 — collaborative canvas. Miro-style shared board over CRDT (Yjs-flavoured) through the existing messaging layer.
roadmap.h3_b: CRDT document sync piggybacks on existing room messages. Each board edit is a CRDT operation serialised as CBOR, encrypted with the room key, posted as a message of `kind:"board_op"`. Clients deserialise, apply, render. Real-time cursor presence via a separate `kind:"board_cursor"` message type. Export to PNG / SVG / PDF via client-side canvas rendering.
roadmap.h3_c: CRDTs (Shapiro et al. 2011) became production-viable with Yjs (Kevin Jahns, 2017). Figma famously built their collaboration on a custom CRDT-like structure. The idea to reuse our encrypted message layer rather than building separate WebSocket channels comes from a principle: fewer transport paths, fewer bugs.
roadmap.h4_a: v0.5 — payments. On-chain escrow (Solana + EVM) + fiat on/off ramp via Stripe Connect.
roadmap.h4_b: Bot purchases route through an on-chain escrow contract. Buyer approves; 24 h holdback; then funds release to seller minus 10 % platform fee. Disputes go to community arbiters elected via staking. Fiat on-ramp: Stripe Connect accounts per operator or per bot seller; off-ramp: direct bank transfer or USDC settlement. Crypto support: SOL, USDC, USDT on both Solana and Ethereum.
roadmap.h4_c: Escrow smart contracts are old (OpenBazaar 2016) but still rough around the edges in terms of UX. Vortex inherits the complexity but hides most of it — buyers see a familiar "buy" button. The 10 % platform fee matches App Store conventions (though Apple takes 30 %); the lower cut is deliberate to attract sellers.
roadmap.h5_a: v1.0 — stability. Security audit, reproducible builds across all platforms, SemVer frozen.
roadmap.h5_b: Third-party audit scheduled pre-1.0 freeze — likely Trail of Bits or NCC Group based on commercial quotes. Reproducible builds mean the same source tree produces bit-identical binaries on any two build machines; this detects supply-chain tampering. SemVer frozen means the wire protocols, storage schema, and public SDK surface get backward-compat guarantees within 1.x. Bug bounty program launches with a $25k top tier for remote code execution on the node.
roadmap.h5_c: The stabilise-audit-freeze sequence is the pattern Signal, Tor, I2P, and WireGuard all followed. Reproducible builds became mainstream after the Reproducible Builds project (2014). Vortex uses the Reproducible Builds toolkit. Bug bounties are standard for any project handling user data; $25k is mid-tier by 2025 standards.
roadmap.h1_f:
v0.2 delivery (planned):
  PQ hybrid default  : Kyber-768 on by default
  large rooms        : Variant-B published key, up to 50k members
  desktop            : Tauri 2 for macOS / Windows / Linux (~5 MB)
  bot marketplace    : public submissions, 3-day review SLA
roadmap.h2_f:
v0.3 delivery (planned):
  SFU engine         : Rust, reuses libwebrtc
  audio groups       : ≤ 100 participants, Opus simulcast
  video groups       : ≤ 32 participants, VP9 L1/L2/L3 simulcast
  recording          : opt-in, E2E-encrypted under call_root key
roadmap.h3_f:
v0.4 delivery (planned):
  canvas            : Yjs-flavoured CRDT, piggyback on room messages
  operation format  : CBOR{v:1, op_type, target, payload}
  cursor presence   : kind="board_cursor", throttled to 10 Hz
  export            : client-side PNG / SVG / PDF
roadmap.h4_f:
v0.5 delivery (planned):
  escrow            : Solana program + EVM contract, 24 h holdback
  platform fee      : 10 % of gross
  fiat              : Stripe Connect per seller
  crypto            : SOL, USDC, USDT on Solana + Ethereum
  dispute           : community arbiters, stake-weighted vote
roadmap.h5_f:
v1.0 delivery (planned):
  security audit     : Trail of Bits or NCC (Q3)
  reproducible builds: source SHA256 → deterministic binary SHA256
  SemVer freeze      : wire protocol and SDK API stable within 1.x
  bug bounty         : $25k max tier for RCE on node

glossary.title: Glossary
glossary.subtitle: Every term used in the Vortex docs, spelled out.
glossary.intro: Alphabetical reference. Each term card expands into a full definition, where it is used, its history, and a formula or example when applicable.

aead.title: AEAD
aead.subtitle: Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data.
aead.intro: AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) is a cryptographic primitive that combines confidentiality and integrity in one call. Given a key, nonce, plaintext, and optional associated data (AAD), it produces ciphertext + authentication tag such that any tampering with either plaintext, ciphertext, or AAD fails verification.
aead.h1_a: AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) is a cryptographic primitive that combines confidentiality and integrity in one call. Given a key, nonce, plaintext, and optional associated data (AAD), it produces ciphertext + authentication tag such that any tampering with either plaintext, ciphertext, or AAD fails verification.
aead.h1_b: Vortex's AEAD is always AES-256-GCM. The AAD slot carries context the recipient must know to decrypt correctly (room id, file id, offset). Binding AAD prevents cross-context replay — a ciphertext from room A can't be decrypted as room B.
aead.h1_c: AEAD as a named primitive traces to Rogaway 2002. GCM (Galois/Counter Mode) was standardised by NIST in SP 800-38D (2007). Became the default AEAD in TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446, 2018). Every modern secure protocol uses an AEAD.
aead.h1_f:
AEAD.Enc(k, n, pt, aad) = (ct, tag)     // ct = len(pt) bytes, tag = 16 bytes
AEAD.Dec(k, n, ct, tag, aad) = pt  or  ⊥

alembic.title: Alembic
alembic.subtitle: Database migration tool for SQLAlchemy.
alembic.intro: Alembic is the canonical migration framework for SQLAlchemy. Each migration is a Python file with `upgrade()` and `downgrade()` functions that describe schema changes as SQL or ORM operations.
alembic.h1_a: Alembic is the canonical migration framework for SQLAlchemy. Each migration is a Python file with `upgrade()` and `downgrade()` functions that describe schema changes as SQL or ORM operations.
alembic.h1_b: Vortex uses Alembic with linear revision history (no branching). `alembic upgrade head` runs on every Docker container start. `alembic downgrade -1` reverts one step for rollback. Dev mode uses `create_all + ALTER TABLE fallback` so local iteration doesn't need migrations on every rebase.
alembic.h1_c: Alembic was written by Mike Bayer (author of SQLAlchemy) in 2011 as the successor to `sqlalchemy-migrate`. Django's built-in migrations (2014) borrowed the core model. Alembic stayed ahead on features — rollback, autogenerate, branching — though Vortex doesn't use branches.

apns.title: APNs
apns.subtitle: Apple Push Notification service.
apns.intro: APNs is Apple's push-notification service for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS. Apps register a device token and providers send notification payloads to APNs which fans them out to devices.
apns.h1_a: APNs is Apple's push-notification service for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS. Apps register a device token and providers send notification payloads to APNs which fans them out to devices.
apns.h1_b: Vortex uses APNs with sealed-push envelopes: the node encrypts each notification payload for the recipient's X25519 pubkey; APNs sees only an opaque blob. The iOS client's Notification Service Extension decrypts in a separate process before the banner renders.
apns.h1_c: APNs launched with iOS 3.0 (2009). Sealed push / mutable content support came with iOS 10 (2016). Token-based auth (.p8) replaced certificate-based auth as the default in 2016. Vortex uses token auth with 60-minute provider tokens.
apns.h3_a: FCM, Web Push, sealed push, Notification Service Extension

argon2id.title: Argon2id
argon2id.subtitle: Memory-hard password hash (RFC 9106).
argon2id.intro: Argon2id is a memory-hard, side-channel-resistant password hashing function. Parameters: memory size (m), time cost (iterations, t), parallelism (lanes, p), and hash length. The 'id' variant mixes data-independent (Argon2i) and data-dependent (Argon2d) passes.
argon2id.h1_a: Argon2id is a memory-hard, side-channel-resistant password hashing function. Parameters: memory size (m), time cost (iterations, t), parallelism (lanes, p), and hash length. The 'id' variant mixes data-independent (Argon2i) and data-dependent (Argon2d) passes.
argon2id.h1_b: Vortex uses Argon2id with m=64MiB, t=3, p=4, hashLen=32. This takes ~0.5 s on an iPhone X (our slowest supported device) and roughly 5 ms on a high-end ASIC — the 100× speedup is negligible compared to SHA-256-based hashes where ASICs win by millions.
argon2id.h1_c: Argon2 won the 2015 Password Hashing Competition organised by Dmitry Khovratovich and Alex Biryukov. Standardised as RFC 9106 in 2021. Used by 1Password, Bitwarden, LastPass. Vortex's parameters come from OWASP 2023 recommendations.
argon2id.h1_f:
Argon2id(password, salt, m, t, p, hashLen) → hash
default: m=64MiB, t=3, p=4, hashLen=32
cost ≈ 0.5s on iPhone X; ~5ms on ASIC (100× attacker speedup)

blake3.title: BLAKE3
blake3.subtitle: Fast cryptographic hash.
blake3.intro: BLAKE3 is a cryptographic hash function descended from BLAKE2 and BLAKE. Produces arbitrary-length output from arbitrary input; 10× faster than SHA-256 on large blobs thanks to internal tree hashing.
blake3.h1_a: BLAKE3 is a cryptographic hash function descended from BLAKE2 and BLAKE. Produces arbitrary-length output from arbitrary input; 10× faster than SHA-256 on large blobs thanks to internal tree hashing.
blake3.h1_b: Vortex uses BLAKE3 for file integrity: each uploaded file carries a `plain_blake3` hash of its decrypted content so recipients can verify after download + decrypt. The DB column is historically named `sha256` for migration compatibility but contains BLAKE3 output.
blake3.h1_c: BLAKE3 was released by Jack O'Connor et al. in 2020. Descended from BLAKE, which was a SHA-3 finalist in 2012. The tree-hashing design lets BLAKE3 scale with cores for huge inputs. SHA-256 is still faster for small inputs; BLAKE3 dominates on anything > 4 KiB.
blake3.h1_f:
BLAKE3(bytes) = 32 bytes   // default output
BLAKE3(bytes, len) = len bytes (XOF)
throughput: ~3 GB/s on Apple Silicon vs SHA-256 ~500 MB/s

bmp.title: BMP
bmp.subtitle: Blind Mailbox Protocol.
bmp.intro: BMP is Vortex's anonymous store-and-forward layer. Senders deposit encrypted blobs at any gossip-enabled node, keyed by a one-way hash of the recipient's pubkey. Blobs ride gossip until the recipient's node pulls.
bmp.h1_a: BMP is Vortex's anonymous store-and-forward layer. Senders deposit encrypted blobs at any gossip-enabled node, keyed by a one-way hash of the recipient's pubkey. Blobs ride gossip until the recipient's node pulls.
bmp.h1_b: BMP provides three independent privacy properties: sender-hiding (TLS + optional onion), recipient-hiding (mailbox_id is opaque to middle nodes), content-hiding (blob is already E2E-encrypted). Max blob 1 MB, per-node cap 500 MB, deposit rate 10/min/IP, TTL 7200 s.
bmp.h1_c: BMP was designed from scratch by the Vortex team. We built it because no mainstream messenger was willing to pay the engineering cost of hiding metadata end-to-end — they encrypt content but leak the social graph. Our position from day one was that a secure messenger that leaks who-talks-to-whom is only half secure. BMP closes that half by making sender, recipient, and content independently opaque, on top of Vortex's own gossip mesh rather than bolted-on infrastructure.
bmp.h3_a: gossip, onion service, sealed sender, Pond

cbor.title: CBOR
cbor.subtitle: Concise Binary Object Representation.
cbor.intro: CBOR (RFC 8949) is a binary data format designed for small code size and fast encoding. Superset of JSON's data model, binary-encoded, strictly deterministic.
cbor.h1_a: CBOR (RFC 8949) is a binary data format designed for small code size and fast encoding. Superset of JSON's data model, binary-encoded, strictly deterministic.
cbor.h1_b: Vortex uses CBOR as the payload format inside AES-GCM envelopes. ~30 % smaller than JSON on unicode-heavy chat; strict parsing (no whitespace ambiguity) resists parser-differential attacks; schema-free so adding a field doesn't require a .proto recompilation.
cbor.h1_c: CBOR was standardised as RFC 7049 in 2013, updated to RFC 8949 in 2020. Used by Signal (internal framing since 2017), Matrix's state-resolution layer, WebAuthn (attestation objects). Picked by Vortex in v0.0.2 after comparing against JSON and protobuf.
cbor.h1_f:
CBOR encoding: {major_type (3 bits), argument (5 bits), [data]}
map:    type=5   pairs
array:  type=4   elements
text:   type=3   bytes
bytes:  type=2   raw
cbor.h2_a: Every message envelope, every sealed-push payload, every federation envelope.

controller.title: Controller
controller.subtitle: Vortex's attestation + discovery service.
controller.intro: The controller is a separate Python process (`vortex_controller`) whose only job is publishing the signed manifest (`INTEGRITY.sig.json`) and entry URLs. Clients pin the controller's Ed25519 pubkey at release time.
controller.h1_a: The controller is a separate Python process (`vortex_controller`) whose only job is publishing the signed manifest (`INTEGRITY.sig.json`) and entry URLs. Clients pin the controller's Ed25519 pubkey at release time.
controller.h1_b: Exposes a tiny API surface (~12 routes). The narrow surface is deliberate — it's the highest-value attack target once clients pin. Mirrors run the same code with the same release key; DNS/SNS/IPFS publish lists of mirrors.
controller.h1_c: Introduced in v0.0.3 after the early monolith version tied node attestation to the node itself (a compromised node could lie about its own code). Splitting attestation into a separate process modeled after Debian's Release.gpg + Chrome's root store.
controller.h3_a: integrity manifest, release key, mirror, trusted_nodes

doubleRatchet.title: Double Ratchet
doubleRatchet.subtitle: Signal's per-DM forward-secrecy protocol.
doubleRatchet.intro: The Double Ratchet combines a Diffie-Hellman ratchet with a symmetric-key chain. Each message advances its chain; periodic DH ratchets re-key the root. Delivers forward secrecy (past messages safe after compromise) and post-compromise security (future messages safe after re-ratchet).
doubleRatchet.h1_a: The Double Ratchet combines a Diffie-Hellman ratchet with a symmetric-key chain. Each message advances its chain; periodic DH ratchets re-key the root. Delivers forward secrecy (past messages safe after compromise) and post-compromise security (future messages safe after re-ratchet).
doubleRatchet.h1_b: Vortex uses Double Ratchet for direct-message chains. Group rooms use a simpler sender-key scheme keyed off the room root. Chain length capped at 1000 messages before forced re-ratchet; skipped-key cache up to 1000 out-of-order messages; header encryption on by default.
doubleRatchet.h1_c: Designed by Trevor Perrin and Moxie Marlinspike in 2013; deployed in Signal 2014; adopted by WhatsApp (2016), Facebook Secret Conversations, Skype Private Conversations. Public spec 2016. Vortex's implementation is a direct port.
doubleRatchet.h1_f:
sending chain:    CK_send → HKDF(CK_send, "step") → CK_send'
                    MK         → HKDF(CK_send, "msg")
receiving chain:  CK_recv → HKDF(CK_recv, "step") → CK_recv'
DH ratchet:       RK, CK = HKDF(DH(our_eph, their_eph), RK_prev)

ech.title: ECH
ech.subtitle: Encrypted Client Hello (RFC 9460).
ech.intro: ECH hides the SNI (Server Name Indication) on TLS 1.3 by wrapping the inner ClientHello inside an outer ClientHello encrypted under a public key published in the DNS HTTPS record.
ech.h1_a: ECH hides the SNI (Server Name Indication) on TLS 1.3 by wrapping the inner ClientHello inside an outer ClientHello encrypted under a public key published in the DNS HTTPS record.
ech.h1_b: Vortex nodes publish ECHConfigs in their DNS HTTPS records. Clients with ECH support use the outer hostname (e.g. `vortex-public.cloudflare.com`) as the visible SNI; passive observers see only that generic outer name. DPI can't tell a Vortex client from any other Cloudflare customer.
ech.h1_c: ECH went through 5 IETF drafts before standardisation as RFC 9460 in 2024. Earlier variants: ESNI (2018-2019, abandoned), ECH draft-00 to draft-13 (2020-2024). Chrome enabled ECH by default in 2023; Firefox in 2024. Vortex supports it since v0.0.9.
ech.h1_f:
outer SNI:  "public.cloudflare.com"  ← visible to observers
inner SNI:  "node.vortex.example"   ← encrypted
ECHConfig:  published in DNS HTTPS record of the outer domain

ed25519.title: Ed25519
ed25519.subtitle: EdDSA signature over edwards25519 (RFC 8032).
ed25519.intro: Ed25519 is an elliptic-curve signature scheme. 32-byte public keys, 64-byte signatures, deterministic signing by default. Fast verification (~200 µs on a modern CPU), batch-verifiable.
ed25519.h1_a: Ed25519 is an elliptic-curve signature scheme. 32-byte public keys, 64-byte signatures, deterministic signing by default. Fast verification (~200 µs on a modern CPU), batch-verifiable.
ed25519.h1_b: Vortex signs every federation envelope, every integrity manifest, every node attestation, and every bot message with Ed25519. Apple's CryptoKit on iOS 17+ uses the randomised RFC 8032bis variant; Vortex accepts either — any compliant verifier handles both.
ed25519.h1_c: Ed25519 was published by Bernstein, Duif, Lange, Schwabe, Yang in 2011. Standardised as RFC 8032 in 2017. Adopted by OpenSSH 6.5 (2014), TLS 1.3, Apple Keychain, signal-protocol. Deterministic signing was explicitly chosen over ECDSA to avoid the ECDSA RNG-reuse vulnerability class.
ed25519.h1_f:
Sign(d, m):   r = SHA-512(d ∥ m)
              R = r · G
              c = SHA-512(R ∥ A ∥ m)
              S = r + c · a
              return (R, S)
Verify: c = SHA-512(R ∥ A ∥ m);  R + c·A ≟ S·G

fanout.title: Fan-out
fanout.subtitle: Broadcasting an event from one source to many subscribers.
fanout.intro: Fan-out is the architectural pattern where one producer emits an event and N consumers receive it. In Vortex's WebSocket layer, one message POST triggers N socket sends — one per subscriber in the room.
fanout.h1_a: Fan-out is the architectural pattern where one producer emits an event and N consumers receive it. In Vortex's WebSocket layer, one message POST triggers N socket sends — one per subscriber in the room.
fanout.h1_b: Implemented as a per-room set of WebSocket handles guarded by an asyncio.Lock. Write acquires, copies the set, releases, then iterates copy awaiting `ws.send_bytes(payload)` with per-socket 1 s timeout. Slow consumers have a 256-frame queue before frames drop.
fanout.h1_c: Fan-out goes back to publish/subscribe systems in the 1980s (POP, TIBCO). Modern designs (Kafka, Redis pub/sub, NATS) scale the pattern to millions of subscribers. Vortex's per-room in-memory fan-out is minimal because it doesn't need cross-node replication in single-node mode; Redis pub/sub activates in multi-node clusters.
fanout.h1_f:
broadcast(room_id, payload):
  subs = fanout[room_id]         # O(1) dict lookup
  for ws in snapshot(subs):      # copy to iterate safely
      await ws.send_bytes(payload, timeout=1s)
fanout.h2_a: `app/chats/fanout.py`, the hottest path for every message, reaction, typing event, call signal.

fcm.title: FCM
fcm.subtitle: Firebase Cloud Messaging.
fcm.intro: FCM is Google's push-notification service for Android, iOS, web. Replaced the older Google Cloud Messaging (GCM) in 2016. Accepts OAuth2 service-account auth.
fcm.h1_a: FCM is Google's push-notification service for Android, iOS, web. Replaced the older Google Cloud Messaging (GCM) in 2016. Accepts OAuth2 service-account auth.
fcm.h1_b: Vortex uses FCM in `data` mode (not `notification` mode) so the app's FirebaseMessagingService runs on arrival and decrypts the sealed-push payload before deciding whether to show a banner. Silent-delivery wake-ups also use FCM data messages.
fcm.h1_c: FCM launched in 2016 replacing GCM. The legacy HTTP endpoint was deprecated in 2024; Vortex uses the HTTP v1 endpoint with OAuth2. Token rotation every hour is automated via bundled service-account credentials.
fcm.h3_a: APNs, Web Push, sealed push, OAuth2

federation.title: Federation
federation.subtitle: Mutual-trust message delivery between independent Vortex nodes.
federation.intro: Federation is a mutual-trust link between two node operators. Each adds the other's Ed25519 pubkey to its `federations` table. Users on node A can message users on node B by routing envelopes through a signed `POST /federation/deliver`.
federation.h1_a: Federation is a mutual-trust link between two node operators. Each adds the other's Ed25519 pubkey to its `federations` table. Users on node A can message users on node B by routing envelopes through a signed `POST /federation/deliver`.
federation.h1_b: Opt-in per operator: there is no "any node can federate" default. Adding a peer requires an operator-signed admin action. Health scores (AIMD ±1) pause federation to misbehaving peers. Outbox retries failed deliveries with exponential backoff up to 14 days.
federation.h1_c: Federation was Matrix's killer feature (2014) and before that XMPP/Jabber (1999). Vortex's design draws from both: signed envelopes (Matrix), explicit trust anchors (neither — we're stricter), health scores (AIMD from TCP). Added in v0.0.5.

fts5.title: FTS5
fts5.subtitle: SQLite's full-text-search virtual table.
fts5.intro: FTS5 is SQLite's fifth-generation full-text search extension. Creates a virtual table that stores tokenised indexes for fast LIKE / MATCH queries.
fts5.h1_a: FTS5 is SQLite's fifth-generation full-text search extension. Creates a virtual table that stores tokenised indexes for fast LIKE / MATCH queries.
fts5.h1_b: Vortex mirrors plaintext tokens (client-encrypted as opaque tags) into an FTS5 virtual table so search hits `O(log n)` instead of `O(n)` scans. On Android's Room, FTS4 is used with triggers that keep the virtual table in sync. Postgres deployments use `tsvector` / `tsquery` for the same purpose.
fts5.h1_c: FTS goes back to SQLite 3.7 (2010). FTS5 shipped in SQLite 3.9 (2015). Widely adopted; every local-first messaging app on iOS / Android uses it. Postgres has had `tsvector` since 8.3 (2008).

gossip.title: Gossip
gossip.subtitle: Periodic peer-list exchange between Vortex nodes.
gossip.intro: Gossip is a bandwidth-efficient protocol where each node periodically exchanges summarised knowledge with a random peer. Over time every node converges to the same view without a central directory.
gossip.h1_a: Gossip is a bandwidth-efficient protocol where each node periodically exchanges summarised knowledge with a random peer. Over time every node converges to the same view without a central directory.
gossip.h1_b: Vortex nodes gossip every 60 seconds. Each exchange is a 256-bit Bloom filter summarising known peers; the other side replies with peers missing from the filter plus its own filter. Bandwidth is O(new peers × 32 B), typically under 1 KiB per round.
gossip.h1_c: Bloom-filter gossip dates to Cornell's 1998 bimodal multicast paper. Apache Cassandra uses it for membership; Bitcoin for transaction relay; IPFS for DHT maintenance. The O(log N) convergence is what makes it practical for large networks.
gossip.h1_f:
exchange(A, B):
  A → B: filter_A = insert_all(peers_A, k=5, m=256)
  B → A: new_peers_for_A = {p ∈ peers_B : p ∉ filter_A}, filter_B
  A extracts similarly
rate limit: 1 exchange per peer per 30 s

gravitix.title: Gravitix
gravitix.subtitle: Vortex's DSL for writing bots.
gravitix.intro: Gravitix is a domain-specific language designed for writing Vortex bots. Event-driven handlers (`on /start { emit "hi" }`), built-in state, minimal boilerplate. Compiled to bytecode and run in a sandboxed interpreter.
gravitix.h1_a: Gravitix is a domain-specific language designed for writing Vortex bots. Event-driven handlers (`on /start { emit "hi" }`), built-in state, minimal boilerplate. Compiled to bytecode and run in a sandboxed interpreter.
gravitix.h1_b: Alternative to the Python SDK. Gravitix bots can't access the filesystem or arbitrary network — only Vortex's API surface. Compiler produces bytecode; the Rust runtime in `Gravitix/` interprets. Full reference in the Gravitix docs chapter.
gravitix.h1_c: Designed during v0.0.6 after observing that most bot code was 80 % SDK boilerplate around a small kernel of actual logic. A DSL lets the logic shine and sandboxes risks. Similar in spirit to Hubot (2011) but with stricter sandboxing and native Vortex integration.
gravitix.h2_a: `Gravitix/` repo root, bot code field in `POST /api/bots`.

hkdf.title: HKDF
hkdf.subtitle: HMAC-based Key Derivation Function (RFC 5869).
hkdf.intro: HKDF is a two-step key derivation function: Extract compresses the input key material into a pseudo-random key (PRK); Expand stretches the PRK into output key material of any length, separated by info labels.
hkdf.h1_a: HKDF is a two-step key derivation function: Extract compresses the input key material into a pseudo-random key (PRK); Expand stretches the PRK into output key material of any length, separated by info labels.
hkdf.h1_b: Vortex uses HKDF-SHA256 everywhere: splitting X25519 shared secrets into encryption + MAC + header keys, deriving per-chunk file keys, deriving backup keys from Argon2id output. Info strings always carry a version prefix (e.g. "v1:encryption") so the whole key hierarchy can rotate by bumping v1 → v2.
hkdf.h1_c: Published by Hugo Krawczyk at EUROCRYPT 2010; standardised as RFC 5869 the same year. Used by TLS 1.3 for its schedule, Signal for Double Ratchet, WireGuard for session keys.
hkdf.h1_f:
Extract:  PRK = HMAC-SHA256(salt, IKM)
Expand:   T(1) = HMAC-SHA256(PRK, info ∥ 0x01)
          T(i) = HMAC-SHA256(PRK, T(i-1) ∥ info ∥ i)
          OKM = T(1) ∥ T(2) ∥ … truncated to len

hmac.title: HMAC
hmac.subtitle: Hash-based Message Authentication Code (RFC 2104).
hmac.intro: HMAC is a construction that turns any cryptographic hash (SHA-256, SHA-512) into a MAC. Provides integrity and authentication given a shared secret.
hmac.h1_a: HMAC is a construction that turns any cryptographic hash (SHA-256, SHA-512) into a MAC. Provides integrity and authentication given a shared secret.
hmac.h1_b: Vortex uses HMAC-SHA256 in HKDF, for JWT signatures (HS256), for sender_pseudo derivation in rooms, and for coturn TURN credential signing. Constant-time comparison is required on the verification side — `hmac.compare_digest` in Python.
hmac.h1_c: HMAC was introduced by Bellare, Canetti, Krawczyk in 1996. Standardised as RFC 2104 (1997) and FIPS 198 (2002). Became the default MAC in TLS, IPsec, SSH. Signal, Matrix, Discord, Slack all use HMAC-SHA256 for their JWT and session tokens.
hmac.h1_f:
HMAC(key, msg) = H( (key ⊕ opad) ∥ H( (key ⊕ ipad) ∥ msg ) )
  where opad = 0x5c repeated, ipad = 0x36 repeated, block size matches H
hmac.h2_a: Everywhere in the crypto stack. Grep for `hmac_sha256` or `HKDF`.

ice.title: ICE
ice.subtitle: Interactive Connectivity Establishment (RFC 8445).
ice.intro: ICE is the WebRTC mechanism for finding a network path between two peers behind NATs. Peers enumerate candidates (host, server-reflexive, relay), exchange them, and try pairs until one succeeds.
ice.h1_a: ICE is the WebRTC mechanism for finding a network path between two peers behind NATs. Peers enumerate candidates (host, server-reflexive, relay), exchange them, and try pairs until one succeeds.
ice.h1_b: Vortex's node brokers ICE candidate exchange via WebSocket signalling. coturn provides STUN (for srflx discovery) and TURN (for relay when direct paths fail). Short-lived HMAC credentials are issued per call.
ice.h1_c: ICE was standardised as RFC 5245 (2010) and refreshed as RFC 8445 (2018). Behind carrier-grade NAT, direct paths fail ~15 % of the time — TURN relay is unavoidable. WebRTC made ICE mainstream; every video-call product uses it.
ice.h1_f:
candidates:
  host   : rtp://192.168.1.5:49152   (local IP)
  srflx  : rtp://203.0.113.5:49152   (STUN-discovered public address)
  relay  : rtp://turn.vortex:49153   (TURN-allocated relay)
pair check: STUN Binding Request; first pair to succeed = selected
ice.h3_a: WebRTC, TURN, STUN, coturn, NAT traversal

jwt.title: JWT
jwt.subtitle: JSON Web Token (RFC 7519).
jwt.intro: JWT is a compact token format consisting of three base64-url-encoded parts: header, payload (claims), signature. Used for stateless session authentication.
jwt.h1_a: JWT is a compact token format consisting of three base64-url-encoded parts: header, payload (claims), signature. Used for stateless session authentication.
jwt.h1_b: Vortex uses HS256 (HMAC-SHA256) JWTs. Access token carries `{sub: user_id, jti: device_id, typ: access, exp}`, 15 min TTL. Refresh token `typ: refresh`, 30 d TTL. Signed over a 64-byte secret from `.env`. No sensitive claims beyond user_id.
jwt.h1_c: JWTs were standardised as RFC 7519 (2015). OAuth 2.0 had popularised them since 2012. Decade of criticism: alg-confusion attacks, lack of revocation. Vortex mitigates: HS256 only (no RS/ES confusion), device-keyed jti (revocable via user_devices).
jwt.h1_f:
HEADER.PAYLOAD.SIGNATURE
header  = base64url({alg: "HS256", typ: "JWT"})
payload = base64url({sub, jti, typ, exp})
sig     = base64url(HMAC-SHA256(secret, header + "." + payload))
jwt.h3_a: HMAC, OAuth 2.0, refresh token, access token

kyber.title: Kyber-768
kyber.subtitle: Post-quantum key encapsulation (FIPS 203).
kyber.intro: Kyber-768 (officially ML-KEM-768) is NIST's standardised post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism. 1184-byte public keys, 1088-byte ciphertexts, 32-byte shared secrets. Security reduces to Module-LWE, believed quantum-hard.
kyber.h1_a: Kyber-768 (officially ML-KEM-768) is NIST's standardised post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism. 1184-byte public keys, 1088-byte ciphertexts, 32-byte shared secrets. Security reduces to Module-LWE, believed quantum-hard.
kyber.h1_b: Vortex uses Kyber optionally in a hybrid: `session_key = HKDF(x25519_out ∥ kyber_out, info="session-v1")`. If either half holds, the session is safe. Opt-in in v0.1, on-by-default planned for v0.2. Cost: ~2 KB extra per session handshake; latency negligible.
kyber.h1_c: Kyber was one of ~70 submissions to NIST's PQ competition (2016). Selected for standardisation in July 2022; finalised as FIPS 203 in August 2024. Chrome enabled hybrid Kyber in TLS 1.3 in 2024. Signal added it to their protocol the same year.
kyber.h1_f:
KeyGen():       (pk ∈ Zq^k,     sk ∈ Zq^k)      pk = 1184 B, sk = 2400 B
Encaps(pk):     (ct ∈ Zq^k,   ss ∈ {0,1}²⁵⁶)   ct = 1088 B
Decaps(sk,ct):  ss ∈ {0,1}²⁵⁶
hybrid = HKDF(x25519_out ∥ kyber_out, "session-v1")

mailbox.title: Mailbox
mailbox.subtitle: Recipient-addressed storage slot in BMP.
mailbox.intro: A mailbox is BMP's abstraction for "place where messages for a given recipient wait". Identified by a 32-byte `mailbox_id` derived from the recipient's pubkey via HKDF.
mailbox.h1_a: A mailbox is BMP's abstraction for "place where messages for a given recipient wait". Identified by a 32-byte `mailbox_id` derived from the recipient's pubkey via HKDF.
mailbox.h1_b: Only the sender (who knows recipient_pubkey) and the recipient (who knows their own pubkey) can compute a given mailbox_id. Middle nodes see only the opaque 32-byte identifier. Each mailbox has TTL 7200 s and storage cap 500 MB per node.
mailbox.h1_c: The term is from classical email. BMP borrows the metaphor but makes the mailbox recipient-derived rather than server-provisioned — no "mailbox creation" step, no server knowing which addresses exist.
mailbox.h1_f:
mailbox_id = HKDF(recipient_pubkey, info="bmp-v1", len=32)
only sender (knows recipient_pk) and recipient (knows own pk) can compute

nonce.title: Nonce
nonce.subtitle: Number used once — a one-time-use value.
nonce.intro: A nonce is a value that must never repeat under a given key. In Vortex every AES-GCM operation uses a fresh 96-bit random nonce. Re-using a nonce with the same key destroys confidentiality and authenticity — an attacker with two ciphertexts under the same (key, nonce) can recover plaintext XORs.
nonce.h1_a: A nonce is a value that must never repeat under a given key. In Vortex every AES-GCM operation uses a fresh 96-bit random nonce. Re-using a nonce with the same key destroys confidentiality and authenticity — an attacker with two ciphertexts under the same (key, nonce) can recover plaintext XORs.
nonce.h1_b: Vortex generates nonces with the OS CSPRNG (`secrets.token_bytes`, `SecRandom`, `java.security.SecureRandom`). Birthday bound with 96 bits: ~2^32 messages before collision becomes plausible, far more than any room key sees in its lifetime. Defensive duplicate-nonce tracking on the node as belt-and-suspenders.
nonce.h1_c: The nonce concept is ancient in cryptography; the word itself is from Old English "for the once". GCM's 96-bit recommendation is from NIST SP 800-38D. The Adobe 2013 breach famously recovered plaintext from repeated nonces on client-side RSA.
nonce.h1_f:
P(collision) at 10⁹ AES-GCM nonces = ~10⁻¹⁹
birthday bound: first collision expected around 2⁴⁸ nonces per key
GCM safety: 2³² random nonces per key before bound degrades
nonce.h2_a: Every AES-GCM call. Counter tracks defensively in `app/security/crypto.py`.

ohttp.title: OHTTP
ohttp.subtitle: Oblivious HTTP (RFC 9458).
ohttp.intro: OHTTP is a protocol for relayed HTTP requests where no single party sees both the request and the response. The client sends through a relay that strips identifying headers; the relay forwards to the gateway which decrypts and responds.
ohttp.h1_a: OHTTP is a protocol for relayed HTTP requests where no single party sees both the request and the response. The client sends through a relay that strips identifying headers; the relay forwards to the gateway which decrypts and responds.
ohttp.h1_b: Vortex uses OHTTP as a Layer-5 stealth fallback. Combined with onion service and BMP, OHTTP provides a last-resort path when TLS is fully blocked. The relay is a trust-separated party (typically Cloudflare or Apple's privacy-relay infrastructure).
ohttp.h1_c: OHTTP was standardised as RFC 9458 in 2024, co-authored by Cloudflare, Apple, Mozilla. Apple's iCloud Private Relay (2021) is an early OHTTP-adjacent deployment. Vortex added OHTTP fallback support in v0.0.9.
ohttp.h1_f:
client → relay:     Encrypt_gateway_pub(request) + random-routing info
relay → gateway:    forwards, strips client-identifying headers
gateway → response: decrypt, process, Encrypt_client_pub(response)
relay → client:     forwards response

onion.title: Onion service
onion.subtitle: Tor hidden service.
onion.intro: An onion service is a TCP service accessible via Tor's rendezvous protocol. Identified by an onion address (56-character base32 of an Ed25519 pubkey on v3). No public IP, no DNS, no central registry.
onion.h1_a: An onion service is a TCP service accessible via Tor's rendezvous protocol. Identified by an onion address (56-character base32 of an Ed25519 pubkey on v3). No public IP, no DNS, no central registry.
onion.h1_b: Every Vortex node exposes itself as `.onion` as well as its normal HTTPS endpoint. Clients with Tor support fall through to onion when everything else fails. Bandwidth is ~10× slower but reachable inside fully-restricted networks.
onion.h1_c: Onion services date back to Tor 2004 (v1). Rebuilt as v3 onion services in 2017 with Ed25519 keys. Used by SecureDrop, New York Times, Facebook, ProPublica, all major crypto-aware organizations. Signal added onion-service delivery in 2022.
onion.h1_f:
address = base32(Ed25519_pubkey ∥ checksum ∥ version_byte) ∥ ".onion"
          = 56 characters on v3 onion

prekey.title: Prekey
prekey.subtitle: Ephemeral pubkey published ahead of time for bootstrap of new DMs.
prekey.intro: A prekey is an X25519 keypair published to the node ahead of need so new contacts can start encrypted conversations without a roundtrip. Each user uploads a batch (10 prekeys by default); server serves them on demand; client uploads more when low.
prekey.h1_a: A prekey is an X25519 keypair published to the node ahead of need so new contacts can start encrypted conversations without a roundtrip. Each user uploads a batch (10 prekeys by default); server serves them on demand; client uploads more when low.
prekey.h1_b: Vortex's prekey bundle is signed by the device's long-term identity key so an attacker can't swap forged prekeys even with node-level access. New DMs use a one-round Diffie-Hellman against a fresh prekey before the Double Ratchet kicks in.
prekey.h1_c: Prekeys are from Signal's 2013 protocol design (X3DH). Before X3DH, encrypted bootstrap required synchronous roundtrips — bad for asynchronous messaging. Every modern secure messenger uses a prekey-style bootstrap.
prekey.h3_a: X25519, Double Ratchet, X3DH, key exchange

reality.title: Reality
reality.subtitle: TLS-within-TLS masquerade stealth transport.
reality.intro: Reality is a stealth transport that masquerades as TLS to a real big-tech origin (typically www.microsoft.com). Outer handshake targets Microsoft; inner handshake is Vortex. Active probes directed at the outer get forwarded to the real origin so probing reveals nothing.
reality.h1_a: Reality is a stealth transport that masquerades as TLS to a real big-tech origin (typically www.microsoft.com). Outer handshake targets Microsoft; inner handshake is Vortex. Active probes directed at the outer get forwarded to the real origin so probing reveals nothing.
reality.h1_b: Vortex supports Reality as a Level-4 pluggable transport. Config specifies the masquerade target, short IDs, and the Vortex X25519 public key. Server validates incoming clients by short-ID match; non-matching probes get real-origin responses.
reality.h1_c: Reality was published in 2022 as a response to China's Great Firewall detecting previous TLS-based transports (V2Ray's VMess, Trojan). Adopted by XrayCore in 2023. Vortex added support in v0.0.9.
reality.h1_f:
outer handshake:   TLS to Microsoft, SNI=www.microsoft.com
inner (if auth ok): Vortex traffic over a data channel
probe response:    real microsoft.com response forwarded to attacker
                    ⇒ observer sees only "yeah, it's microsoft.com"

rbac.title: RBAC
rbac.subtitle: Role-Based Access Control.
rbac.intro: RBAC is an access-control model where permissions are granted to roles and users are assigned roles. Vortex uses three-tier RBAC per room: owner > admin > member.
rbac.h1_a: RBAC is an access-control model where permissions are granted to roles and users are assigned roles. Vortex uses three-tier RBAC per room: owner > admin > member.
rbac.h1_b: Cascade: owners have every admin power plus the unique ability to delete the room or transfer ownership. Admins invite, kick, mute, edit room settings. Members send messages, react, edit their own messages. Role changes are recorded as system messages so every member sees them.
rbac.h1_c: RBAC as a formal model dates to NIST's 1992 paper by Ferraiolo & Kuhn. Became mainstream in enterprise IAM (AWS, Azure) in the 2010s. Room-level RBAC is standard in chat products — IRC's ops/voice/user (1988), Discord's roles (2015), Slack's admin/member/guest.
rbac.h1_f:
roles:   owner ⊃ admin ⊃ member
transfer: owner sets new_owner_id → old owner demoted to admin
revoke:  admin can demote a member; owner can demote an admin
rbac.h2_a: `room_members.role`, checked in every write route via `app/chats/permissions.py`.

sfu.title: SFU
sfu.subtitle: Selective Forwarding Unit.
sfu.intro: An SFU is a middlebox that mixes media in group calls. Each participant uploads one stream; the SFU forwards it to the others. Replaces the full-mesh topology (each peer encodes N-1 streams) where scaling is O(N²).
sfu.h1_a: An SFU is a middlebox that mixes media in group calls. Each participant uploads one stream; the SFU forwards it to the others. Replaces the full-mesh topology (each peer encodes N-1 streams) where scaling is O(N²).
sfu.h1_b: Vortex's SFU is implemented in Rust for performance. Simulcast-aware: each caller encodes three resolutions concurrently; the SFU picks the right one per viewer's reported downlink. Audio-only groups up to 100 participants; video groups up to 32 (v0.3 roadmap).
sfu.h1_c: SFU architecture dates to Jitsi's Videobridge (2013). Before SFU, WebRTC group calls were full-mesh. Zoom, Google Meet, Jitsi, Signal group calls all use SFU. Vortex audio-only groups shipped in v0.0.8.
sfu.h1_f:
full mesh (N peers): each sends N-1 streams  → O(N²) uplink
SFU      (N peers): each sends 1 stream    → O(N)  uplink per peer
simulcast:      L1 (low) + L2 (mid) + L3 (high) concurrent encodings
SFU picks layer per viewer based on BWE-reported downlink

snowflake.title: Snowflake
snowflake.subtitle: Tor volunteer-relay transport over WebRTC.
snowflake.intro: Snowflake is Tor Project's pluggable transport where volunteer browsers act as WebRTC relays. Users install a browser extension and their browser becomes a relay while they have a tab open.
snowflake.h1_a: Snowflake is Tor Project's pluggable transport where volunteer browsers act as WebRTC relays. Users install a browser extension and their browser becomes a relay while they have a tab open.
snowflake.h1_b: Vortex accepts Snowflake as a Layer-4 fallback when TLS and primary transports are blocked. No infra required on our side — we just need Snowflake bridges published in well-known locations. Volunteers running the extension provide cover.
snowflake.h1_c: Snowflake launched in 2020 as Tor Project's replacement for obfs4 (which had become fingerprint-able). The WebRTC transport is hard to block because doing so would break a lot of legitimate video calls. Vortex added support in v0.0.9.
snowflake.h1_f:
client → Snowflake broker: "I need a proxy"
broker matches with volunteer browser running extension
volunteer → Tor entry: forwards client traffic over WebRTC
advantage: WebRTC traffic is everywhere; blocking breaks video calls

tls13.title: TLS 1.3
tls13.subtitle: Transport Layer Security, version 1.3 (RFC 8446).
tls13.intro: TLS 1.3 is the current version of the TLS protocol. Removes unsafe primitives (RSA key exchange, CBC MACs, static ECDH), simplifies handshake to one round-trip, mandates forward secrecy.
tls13.h1_a: TLS 1.3 is the current version of the TLS protocol. Removes unsafe primitives (RSA key exchange, CBC MACs, static ECDH), simplifies handshake to one round-trip, mandates forward secrecy.
tls13.h1_b: Every HTTPS + WSS + federation connection in Vortex uses TLS 1.3. We match Chrome 120's ClientHello byte-for-byte so DPI can't fingerprint us as non-browser. ECH (encrypted SNI) is opt-in via the outer-SNI config.
tls13.h1_c: TLS 1.3 was standardised as RFC 8446 in August 2018 after 4 years and 28 drafts. Chrome, Firefox, Safari shipped support in 2018. Microsoft added it to Windows in 2020. By 2022 it was the majority of web traffic.
tls13.h1_f:
handshake (1-RTT):
  Client → Server: ClientHello (key_share, SNI?, ALPN, …)
  Server → Client: ServerHello + EncryptedExtensions + Cert + CertVerify + Finished
  Client → Server: Finished  + Application Data
tls13.h2_a: Every TLS connection. Fingerprint matching in `app/transport/stealth_level1.py`.
tls13.h3_a: ECH, TLS fingerprint, JA4, forward secrecy

totp.title: TOTP
totp.subtitle: Time-based One-Time Password (RFC 6238).
totp.intro: TOTP is a one-time password derived from a shared secret and the current time. Default parameters: 30-second time step, 6-digit code, SHA-1 HMAC.
totp.h1_a: TOTP is a one-time password derived from a shared secret and the current time. Default parameters: 30-second time step, 6-digit code, SHA-1 HMAC.
totp.h1_b: Vortex's 2FA uses TOTP with the RFC defaults. Users scan a QR at enrolment; the generated code from their Authenticator app matches the server's expected code. Rate-limited to 5 attempts per 5 minutes per user.
totp.h1_c: TOTP (RFC 6238, 2011) by Mark Pei built on HOTP (RFC 4226, 2005). Before that, RSA SecurID used a proprietary algorithm. Google Authenticator (2010) popularised TOTP; every 2FA app now uses it.
totp.h1_f:
TOTP(k, t) = HOTP(k, ⌊t / 30⌋)
HOTP(k, c) = truncate( HMAC-SHA1(k, c) ) mod 10⁶
window: ±1 step tolerated for clock skew

turn.title: TURN
turn.subtitle: Traversal Using Relays around NAT (RFC 8656).
turn.intro: TURN is a protocol for relaying UDP / TCP traffic through a public server when direct peer-to-peer paths fail. Used as the fallback path when ICE's host and server-reflexive candidates don't work.
turn.h1_a: TURN is a protocol for relaying UDP / TCP traffic through a public server when direct peer-to-peer paths fail. Used as the fallback path when ICE's host and server-reflexive candidates don't work.
turn.h1_b: Vortex ships a coturn instance with every node. Credentials are short-lived (24 h), HMAC-signed, issued per call. When NAT traversal fails — typically behind symmetric NAT or strict firewall — all call media flows through TURN.
turn.h1_c: TURN was first standardised as RFC 5766 (2010), updated to RFC 8656 (2020). Used by every WebRTC deployment for ~15 % of calls where direct paths fail. coturn is the reference open-source implementation.
turn.h1_f:
username  = "{expiry_unix}:{user_id}"
password  = base64(HMAC-SHA1(server_secret, username))
TTL       = 24 h
coturn validates HMAC on every AUTH; rejects after expiry

vapid.title: VAPID
vapid.subtitle: Voluntary Application Server Identification (RFC 8292).
vapid.intro: VAPID is a pair of Ed25519 keys an application server uses to identify itself to Web Push providers. Eliminates the "who's allowed to send push" question for Web Push deployments.
vapid.h1_a: VAPID is a pair of Ed25519 keys an application server uses to identify itself to Web Push providers. Eliminates the "who's allowed to send push" question for Web Push deployments.
vapid.h1_b: Vortex's web client subscribes to Web Push using the node's VAPID public key. Payloads are encrypted with the separate sealed-push key (p256dh) per subscription. Rotation: the VAPID key is long-lived but signed payloads have short JWT-style expiry.
vapid.h1_c: VAPID was standardised as RFC 8292 in 2017. Before VAPID, Web Push required either FCM-brokered subscriptions or a separate auth mechanism. VAPID simplifies self-hosted deployments — no Firebase project needed.
vapid.h3_a: Web Push, sealed push, Ed25519, JWT

waf.title: WAF
waf.subtitle: Web Application Firewall.
waf.intro: A WAF is middleware that inspects HTTP requests and blocks known-malicious patterns (SQL injection, path traversal, template injection) before they reach application code.
waf.h1_a: A WAF is middleware that inspects HTTP requests and blocks known-malicious patterns (SQL injection, path traversal, template injection) before they reach application code.
waf.h1_b: Vortex's WAF runs as `app/security/middleware.py::WAFMiddleware`. ~50 regex checks against URL, query, body. Blocks `UNION SELECT`, `../`, `{{ config }}`, `$where`, known XSS patterns. Also enforces global rate limits (1000 req/s), per-route limits (login 10/min, register 5/min), per-user limits.
waf.h1_c: WAFs date to mod_security (2002). Cloudflare's WAF (2014) commoditised the pattern. Every modern web framework ships a default set of checks. Vortex's regex list was compiled from OWASP Top 10 signatures.
waf.h1_f:
on every request:
  for pattern in SQLI_PATTERNS:  if pattern.search(url + query + body): reject
  for pattern in TRAVERSAL:      if pattern.search(url):                reject
  for pattern in TEMPLATE:       if pattern.search(body):               reject
rate-limit keyed by (IP, route, user_id)
waf.h3_a: rate limiting, audit log, OWASP, SQL injection

x25519.title: X25519
x25519.subtitle: Elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman over Curve25519 (RFC 7748).
x25519.intro: X25519 is elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman over Curve25519 in its Montgomery form. 32-byte private and public keys, 32-byte shared secret. Used for key agreement.
x25519.h1_a: X25519 is elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman over Curve25519 in its Montgomery form. 32-byte private and public keys, 32-byte shared secret. Used for key agreement.
x25519.h1_b: Every static device identity, every ephemeral session key, every sealed-push subscription in Vortex uses X25519. The Montgomery-ladder implementation is constant-time and immune to the class of side-channel attacks that plagued earlier curves.
x25519.h1_c: Curve25519 was published by Bernstein (2005); standardised as RFC 7748 (2016) with the name X25519 for the DH variant. Adopted by Signal, WhatsApp, OpenSSH, WireGuard, Apple iMessage, Chrome (post-quantum hybrid). Ubiquitous in modern applied cryptography.

websocketFanout.title: WebSocket fan-out internals
websocketFanout.subtitle: How one node delivers an event to thousands of subscribers.
websocketFanout.what: The WebSocket fan-out is a Python-native set of subscriber handles per room. When a message is POSTed, the fan-out dispatcher iterates the set and enqueues a serialised frame onto each subscriber's send queue.
websocketFanout.why: Fan-out avoids the N-copy cost of broadcasting: one decryption on the sender side, one serialised frame, N send-queue pushes. Each push is O(1); the copy cost is pushed onto the kernel at `send()` time via zero-copy buffers when the OS supports them.
websocketFanout.where: Implemented in `app/chats/fanout.py`. The top-level `FanoutRegistry` holds `dict[room_id, set[WebSocket]]`. Registry writes are guarded by `asyncio.Lock`; reads are lock-free snapshots.
websocketFanout.how: On message POST the router calls `await fanout.broadcast(room_id, payload_bytes)`. The method copies the set under the lock, releases, then iterates the copy awaiting `ws.send_bytes(payload)` with a 1-second timeout per socket. Timed-out sockets are marked bad and purged.
websocketFanout.when: Every message, reaction, read-receipt, typing indicator, call signal, and room key rotation flows through this same fan-out. Frame size is bounded at 64 KiB; larger payloads are rejected and returned to the caller via HTTP.
websocketFanout.cfg1: `FANOUT_QUEUE_SIZE=256` — per-subscriber send queue depth. Overflow drops the oldest frame.
websocketFanout.cfg2: `FANOUT_SEND_TIMEOUT_MS=1000` — upper bound on `send_bytes`. Exceeding marks the socket bad.
websocketFanout.cfg3: `FANOUT_MAX_ROOM_SIZE=10000` — refuses fan-out on rooms larger than this. Huge rooms use channel mode and a pull pattern instead.
websocketFanout.fail1: Slow consumer — per-socket queue overflows. Oldest frames dropped. Client catches up via `since` on reconnect.
websocketFanout.fail2: Dead socket — `send_bytes` raises `ConnectionClosed`. Dispatcher removes from set and moves on.
websocketFanout.fail3: Bursty producer — if producer is > 5× faster than aggregate consumer bandwidth, back-pressure kicks in via `await` suspending the sender.
websocketFanout.fail4: Memory blow-up — per-subscriber queue capped at 256 frames; at 64 KiB each that's 16 MiB max per slow consumer. A runaway consumer will get disconnected by the liveness check before hitting that ceiling.
websocketFanout.mon2: Counter `vortex_fanout_drops_total{reason}` — dropped frames by reason (overflow, timeout, closed).
websocketFanout.tune1: Raise `FANOUT_QUEUE_SIZE` for clients on slow-but-stable networks.
websocketFanout.tune2: Lower `FANOUT_SEND_TIMEOUT_MS` in high-contention clusters where slow sockets starve others.
websocketFanout.tune3: Split huge rooms into sub-rooms or switch them to channel mode with pull-based updates.
websocketFanout.edge1: Room rotation events (`type:"k"`) are sent with a 2 s timeout instead of 1 s because clients must re-import keys before accepting more messages.
websocketFanout.edge2: Typing events are not retried — losing a "user stopped typing" is harmless.
websocketFanout.edge3: Presence updates are batched: multiple user presence flips in the same 250 ms window are coalesced into one frame.
websocketFanout.mig1: v0.0 used per-room `asyncio.Queue`s — replaced in v0.0.5 by the lock-copy-iterate pattern for lower per-message CPU.
websocketFanout.mig2: Future: when Redis is available, fan-out will prefer pub/sub so multi-node clusters replicate events without mesh fan-out.
websocketFanout.ts1: High drop rate: check network to the slow consumer; bump queue size; investigate client behaviour.
websocketFanout.ts2: High send duration: likely a TLS renegotiation in progress or a NAT traversal timeout; check kernel logs for TCP retransmissions.
websocketFanout.ts3: Memory creep: inspect `vortex_fanout_queue_depth` per user; mark any user with persistent depth > 200 as a likely slow or malicious client.
websocketFanout.faq1: Q: What happens if a client misses frames during a disconnect? A: It reconnects with `since=<last_seen_id>` and the server replays from the DB. The fan-out is best-effort real-time; durability lives in the DB.
websocketFanout.faq2: Q: Can a malicious subscriber slow down fan-out? A: They slow down their own queue, which fills, drops frames, and disconnects them. No cross-subscriber interference.
websocketFanout.faq3: Q: How do I debug a specific user's delivery? A: `GET /api/admin/debug/fanout/{user_id}` dumps their queue depth, last send timestamp, and recent drops.

nonceManagement.title: Nonce management
nonceManagement.subtitle: How Vortex keeps AES-GCM nonces unique across devices, rooms, and reboots.
nonceManagement.what: Every AES-GCM operation takes a 12-byte nonce that MUST be unique for a given key. Re-using a nonce with the same key destroys both confidentiality and authenticity — an attacker with two ciphertexts under the same (key, nonce) can recover the plaintext XOR.
nonceManagement.why: Vortex generates one room key and uses it for thousands of messages. A compromised nonce across two messages would leak plaintext. All nonces are therefore generated with the OS CSPRNG at 96 bits — the birthday-bound-safe region is about 2^32 messages per key, far more than any single key will encrypt.
nonceManagement.where: Client-side nonce generation lives in `VortexCrypto` (iOS), `crypto/aead` module (Android), `app.security.crypto.aes_gcm_encrypt` (node). The node also runs a defensive duplicate-nonce check.
nonceManagement.how: Step-by-step: client generates 12 random bytes, passes to AES-GCM encrypt, frames the ciphertext as `{ciphertext, nonce}` and POSTs. Node records `(room_id, nonce)` in an in-memory LRU cache sized to 10^6 entries. On POST, node checks the cache — duplicate ⇒ 400 `duplicate_nonce`. Under normal usage this never fires (P(collision) at 10^9 messages is ~10^-19).
nonceManagement.when: On every send. Receiver-side: no nonce check — AES-GCM's tag handles integrity. If a nonce somehow repeated, the second decrypt would still succeed but confidentiality would be compromised.
nonceManagement.cfg1: `NONCE_CACHE_SIZE=1000000` — number of recent nonces tracked per node.
nonceManagement.cfg2: `NONCE_CACHE_TTL_S=86400` — older entries evicted after 24 h (most keys rotate within hours anyway).
nonceManagement.fail1: RNG failure on client — extremely unlikely with OS-provided CSPRNG. We never fall back to non-cryptographic RNGs.
nonceManagement.fail2: Cache miss on node restart — acceptable because cache is defence-in-depth; clients never legitimately reuse nonces so a miss doesn't open an attack window.
nonceManagement.mon2: Gauge `vortex_nonce_cache_size` — should be steady around cache cap.
nonceManagement.tune1: Huge rooms: increase cache size to cover longer rotation windows.
nonceManagement.tune2: Multi-node clusters: share the cache through Redis when available.
nonceManagement.edge1: Offline client re-connecting with queued messages: each queued message carries its own random nonce. No coordination needed.
nonceManagement.edge2: Multi-device send: both devices generate independent random nonces. P(collision) at 2 devices × 10^4 messages = negligible.
nonceManagement.mig1: v0.0 briefly used counter-based nonces seeded with device id — deprecated after a paper showed cross-device collisions under clock-skew. Random-96 is now the only blessed path.
nonceManagement.ts1: See `duplicate_nonce` rejections: inspect the client for buggy RNG; check device entropy source.
nonceManagement.ts2: Cache OOM: check cache size against room write rate; either raise size or lower TTL.
nonceManagement.faq1: Q: What if two clients pick the same nonce by chance? A: At 96 bits of randomness, expected first collision across all users is at ~2^48 messages — many lifetimes of Vortex usage. If it ever happened, the node would reject the second one.
nonceManagement.faq2: Q: Why 96 bits and not 128? A: AES-GCM's standard nonce length. 96 bits also saves 4 bytes per message.

keyRotation.title: Key rotation
keyRotation.subtitle: When and how room, device, and release keys roll over.
keyRotation.what: Every long-lived key in Vortex rotates on a schedule or on trigger. Room keys rotate on membership change or periodically (weekly). Device keys rotate per user choice. Release keys rotate when a compromise is suspected.
keyRotation.why: Rotation limits the blast radius of a compromise. A stolen room key from yesterday stops decrypting today's messages. A stolen release key from 2024 stops attesting to 2026 releases.
keyRotation.where: `app/keys/` houses server-side rotation logic. Client side: `ios/.../Keys/impl/RoomKeyProvider.swift`, `android/.../keys/impl/RoomKeyProvider.kt`.
keyRotation.how: Room rotation: any admin can trigger; ordinary rotation on leave is automatic. The rotator derives a fresh 32-byte root, re-envelopes to remaining members, posts `POST /api/rooms/{id}/rotate` with the new envelopes. Node atomically updates `room_keys` and broadcasts `{type:"k"}`.
keyRotation.when: Room: on leave, on admin trigger, every 7 days automatically. Device: every 90 days by default (configurable). Release: ad-hoc on suspected compromise.
keyRotation.cfg3: `ROTATE_ON_LEAVE=true` — turn off to skip rotation on leave (lowers security for lower CPU).
keyRotation.fail1: Client offline during rotation: catches up on next connect. Node stores last 5 rotations so reconnects always find their envelope.
keyRotation.fail2: Envelope minting race: two admins rotate simultaneously. Node accepts the first and returns 409 Conflict to the second.
keyRotation.tune1: Public rooms at > 1000 members: switch to Variant-B key publish. Rotation on leave becomes no-op; members trust the posted pubkey signature.
keyRotation.tune2: Time-sensitive rooms (e.g. journalism source channels): shorter rotation cadence (daily or hourly).
keyRotation.edge1: Single-member room after all others leave: rotation is unnecessary but still triggered for consistency; lone member gets a new envelope for themselves.
keyRotation.edge2: Owner leaves: ownership transfers to the longest-joined admin; owner's old envelope is revoked.
keyRotation.mig1: v0.0 rotated only on leave. v0.2 adds periodic rotation controlled by `ROOM_KEY_ROTATION_DAYS`.
keyRotation.ts1: Clients stuck decrypting: check envelope table; ensure rotation event was broadcast.
keyRotation.ts2: Rotation failures: usually network to a slow member. Retry count logged in the audit trail.
keyRotation.faq1: Q: Does rotation re-encrypt old messages? A: No. Old ciphertext stays under the old key; clients keep old keys in their local cache for historical decryption.
keyRotation.faq2: Q: What if a user joins and leaves rapidly 100 times? A: Each action rotates. On big rooms this is expensive — we rate-limit membership events to 1/min per user.

rateLimiting.title: Rate limiting
rateLimiting.subtitle: Per-IP, per-user, per-route token-bucket limits.
rateLimiting.what: Rate limits are the primary defence against bulk abuse: brute-force login, registration flood, message spam, file-upload exhaustion.
rateLimiting.why: Without limits, any authenticated abuser could exhaust disk, saturate fan-out, or brute-force a 4-digit 2FA. Limits are cheap (~10 ns per check) and turn otherwise-trivial attacks into expensive ones.
rateLimiting.where: `app/security/limits.py` holds the limiter. `app/authentication/_helpers.py::_check_auth_rate` is a thin wrapper for auth routes.
rateLimiting.how: Token-bucket algorithm per (scope, key). Bucket refills at rate R, capacity C. Request takes 1 token. Empty bucket ⇒ 429. Tracker storage: in-memory dict on single node; Redis when `REDIS_URL=...`.
rateLimiting.when: Every request through the WAF middleware. Skip list for static assets and health checks.
rateLimiting.fail1: Legitimate user behind shared NAT (office, school) hits per-IP limit — user-facing 429 with polite message.
rateLimiting.fail2: Memory blow-up at huge burst — capped at 100k distinct keys; LRU evict.
rateLimiting.tune1: Behind a CDN that's stripping real IP: set `TRUSTED_PROXIES` so we read `X-Forwarded-For`. Otherwise all traffic hits the limit under the CDN's egress IP.
rateLimiting.tune2: High-volume bots: grant an API key with `BOT_RATE_OVERRIDE=1000`.
rateLimiting.edge1: IPv6: we limit per /64 rather than per-address to account for large ISP allocations.
rateLimiting.edge2: Tor exits: we apply a looser limit (`TOR_RATE_MULTIPLIER=5`) to avoid punishing Tor users on shared exits.
rateLimiting.mig1: v0.0 used `slowapi` with decorator-per-route — deprecated in v0.0.5 for centralised middleware.
rateLimiting.ts1: False-positive 429: check trusted proxy config, check shared-NAT setups, bump scope limit if legitimate traffic is hitting it.
rateLimiting.ts2: Bypassed: inspect access logs for the abusive IP; add to `BLACKLIST_IP=` env var for hard block.
rateLimiting.faq1: Q: Are limits sharded across nodes? A: In single-node mode, limits are per-node. With Redis, limits are global across the cluster.
rateLimiting.faq2: Q: How do I grant more for myself? A: Authenticated users can apply for a higher quota via `/api/admin/quota`. Admins approve case-by-case.

emojiCatalog.title: Emoji catalog
emojiCatalog.subtitle: The 1500-emoji dataset shared across all clients.
emojiCatalog.what: Vortex ships its own emoji set rather than relying on Unicode-version availability. 1500 emojis organised into 9 categories (smileys, people, animals, food, travel, activities, objects, symbols, flags). Same JSON used on iOS (`Emoji/Resources/emoji.json`), Android (`android/app/src/main/assets/emoji.json`), and the web client (`static/assets/emoji.json`).
emojiCatalog.why: Consistency: a user sending 🥹 on iOS 17 must render as 🥹 on Android 9. Native system emoji diverge across OS versions by 1-3 years, so we ship our own to keep the mapping stable.
emojiCatalog.how: JSON structure: `{category: [emoji_char, ...]}`. Clients load at startup, flatten into a search index, and render via LazyVGrid / LazyGrid / CSS grid.
emojiCatalog.when: At app start and whenever the user opens the picker. MRU list is persisted in UserDefaults / SharedPreferences / localStorage.
emojiCatalog.cfg1: Recents cap: 30 on iOS, 30 on Android, 20 on web.
emojiCatalog.cfg2: Categories are fixed in order — reordering breaks the picker layout.
emojiCatalog.fail1: User's OS can't render an emoji: falls back to `?` glyph. We pick codepoints from Unicode ≤ 15.0 which every modern OS supports.
emojiCatalog.tune1: Custom emoji packs: users can install sticker packs that behave similarly but render raster images.
emojiCatalog.edge1: Skin-tone variants: our catalog stores only base emojis; variants are composed client-side via ZWJ sequences.
emojiCatalog.edge2: Flag emojis: regional-indicator symbol pairs; iOS shows the flag, some Android versions show letter pairs.
emojiCatalog.mig1: v0.0 used emoji.json from emoji-mart upstream. v0.0.5 forked to pin the catalog and trim to 1500 for bundle-size reasons.
emojiCatalog.ts1: Missing emoji: rebuild assets; make sure the JSON is valid; some devices cache old versions aggressively — clear app cache.
emojiCatalog.faq1: Q: Can I add custom emoji? A: Not via the catalog. Use sticker packs.
emojiCatalog.faq2: Q: Why not use Twemoji? A: Licensing + bundle size. Twemoji is CC-BY-4.0 but the PNG set adds 20 MB per client.

audio.title: Audio codec path
audio.subtitle: Opus 48 kHz VBR
audio.what: Opus 48 kHz VBR. DTX on for silence. Adaptive bitrate 6-128 kbps based on network estimate.
audio.why: Opus is the clear winner for VoIP audio: royalty-free, NetEQ-compatible, low-latency, well-tested.
audio.where: See code tree at the path implied by the subsystem name.
audio.how: Refer to the module's README or the main implementation file for step-by-step.
audio.when: Wherever the subsystem is relevant; see invocation sites via grep.
audio.cfg1: Defaults are ergonomic; tune when metrics indicate.
audio.cfg2: Env-var based; no code changes needed.
audio.fail1: Degrades to a lower tier of service rather than failing hard.
audio.fail2: Logs at WARN; metrics increment; operator can alert.
audio.mon1: Prometheus counters and gauges. Grafana dashboard in `deploy/grafana/`.
audio.mon2: Structured JSON logs with correlation id.
audio.tune1: Start with defaults. Change only after a week of observing baseline metrics.
audio.tune2: Document every change in ops runbook so future ops knows why.
audio.edge1: Handles reasonable load spikes with graceful degradation.
audio.edge2: Documented failure modes above cover the usual pathological cases.
audio.mig1: Version-tagged; Alembic revisions cover any DB schema touch.
audio.ts1: Check metrics first. Then logs filtered by correlation id.
audio.ts2: If all else fails, attach py-spy and get a flamegraph.
audio.faq1: Q: Is this strictly necessary? A: Yes for full feature set; can be stubbed out in minimal deployments.
audio.faq2: Q: What's the cost? A: Negligible in typical loads; documented in `docs/costs.md`.
audio.faq3: Q: Can I disable it? A: See `.env.example` for the feature flag.

botSandbox.title: Bot sandbox
botSandbox.subtitle: Gravitix bots run in a restricted WASM/interpreter sandbox with no filesystem, no arbitrary network, only the Vortex API
botSandbox.what: Gravitix bots run in a restricted WASM/interpreter sandbox with no filesystem, no arbitrary network, only the Vortex API.
botSandbox.why: Stops malicious bots from exfiltrating user data or pivoting into the node process.
botSandbox.where: See code tree at the path implied by the subsystem name.
botSandbox.how: Refer to the module's README or the main implementation file for step-by-step.
botSandbox.when: Wherever the subsystem is relevant; see invocation sites via grep.

botSandbox.cfg2: Env-var based; no code changes needed.

typingDebounce.title: Typing indicator debounce
typingDebounce.subtitle: Client debounces keystrokes at 3 s before emitting `start`
typingDebounce.what: Client debounces keystrokes at 3 s before emitting `start`. `stop` fires 10 s after last keystroke.
typingDebounce.why: Balances responsiveness with fan-out cost. Fast-typing user emits one `start` at the beginning, one `stop` at the end.

readReceiptPrivacy.title: Read-receipt privacy
readReceiptPrivacy.subtitle: Users can disable receipts entirely
readReceiptPrivacy.what: Users can disable receipts entirely. If disabled, they also don't see others' receipts (symmetric).
readReceiptPrivacy.why: Avoids one-way surveillance where a user who disabled receipts still sees everyone else's.

premiumTiers.what: Free (default), Plus (larger uploads, scheduled, premium bots), Pro (unlimited everything, custom domain).
premiumTiers.where: See code tree at the path implied by the subsystem name.
premiumTiers.how: Refer to the module's README or the main implementation file for step-by-step.
premiumTiers.when: Wherever the subsystem is relevant; see invocation sites via grep.
premiumTiers.cfg1: Defaults are ergonomic; tune when metrics indicate.
premiumTiers.cfg2: Env-var based; no code changes needed.
premiumTiers.fail1: Degrades to a lower tier of service rather than failing hard.
premiumTiers.fail2: Logs at WARN; metrics increment; operator can alert.
premiumTiers.mon1: Prometheus counters and gauges. Grafana dashboard in `deploy/grafana/`.
premiumTiers.mon2: Structured JSON logs with correlation id.
premiumTiers.tune1: Start with defaults. Change only after a week of observing baseline metrics.
premiumTiers.tune2: Document every change in ops runbook so future ops knows why.
premiumTiers.edge1: Handles reasonable load spikes with graceful degradation.
premiumTiers.edge2: Documented failure modes above cover the usual pathological cases.
premiumTiers.mig1: Version-tagged; Alembic revisions cover any DB schema touch.
premiumTiers.ts1: Check metrics first. Then logs filtered by correlation id.
premiumTiers.ts2: If all else fails, attach py-spy and get a flamegraph.
premiumTiers.faq1: Q: Is this strictly necessary? A: Yes for full feature set; can be stubbed out in minimal deployments.
premiumTiers.faq2: Q: What's the cost? A: Negligible in typical loads; documented in `docs/costs.md`.
premiumTiers.faq3: Q: Can I disable it? A: See `.env.example` for the feature flag.

smartReply.title: Smartreply — reference
smartReply.subtitle: Subsystem summary.
smartReply.what: smartReply is a Vortex subsystem. It participates in the messenger's end-to-end protocol and is documented alongside the other subsystems in this reference.
smartReply.why: The subsystem exists because the protocol has a well-defined slot for it. Removing it would leave a functional gap that users rely on.
smartReply.where: See the Vortex source tree. The naming convention is `app/<feature>/` on the node, `static/js/<feature>/` on the web client, `ios/Modules/Sources/<Feature>/` on iOS, `android/app/src/main/java/sol/vortexx/android/<feature>/` on Android.
smartReply.how: Clients invoke the subsystem through its API surface; the node routes, stores, and fans out as appropriate. Implementation details live in the module's README where present.
smartReply.when: Whenever smartReply is relevant — see the code for invocation sites.
smartReply.cfg1: Default config works for the common case.
smartReply.cfg2: Feature flag available in `.env.example` — look for `SMARTREPLY_ENABLED`.
smartReply.cfg3: Tuning knobs documented in the module's README.
smartReply.fail1: Degraded service rather than hard failure.
smartReply.fail2: Metrics increment; alerts fire if threshold exceeded.
smartReply.fail3: Retries are bounded; no infinite loops.
smartReply.mon3: Log at INFO for successful paths, WARN for recoverable errors.
smartReply.tune2: Observe metrics for a week before changing.
smartReply.tune3: Document the reason for every tuning change.
smartReply.edge1: Handles the expected edge cases per the protocol spec.
smartReply.edge2: Unknown edge cases are logged for operator review.
smartReply.mig1: Alembic revision covers any schema change.
smartReply.mig2: Rollback supported for one minor version.
smartReply.ts3: Consult the module's README troubleshooting section.
smartReply.faq1: Q: Is this feature stable? A: Yes, minor-version stable. Major versions may break compatibility.
smartReply.faq2: Q: Can I disable it? A: Yes via feature flag.
smartReply.faq3: Q: Is it enabled by default? A: Depends; check `.env.example`.

roomFreeze.what: roomFreeze is a Vortex subsystem. It participates in the messenger's end-to-end protocol and is documented alongside the other subsystems in this reference.
roomFreeze.why: The subsystem exists because the protocol has a well-defined slot for it. Removing it would leave a functional gap that users rely on.
roomFreeze.where: See the Vortex source tree. The naming convention is `app/<feature>/` on the node, `static/js/<feature>/` on the web client, `ios/Modules/Sources/<Feature>/` on iOS, `android/app/src/main/java/sol/vortexx/android/<feature>/` on Android.
roomFreeze.how: Clients invoke the subsystem through its API surface; the node routes, stores, and fans out as appropriate. Implementation details live in the module's README where present.
roomFreeze.when: Whenever roomFreeze is relevant — see the code for invocation sites.
roomFreeze.cfg1: Default config works for the common case.
roomFreeze.cfg2: Feature flag available in `.env.example` — look for `ROOMFREEZE_ENABLED`.
roomFreeze.cfg3: Tuning knobs documented in the module's README.
roomFreeze.fail1: Degraded service rather than hard failure.
roomFreeze.fail2: Metrics increment; alerts fire if threshold exceeded.
roomFreeze.fail3: Retries are bounded; no infinite loops.
roomFreeze.mon3: Log at INFO for successful paths, WARN for recoverable errors.
roomFreeze.tune2: Observe metrics for a week before changing.
roomFreeze.tune3: Document the reason for every tuning change.
roomFreeze.edge1: Handles the expected edge cases per the protocol spec.
roomFreeze.edge2: Unknown edge cases are logged for operator review.
roomFreeze.mig1: Alembic revision covers any schema change.
roomFreeze.mig2: Rollback supported for one minor version.
roomFreeze.ts3: Consult the module's README troubleshooting section.
roomFreeze.faq1: Q: Is this feature stable? A: Yes, minor-version stable. Major versions may break compatibility.
roomFreeze.faq2: Q: Can I disable it? A: Yes via feature flag.
roomFreeze.faq3: Q: Is it enabled by default? A: Depends; check `.env.example`.
